The current confidence architecture is the Bayesian-compatible confidence-weighted Conf_total specification integrated in Appendix R. The current distributed-systems architecture is the workload model and runtime validation plan in Appendix A. The current PRU valuation architecture is the bounded value function integrated in Appendix R. The current AI/offline runtime architecture is specified in Part IV and Appendix W.

**Canon Continuity Notice**

This edition preserves the full Prometheus canon corpus and integrates the current methodology as the operative canon interpretation for computability, evidence confidence, PRU boundedness, portfolio risk, AI authority limits and offline runtime continuity. No original table, image, appendix, legal boundary, access covenant, operational architecture or philosophical source text is intentionally removed. The canon evolves from inside its own architecture: continuity is preserved, ambiguity is bounded, and operational meaning is made more reviewable.

# Institutional Boundary

This White Paper is an architectural, conceptual, scientific, technological, and strategic document.

It defines a framework, not a public offering.

Any future financial instrument, tokenized representation, investment vehicle, SPV, fund, note, security, governance mechanism, or market-facing product derived from the Prometheus framework will require appropriate jurisdictional analysis, legal structuring, regulatory review, investor disclosure, compliance procedures, and independent professional assessment.

Financial rights, investor exposure, cash-flow rights, land-related rights, governance rights, operational rights, and tokenized representations are treated as distinct layers of the architecture.

This separation is essential to preserve clarity, prevent conceptual inflation, and support institutional trust.

**Within this architecture:**

PRU represents the analytical interface through which verified regenerative performance may become comparable, reviewable, maturity-classified, and capital-readable.

TRBK represents a governance, coordination, access, or participation layer, subject to its own legal and functional boundaries.

HoloFuel represents an operational transaction and infrastructure layer within agent-centric digital systems.

# Legal Opinion Gate and Rights-Creation Boundary

Prometheus distinguishes analytical meaning, governance participation, operational coordination, digital representation and enforceable legal rights.

PRU analysis does not create rights.

RAP maturity does not create liquidity.

TRBK does not create investor entitlement.

HoloFuel does not create PRU value.

Dashboard display does not create legal status.

Governance participation does not create economic entitlement.

Runtime execution does not create investor protection.

Access Covenant acknowledgement does not create universal contractual enforceability.

Only a valid legal instrument can create enforceable rights.

No Prometheus-related instrument, representation, token, access pathway, governance right, revenue right, land-related right, fund interest, note, SPV interest, certificate, digital representation, bundled structure, dashboard status, capital-facing pathway or market-facing product may be offered, marketed, sold, transferred, admitted, represented or relied upon without jurisdiction-specific legal review.

Until legal review is complete, Prometheus materials may describe only:

architecture;

methodology;

research;

pilots;

governance boundaries;

conditional future pathways;

non-investment participation;

evidence-gated development;

legal structuring requirements.

No material may imply ownership, liquidity, yield, investor protection, transferable rights, redemption, asset backing, certification, regulatory approval, enforceable claim, secured exposure, revenue participation, land-related entitlement, fund interest, note, security, insurance recognition or public-procurement eligibility without legal admission.

The Legal Opinion Package is therefore a gate, not a decoration.

It must precede any capital-facing, investor-facing, token-facing, instrument-facing, revenue-facing, land-rights-facing or market-facing use of PRU, RAP, TRBK, HoloFuel, digital representations or bundled rights.

# Civic Neutrality, Political Independence, and Mission-Compatibility Boundary

Prometheus is a civic, ecological, evidentiary, governance, and regenerative-infrastructure architecture. It is not the institutional, technological, financial, electoral, communications, membership, voter-engagement, or campaign infrastructure of any political party, candidate, electoral committee, public official, ideological movement, religious body, corporation, foundation, investor, donor, government, or supranational institution.

Prometheus may support mission-aligned public-interest, community, educational, territorial, ecological, philanthropic, institutional, or policy initiatives when they remain compatible with the Access Covenant, the Eternity Clauses, the evidence-before-value principle, human-dignity safeguards, applicable law, and the non-capture architecture.

Mission compatibility does not create ownership.

Participation does not create canonical authority.

Political alignment does not create methodological authority.

Electoral success does not create governance authority.

Public office does not create PRU or RAP authority.

Funding does not create constitutional authority.

Technical custody does not create institutional sovereignty.

A political party, elected administration, civic movement, public agency, municipality, foundation, university, cooperative, Community Land Trust, or community organisation may adopt, fund, test, recommend, or deploy Prometheus-compatible methods. No such relationship converts Prometheus into the proprietary platform, official ideology, campaign instrument, policy-certification authority, or data infrastructure of that participant.

The controlling distinction is:

mission compatibility ≠ political affiliation;

public-interest deployment ≠ electoral endorsement;

civic participation ≠ voter mobilisation;

policy relevance ≠ party control;

evidence support ≠ political validation;

Prometheus compatibility ≠ Prometheus ownership.

**Party-Independent Infrastructure Rule**

Prometheus shall remain party-independent and institutionally plural.

It may serve different communities, jurisdictions, institutions, administrations, political traditions, indigenous authorities, religious communities, civic organisations, educational systems, and public-interest actors where their participation remains compatible with the constitutional boundaries of the system.

No participant may require Prometheus to:

• endorse a candidate, political party, electoral platform, referendum position, lobbying campaign, or ideological doctrine;

• exclude a person or community solely because of lawful political affiliation, non-affiliation, religion, philosophy, or civic viewpoint;

• alter ecological evidence, MRV status, PRU analysis, RAP maturity, Claims Register status, governance records, or public reporting for electoral or political advantage;

• convert political objectives into verified ecological, social, health, economic, or financial outcomes without evidence;

• treat electoral mandate, legislative approval, public popularity, party membership, or political sponsorship as scientific, methodological, ecological, legal, or financial validation;

• condition access to food, plants, land, education, public services, community infrastructure, ecological benefits, or Prometheus participation on political support, voting behaviour, campaign activity, party membership, or ideological conformity.

**Electoral and Campaign Firewall**

Prometheus operational infrastructure shall remain segregated from electoral and campaign infrastructure.

The following data and systems shall not be combined by default:

• ecological MRV data and voter files;

• stewardship records and political-preference data;

• Claims Register records and campaign-targeting systems;

• OHE participation data and voter-profiling systems;

• community food-distribution records and electoral persuasion systems;

• school, family, health, biometric, land, geolocation, volunteer, or beneficiary data and campaign analytics;

• PRU, RAP, token, governance, treasury, donor, or operational data and political micro-targeting systems.

Any legally permitted interaction between a Prometheus-compatible civic deployment and a political or electoral organisation must be governed by explicit purpose limitation, data minimisation, role separation, access control, retention limits, legal review, conflict-of-interest disclosure, and an auditable decision record.

No ecological, social, educational, behavioural, biometric, economic, or territorial data generated through Prometheus may be used to infer political affiliation, voting intention, ideological orientation, electoral susceptibility, or campaign-targeting status.

No Prometheus score, indicator, credential, governance role, access status, stewardship record, public-benefit record, contribution history, or digital identity may determine political eligibility, voting rights in public elections, access to public services, or inclusion in political communications.

**Public-Resource Non-Capture Rule**

Where Prometheus-compatible deployments use public land, public funds, schools, municipal infrastructure, charitable assets, community assets, donated plants, public datasets, or public-benefit programmes, such resources shall remain governed by their applicable public, charitable, educational, community, or statutory purpose.

A political sponsor, administration, party, elected representative, donor, investor, operator, vendor, or technical custodian may not convert a public-interest deployment into:

• a proprietary electoral asset;

• a voter-recruitment channel;

• a partisan membership system;

• a campaign-benefit exchange;

• an exclusive political branding platform;

• a private claim over publicly generated ecological value;

• an unauthorised claim over community land, data, infrastructure, produce, evidence, or governance;

• a source of investor, token, land, revenue, or governance rights without an independently valid legal instrument.

Public-facing deployments should, where legally and operationally feasible, remain accessible under transparent, non-discriminatory, mission-consistent participation rules.

**Public Claims Boundary**

Political programmes, manifestos, campaign statements, policy aspirations, civic visions, and public commitments remain programme-level claims until supported by the applicable evidence.

A political statement does not become a verified indicator because it appears in a manifesto.

A public-policy target does not become an achieved outcome because legislation is adopted.

A funded programme does not become effective because expenditure occurs.

A food forest does not demonstrate food security merely because it is planted.

A school garden does not demonstrate educational improvement merely because students participate.

A community programme does not demonstrate health improvement, crime reduction, social cohesion, resilience, cost savings, or return on investment without a defined baseline, population, comparator, method, period, evidence package, uncertainty disclosure, and reviewer trail.

All material civic, educational, health, social, public-value, fiscal, resilience, or policy claims shall be classified through the Claims Register as one or more of the following:

• constitutional aspiration;

• policy objective;

• programme theory;

• external-literature signal;

• pilot hypothesis;

• field observation;

• evidence-supported outcome;

• independently reviewed outcome;

• legally or institutionally admitted public claim.

The lowest unresolved civic, evidentiary, legal, ethical, privacy, or governance gate controls the permitted public claim.

**Institutional Separation Rule**

Where a political party, candidate organisation, elected administration, foundation, nonprofit entity, educational institution, municipality, public agency, cooperative, trust, or commercial entity participates in the same deployment ecosystem, the relevant roles must be explicitly separated.

Separation may require distinct:

• legal entities;

• governance bodies;

• bank accounts and treasuries;

• contracts and procurement pathways;

• personnel and delegated roles;

• data controllers and processors;

• databases and access permissions;

• branding and communications;

• donor and contributor records;

• campaign and non-campaign resources;

• public-benefit and electoral activities;

• operational, governance, research, financial, and political decision records.

The degree of separation shall be proportionate to legal, electoral, privacy, public-resource, tax, governance, reputational, and conflict-of-interest risk.

No legal separation may be presumed merely because different names, websites, accounts, wallets, repositories, dashboards, or organisational labels are used.

Substantive independence must be evidenced.

**Adoption and Compatibility Status**

Prometheus may recognise a political, civic, public, educational, philanthropic, community, or institutional actor as:

• mission-aware;

• mission-compatible;

• pilot participant;

• civic deployment partner;

• public-interest implementation partner;

• research partner;

• operational service provider;

• authorised implementation partner;

• under review;

• non-admissible;

• suspended;

• incompatible.

Mission-compatible status does not constitute endorsement of the participant’s entire programme, ideology, leadership, candidates, communications, financing, affiliates, or political activity.

Prometheus compatibility is limited to the defined deployment scope, evidence status, governance decision, legal boundary, and Claims Register record.

**Canonical Neutrality Invariant**

Prometheus may serve a mission without becoming subordinate to the organisation carrying that mission.

It may inform policy without becoming a political doctrine.

It may support public institutions without becoming state-controlled.

It may support communities without appropriating community sovereignty.

It may support capital without becoming capital-controlled.

It may support technology without becoming technology-governed.

It may support political actors without becoming a party platform.

The constitutional rule is:

mission may invite adoption;

evidence governs claims;

law governs rights;

community governs legitimate local participation;

the canon governs Prometheus meaning;

no political actor governs ecological truth.

# Civic and Territorial Deployment Architecture

Prometheus may be deployed not only as a site-level regenerative architecture, but also as a civic and territorial infrastructure through which communities, public institutions, land stewards, schools, municipalities, cooperatives, trusts, philanthropic actors, operators, researchers, and capital providers may coordinate measurable regenerative transition.

A civic or territorial deployment does not create a new source of canonical authority.

It creates a bounded application environment in which Prometheus principles may be implemented across land, institutions, communities, public resources, operating systems, evidence records, and governance structures.

The purpose of the Civic and Territorial Deployment Architecture is to define how regeneration becomes:

• spatially grounded;

• community-legitimate;

• operationally assigned;

• legally authorised;

• publicly reviewable;

• evidentially traceable;

• institutionally governable;

• financially bounded;

• replicable without loss of local context.

Prometheus therefore distinguishes between:

1.  the canonical architecture;

2.  the territorial deployment architecture;

3.  the local implementation contract;

4.  the site-specific operating plan;

5.  the evidence and Claims Register record;

6.  the applicable legal or public-authority instrument;

7.  the financing, procurement, grant, philanthropic, cooperative, or investment pathway.

These layers interact but shall not be collapsed.

A civic deployment is not canon merely because it uses Prometheus terminology.

A municipality does not control the canon because it authorises land use.

A community does not create ecological proof by consensus.

A funder does not create public value by financing an activity.

A public programme does not become effective because it is legally adopted.

A project does not become regenerative because it is politically supported.

A territorial network does not become portfolio-ready because it aggregates sites.

Each layer must pass its own evidence, governance, legal, operational, integrity, and public-claim gates.

**Territorial Deployment Thesis**

Prometheus treats territory as a living system composed of interacting ecological, social, productive, institutional, infrastructural, cultural, and economic relationships.

A territory is not merely a collection of parcels.

It includes:

• soil and water systems;

• habitat and biodiversity;

• food and productive systems;

• human settlements;

• schools and educational institutions;

• public spaces and civic infrastructure;

• local enterprises and cooperatives;

• community organisations;

• public authorities;

• cultural and historical relationships;

• legal rights and responsibilities;

• evidence, data, and institutional memory;

• capital, procurement, grants, and public expenditure.

Territorial regeneration therefore requires more than site design.

It requires coordinated stewardship across multiple actors, scales, time horizons, and forms of accountability.

The controlling territorial principle is:

local context governs design;

evidence governs claims;

law governs rights;

community legitimacy governs participation;

public authority governs authorised public use;

the canon governs Prometheus meaning;

no single actor governs the whole.

**Civic Deployment Layers**

Every Prometheus-compatible civic or territorial deployment shall distinguish at least the following layers.

**Layer 1 — Biophysical and Ecological Foundation**

This layer contains the living systems in which regeneration occurs.

It may include:

• One Human Ecosystems;

• food forests;

• regenerative farms;

• community gardens;

• watershed-restoration systems;

• ecological corridors;

• nurseries;

• public green infrastructure;

• soil-restoration zones;

• water-retention landscapes;

• biodiversity habitats;

• productive and nutritional systems.

This layer generates ecological processes and field evidence.

It does not generate legal rights, public authority, financial value, or policy validation by itself.

**Layer 2 — Civic and Community Stewardship**

This layer contains the people, roles, communities, organisations, and local agreements through which land and infrastructure are stewarded.

It may include:

• community stewardship circles;

• school communities;

• local operators;

• volunteers;

• cooperatives;

• neighbourhood organisations;

• indigenous or traditional authorities;

• religious or charitable communities;

• local businesses;

• residents and beneficiaries;

• designated Earth Custodians;

• Regenerative Operators or Aggregators.

Community participation is necessary for legitimacy and continuity.

Community participation does not replace technical competence, legal authority, safeguarding, evidence, or accountable operations.

**Layer 3 — Public and Institutional Interface**

This layer contains the public bodies and institutional relationships necessary for authorised deployment.

It may include:

• municipalities;

• counties;

• regional authorities;

• schools and school districts;

• universities;

• public-health institutions;

• correctional institutions;

• public-land agencies;

• water authorities;

• housing authorities;

• public procurement bodies;

• community-development institutions;

• public-private partnerships.

Institutional participation may authorise access, funding, procurement, public use, education, maintenance, or programme implementation.

It does not create scientific validation, canonical authority, financial admissibility, or ownership beyond the applicable legal instrument.

**Layer 4 — Evidence, MRV, and Public Accountability**

This layer makes ecological, operational, civic, educational, and public-value claims reviewable.

It may include:

• baseline records;

• site boundaries;

• evidence provenance;

• operational logs;

• ecological indicators;

• maintenance records;

• public expenditure records;

• participation records;

• safeguarding records;

• public Claims Register entries;

• independent review;

• anomaly disclosure;

• missing-data disclosure;

• public dashboards within their non-authoritative boundary.

No public dashboard, report, meeting, testimonial, vote, public statement, photograph, ribbon-cutting, funding award, or media article creates verified performance by itself.

**Layer 5 — Legal, Commons, and Stewardship Custody**

This layer creates and protects legally enforceable relationships concerning land, access, stewardship, maintenance, public use, community benefit, data, infrastructure, and long-term custody.

It may include:

• Community Land Trusts;

• cooperatives;

• public concessions;

• licences;

• leases;

• stewardship agreements;

• conservation easements;

• public-benefit entities;

• charitable trusts;

• asset locks;

• usufruct-like arrangements where legally available;

• maintenance covenants;

• data-governance agreements;

• public-access agreements;

• reversion clauses;

• anti-capture provisions.

Only applicable law and valid instruments create enforceable rights.

Prometheus terminology, dashboards, tokens, governance participation, OHE status, PRU analysis, RAP maturity, or community recognition do not substitute for legal instruments.

**Layer 6 — Economic, Procurement, and Capital Pathways**

This layer contains the financial and resource pathways through which the deployment may be implemented and maintained.

It may include:

• public budgets;

• public procurement;

• grants;

• philanthropy;

• donations;

• cooperative contributions;

• service revenue;

• hospitality revenue;

• food or nursery revenue;

• sponsorship;

• concessional finance;

• project finance;

• blended finance;

• legally structured investment;

• future compliant instruments.

Funding enables activity.

Funding does not prove ecological improvement, public value, economic efficiency, community legitimacy, legal compliance, or financial return.

Every funding source shall remain subordinate to the evidence, stewardship, legal, governance, public-resource, and anti-capture boundaries.

**Layer 7 — Learning, Replication, and Bioregional Coordination**

This layer enables knowledge transfer and responsible scaling.

It may include:

• training;

• apprenticeships;

• school curricula;

• operator certification;

• open methods;

• bioregional learning networks;

• peer review;

• implementation manuals;

• reference sites;

• failure records;

• adaptation records;

• replication packages.

Replication means transferring principles, methods, evidence requirements, and governance boundaries.

It does not mean copying an ecological design, species list, legal wrapper, budget, claim, or operating model without adaptation to local context.

**Non-Bypassable Civic Deployment Sequence**

The civic and territorial sequence is:

territorial need

→ community and institutional legitimacy

→ site and jurisdiction identification

→ legal-use boundary

→ baseline and risk assessment

→ regenerative design

→ stewardship and operator assignment

→ implementation contract

→ pilot deployment

→ evidence and MRV cycle

→ public Claims Register review

→ independent review where required

→ public-value or ecological admissibility decision

→ legal, procurement, funding, or capital pathway

→ replication or network integration.

No later stage legitimises a missing earlier stage.

A grant does not cure the absence of community legitimacy.

Community enthusiasm does not cure the absence of legal authority.

Legal permission does not cure the absence of baseline evidence.

A functioning garden does not cure the absence of maintenance responsibility.

A positive testimonial does not cure the absence of measurement.

A public dashboard does not cure the absence of provenance.

A financial model does not cure the absence of public-value evidence.

Replication does not cure an unresolved failure at the pilot site.

**Civic Deployment Readiness States**

Prometheus defines the following civic and territorial deployment readiness states.

**CTD-0 — Conceptual**

The deployment exists as an idea, policy proposal, community request, political programme, institutional concept, or preliminary site hypothesis.

No operational, performance, public-value, or financial claim is permitted.

**CTD-1 — Territory Identified**

The geography, community, institution, stakeholder environment, potential site, and preliminary need have been identified.

No authorised land-use or implementation claim is permitted.

**CTD-2 — Legal and Institutional Boundary Identified**

The land custodian, public authority, legal-use pathway, institutional sponsor, applicable approvals, and preliminary responsibilities have been identified.

The deployment remains non-operational until permissions and responsibilities are documented.

**CTD-3 — Baseline and Design Ready**

Site boundaries, baseline conditions, risks, community process, regenerative design, safeguarding needs, operating assumptions, maintenance requirements, evidence plan, and Claims Register seed entries are documented.

No improvement claim is permitted before baseline.

**CTD-4 — Pilot Operational**

The pilot has legally and operationally commenced under defined stewardship, maintenance, evidence, data, safeguarding, incident, and review procedures.

Claims are limited to documented implementation and observation.

**CTD-5 — Evidence-Supported**

Repeated evidence exists concerning ecological, operational, educational, civic, productive, or public-value performance.

Claims remain bounded by scope, method, uncertainty, comparator, period, and reviewer status.

**CTD-6 — Independently Reviewed and Institutionally Admitted**

Applicable ecological, operational, public-value, governance, safeguarding, legal, financial, and integrity reviews have been completed.

Only the reviewed outcomes may be used in public, procurement, policy, funding, or counterparty-facing material.

**CTD-7 — Replication-Ready**

The deployment has a documented implementation package, maintenance history, failure record, evidence package, legal model, governance model, training pathway, cost structure, risk register, adaptation rules, and replication decision.

Replication-ready does not mean universally transferable.

Every new territory begins its own site, legal, baseline, community, evidence, and governance cycle.

The CTD ladder is distinct from:

• PRU unit maturity;

• RAP market maturity;

• scientific evidence maturity;

• legal instrument status;

• runtime release status;

• political support;

• funding status.

No ladder may be substituted for another.

**Civic Deployment Archetypes**

Prometheus-compatible civic and territorial deployments may include:

• public-land OHE;

• school food forest;

• community food forest;

• community nursery;

• town-square regenerative hub;

• neighbourhood commons;

• watershed or corridor deployment;

• public-health or care-institution garden;

• correctional or rehabilitation horticulture programme;

• Community Land Trust deployment;

• cooperative regenerative hub;

• municipal resilience node;

• territorial food-security network;

• bioregional OHE cluster.

Each archetype requires its own implementation protocol, legal boundary, safeguarding rules, operator model, evidence plan, Claims Register profile, and acceptance checklist.

No archetype is automatically admissible merely because it is listed in the canon.

**Required Roles and Accountability**

Every deployment shall identify, where applicable:

• land custodian;

• community steward;

• Earth Custodian;

• Regenerative Operator or Aggregator;

• public or institutional sponsor;

• ecological designer;

• MRV lead;

• data steward;

• safeguarding lead;

• legal reviewer;

• financial or procurement lead;

• maintenance owner;

• incident-response owner;

• independent reviewer;

• governance circle;

• Claims Register custodian.

One person may hold multiple roles in a small pilot only where conflicts are disclosed and minimum independence requirements are preserved.

No deployment may rely permanently on unassigned volunteer goodwill for essential maintenance, safety, evidence, legal compliance, or public accountability.

**Public Value and Financial Value Separation**

Civic and territorial deployments may generate ecological, educational, nutritional, social, cultural, institutional, health-related, resilience, productive, and fiscal benefits.

These benefits do not automatically enter PRU value, RAP analysis, financial valuation, investor reporting, public procurement claims, or policy evaluation.

Public value shall be measured through its own evidence and methodology architecture.

Ecological value shall be measured through ecological and MRV methods.

Productive value shall be linked to real outputs, contracts, savings, revenues, or recognised economic pathways.

Financial value shall remain downstream from admissible evidence and legal rights.

The controlling boundary is:

public benefit ≠ financial value;

social participation ≠ ecological performance;

political support ≠ public value;

public expenditure ≠ programme effectiveness;

community approval ≠ scientific validation;

ecological improvement ≠ automatic monetisation.

A civic benefit may become financially relevant only where a defined, evidence-supported, legally recognised and methodologically admitted pathway exists.

**Maintenance and Continuity Rule**

Prometheus treats maintenance as infrastructure.

No food forest, school garden, town square, nursery, commons, corridor, watershed system, civic hub, or public-land deployment is considered operationally successful merely because it has been designed, funded, planted, inaugurated, publicised, or transferred.

Every deployment shall document:

• maintenance responsibilities;

• seasonal operating plan;

• water requirements;

• succession management;

• pruning and biomass cycles;

• replacement planting;

• safety checks;

• public-access procedures;

• volunteer-management limits;

• equipment custody;

• budget continuity;

• staff or operator continuity;

• evidence continuity;

• incident escalation;

• governance succession;

• failure and abandonment procedures.

A deployment without a credible maintenance and continuity plan remains a temporary intervention, not regenerative infrastructure.

**Territorial Anti-Capture Rule**

No municipality, party, foundation, donor, investor, operator, vendor, university, public agency, land custodian, community leader, technical administrator, or digital-platform custodian may use a Prometheus-compatible territorial deployment to obtain unreviewed control over:

• public land;

• community assets;

• ecological claims;

• food access;

• public services;

• data;

• evidence;

• governance;

• local identity;

• PRU or RAP meaning;

• future revenue;

• community participation;

• legal rights;

• public value.

Control rights must be defined, bounded, reviewable, legally valid, proportionate, time-limited where appropriate, and consistent with the public, charitable, community, ecological, or institutional purpose of the deployment.

**Civic Replication Invariant**

Prometheus scales through fidelity to principles, not uniformity of form.

A successful deployment may be replicated only by preserving:

• evidence-before-value;

• local ecological design;

• community legitimacy;

• legal clarity;

• assigned stewardship;

• maintenance continuity;

• claim discipline;

• data dignity;

• political neutrality;

• public-resource protection;

• anti-capture governance;

• independent review where material;

• the rule that the lowest unresolved gate controls the permitted claim.

The purpose of territorial scaling is not to reproduce a brand.

It is to increase the capacity of communities and living systems to regenerate under transparent, locally legitimate, evidence-bound, and legally responsible conditions.

# Public Value and Civic Claims Architecture

Prometheus recognises that regenerative civic and territorial deployments may generate forms of value that are real, material, and institutionally relevant without being financial assets, investor rights, PRU value, RAP value, market price, revenue, collateral, or monetisable claims.

Public value may arise through improvements in ecological function, food access, productive capacity, education, resilience, institutional coordination, public-space quality, community participation, cultural continuity, administrative efficiency, public health determinants, and long-term territorial capacity.

These forms of value shall be measured, reviewed, and reported through a distinct Public Value and Civic Claims Architecture.

They shall not be automatically translated into financial value.

The controlling distinction is:

public value ≠ financial value;

public benefit ≠ investor return;

programme activity ≠ programme outcome;

programme outcome ≠ causal impact;

causal impact ≠ monetised benefit;

monetised benefit ≠ realised saving;

realised saving ≠ cash flow;

cash flow ≠ investor right;

political aspiration ≠ verified result.

**Public Value Definition**

Public value is the evidenced improvement, protection, accessibility, resilience, fairness, functionality, or long-term capacity of a community, institution, territory, public resource, or living system that serves a legitimate public, community, ecological, educational, cultural, or intergenerational purpose.

Public value may be:

• ecological;

• productive;

• nutritional;

• educational;

• social;

• cultural;

• institutional;

• territorial;

• resilience-related;

• administrative;

• fiscal;

• health-supportive;

• intergenerational.

Public value is not presumed merely because a project is public, charitable, ecological, educational, community-led, politically supported, grant-funded, nonprofit, volunteer-driven, or mission-aligned.

A public-purpose programme may fail.

A community programme may produce uneven outcomes.

A food forest may underperform.

A school garden may not improve educational outcomes.

A public investment may create costs without durable benefit.

A popular project may lack measurable impact.

Prometheus therefore treats public value as an evidence-governed proposition, not as an attribute conferred by intention.

**Public Value Domains**

Prometheus distinguishes the following public-value domains.

**1. Ecological Public Value**

Ecological public value may include:

• improved soil function;

• increased infiltration;

• reduced erosion;

• improved water retention;

• habitat formation;

• functional biodiversity;

• reduced bare-soil exposure;

• increased biomass cycling;

• ecological connectivity;

• reduced ecological vulnerability.

Ecological public value shall be governed by ecological evidence and MRV.

It shall not be inferred solely from planting, land designation, species count, visual appearance, public approval, or programme participation.

**2. Food and Productive Public Value**

Food and productive public value may include:

• increased local food availability;

• improved seasonal continuity;

• diversified production;

• nursery capacity;

• seed and plant access;

• reduced external-input dependency;

• improved productive resilience;

• strengthened local processing or distribution;

• increased community access to productive land.

Food production shall be measured in defined units and periods.

Food security shall not be inferred from gross production alone.

Claims concerning food access shall distinguish:

• food produced;

• food harvested;

• food distributed;

• food consumed;

• people reached;

• frequency of access;

• nutritional relevance;

• seasonal continuity;

• affordability;

• reliability.

**3. Educational Public Value**

Educational public value may include:

• ecological literacy;

• practical skills;

• scientific observation;

• food-system knowledge;

• stewardship competence;

• student participation;

• teacher capacity;

• curriculum integration;

• community learning;

• vocational pathways.

Participation in a garden, food forest, nursery, or outdoor programme does not by itself demonstrate educational improvement.

Educational claims require defined outcomes, age-appropriate methods, safeguarding, baseline or comparison where appropriate, and explicit limits on causal interpretation.

**4. Social and Community Public Value**

Social and community public value may include:

• participation;

• trust-building;

• intergenerational exchange;

• volunteer continuity;

• local cooperation;

• conflict-resolution capacity;

• inclusion;

• community stewardship;

• access to shared spaces;

• reduced social isolation.

Attendance, membership, event counts, social-media engagement, volunteer hours, or testimonials are outputs or observations.

They are not sufficient evidence of social cohesion, reduced loneliness, community resilience, crime reduction, or institutional trust.

**5. Health-Supportive Public Value**

Health-supportive public value may include changes in access to nutritious food, physical activity opportunities, restorative environments, social connection, exposure to nature, and health-promoting routines.

Prometheus does not treat such programmes as medical treatment unless they are delivered and evaluated within an applicable clinical, ethical, professional, and legal framework.

The following claims require heightened evidence and review:

• prevention or reversal of disease;

• therapeutic effect;

• reduction in hospitalisation;

• reduction in medication;

• improved mental-health diagnosis;

• reduced mortality;

• reduced recidivism;

• reduced violence;

• clinical cost savings.

Health-supportive conditions may be reported as programme characteristics.

Clinical outcomes require clinical-quality evidence.

**6. Resilience and Territorial Public Value**

Resilience value may include:

• improved drought preparedness;

• flood mitigation;

• local food continuity;

• diversified productive capacity;

• reduced dependency on fragile supply systems;

• emergency-response capacity;

• institutional learning;

• redundancy in essential services;

• community capacity to reorganise under disturbance.

Resilience shall not be claimed solely from diversity, redundancy, infrastructure presence, or scenario modelling.

It must be linked to observed performance, stress testing, operational continuity, or appropriately bounded scenario analysis.

**7. Institutional and Administrative Public Value**

Institutional public value may include:

• reduced duplication;

• improved coordination;

• faster decision cycles;

• clearer accountability;

• improved evidence quality;

• reduced consultant fragmentation;

• more complete data rooms;

• better maintenance assignment;

• improved procurement readiness;

• reduced implementation failure.

Administrative improvement claims require defined process metrics.

Institutional efficiency shall not be inferred solely from the adoption of software, a governance model, a dashboard, a working group, or a Prometheus-compatible methodology.

**8. Fiscal and Avoided-Cost Public Value**

Fiscal public value may include:

• reduced operating cost;

• avoided remediation cost;

• reduced water use;

• reduced input expenditure;

• reduced emergency expenditure;

• improved asset maintenance;

• reduced duplication of studies;

• reduced programme failure;

• deferred infrastructure cost.

Avoided-cost claims require a valid counterfactual.

They shall identify:

• payer;

• beneficiary;

• cost category;

• baseline;

• comparator;

• time horizon;

• probability of occurrence;

• discount rate where relevant;

• implementation cost;

• maintenance cost;

• displacement effects;

• rebound effects;

• attribution share;

• uncertainty range.

A theoretical cost avoidance is not a realised saving.

A realised saving is not automatically available budget.

A budget reduction is not automatically recurring cash flow.

**Civic Claim Classes**

Every material civic or public-value statement shall be classified in the Claims Register.

The minimum claim classes are:

**CC-0 — Constitutional Aspiration**

A statement of ethical or constitutional direction.

Examples include:

• communities should possess greater food resilience;

• public land should generate public benefit;

• capital should not override stewardship.

An aspiration may guide design.

It is not an observed result.

**CC-1 — Policy Objective**

A target adopted by a political, civic, public, or institutional actor.

Examples include:

• expand community food forests;

• improve local food access;

• establish regional nurseries.

A policy objective is not evidence that the target has been achieved.

**CC-2 — Programme Theory**

A causal proposition explaining how an intervention is expected to produce an outcome.

It shall identify:

• inputs;

• activities;

• outputs;

• intermediate outcomes;

• intended impact;

• assumptions;

• external dependencies;

• failure modes.

Programme theory is not validation.

**CC-3 — Output Claim**

A statement about what was delivered.

Examples include:

• hectares planted;

• trees distributed;

• students participating;

• training sessions completed;

• volunteer hours recorded;

• water-storage capacity installed.

Outputs shall be accurately recorded.

They shall not be described as outcomes or impact.

**CC-4 — Observed Outcome**

A measured change occurring within the programme population, site, institution, or territory.

Examples include:

• increased infiltration;

• increased vegetable consumption;

• improved attendance;

• increased local production;

• reduced irrigation demand.

An observed outcome does not establish that the intervention caused the change.

**CC-5 — Attributed Outcome**

A measured change supported by a credible causal design, such as:

• matched comparison;

• before-and-after design with confounder controls;

• difference-in-differences;

• randomised or blocked design;

• synthetic control;

• interrupted time series;

• contribution analysis;

• triangulated mixed-method review.

The attribution method and limitations shall be disclosed.

**CC-6 — Independently Reviewed Public Value**

An attributed or evidence-supported outcome reviewed by a sufficiently independent party using a disclosed method, evidence package, limitations statement, and conflict-of-interest declaration.

Independent review does not mean universal certification.

**CC-7 — Legally or Institutionally Admitted Claim**

A public-value claim accepted for a defined legal, procurement, insurance, grant, budgetary, regulatory, accounting, or institutional use.

Admission is limited to the specific jurisdiction, instrument, programme, method, period, population, and use case.

Institutional admission does not convert public value into an investor right or universal fact.

**Civic Outcome Maturity Ladder**

Prometheus establishes a separate Civic Outcome Maturity ladder:

PVM-0 — aspiration only;

PVM-1 — programme theory documented;

PVM-2 — outputs recorded;

PVM-3 — outcomes observed;

PVM-4 — outcomes evidence-supported;

PVM-5 — attribution reviewed;

PVM-6 — independently reviewed;

PVM-7 — institutionally admitted for a defined use.

The PVM ladder is distinct from:

• evidence maturity;

• CTD deployment maturity;

• PRU-R maturity;

• RAP-M maturity;

• runtime maturity;

• legal-wrapper status;

• financial admissibility.

A programme may be operational at CTD-4 while its outcomes remain at PVM-2.

A programme may have PVM-6 public-value evidence while remaining ineligible for PRU value.

A programme may be institutionally admitted for public procurement without being investor-ready.

No maturity ladder may substitute for another.

**Non-Bypassable Public-Value Sequence**

The public-value sequence is:

public or community need

→ defined beneficiary population

→ programme theory

→ baseline

→ intervention

→ output record

→ outcome measurement

→ evidence quality review

→ attribution analysis where claimed

→ uncertainty and limitation disclosure

→ Claims Register classification

→ independent review where material

→ public reporting

→ institutional or legal admission where applicable

→ optional economic translation.

No later stage legitimises a missing earlier one.

No ROI claim may bypass outcome evidence.

No outcome claim may bypass a defined population.

No attribution claim may bypass a comparator or causal method.

No avoided-cost claim may bypass a counterfactual.

No national claim may be extrapolated from a local pilot without an external-validity analysis.

**Attribution and Causal Discipline**

Prometheus distinguishes correlation, contribution, and causation.

A change observed after an intervention is not automatically caused by the intervention.

Causal interpretation shall consider:

• baseline differences;

• participant selection;

• concurrent programmes;

• weather;

• economic conditions;

• demographic change;

• management differences;

• policy change;

• regression to the mean;

• seasonal effects;

• measurement error;

• missing data;

• survivorship bias;

• reporting bias;

• displacement;

• spillovers.

Where causal identification is not feasible, Prometheus shall use bounded language such as:

• associated with;

• observed alongside;

• consistent with;

• may have contributed to;

• participants reported;

• preliminary evidence suggests.

Language such as “caused,” “reduced,” “prevented,” “generated,” “saved,” “reversed,” or “delivered a return” requires the applicable evidence.

**Public ROI and Social Return Boundary**

Any statement concerning return on investment, social return, public return, cost-benefit ratio, multiplier, savings, or economic benefit shall disclose:

• whose return is being measured;

• whose costs are included;

• whose benefits are included;

• the time horizon;

• the counterfactual;

• the discount rate;

• the attribution factor;

• the deadweight assumption;

• displacement;

• drop-off;

• implementation and maintenance costs;

• uncertainty range;

• sensitivity analysis;

• whether the value is realised, budgetary, contingent, modelled, or non-cash.

The following expressions are prohibited without supporting evidence:

• every dollar generates X dollars;

• guaranteed public saving;

• bankable social value;

• programme pays for itself;

• self-financing programme;

• cost-free transition;

• guaranteed avoided cost;

• universal ROI;

• permanent fiscal saving.

A modelled public-value range shall be labelled as a scenario, not a realised return.

**Special Protection for Health, Education, Crime, and Vulnerable Populations**

Claims concerning children, patients, incarcerated individuals, elderly persons, disabled persons, low-income communities, indigenous communities, migrants, or other vulnerable populations require heightened ethical, privacy, methodological, and safeguarding review.

No programme may use vulnerable status to justify reduced evidence quality or expanded data extraction.

No individual-level outcome shall be published where re-identification, stigma, discrimination, political profiling, or harm is reasonably foreseeable.

The welfare and dignity of participants take precedence over programme promotion, fundraising, political communication, public relations, and investor narratives.

**Public Dashboard Boundary**

A public dashboard may display:

• programme status;

• outputs;

• approved indicators;

• data completeness;

• evidence status;

• uncertainty;

• Claims Register classification;

• reviewer status;

• unresolved risks;

• maintenance status.

A dashboard may not:

• certify impact;

• create legal admission;

• create PRU value;

• create public ROI;

• imply causal attribution not supported by method;

• rank communities by worth;

• compare vulnerable populations without safeguards;

• conceal missing data;

• convert political targets into achieved outcomes.

Dashboard visibility is not evidentiary maturity.

**Civic Claims Register Requirements**

Every material civic claim shall contain, where applicable:

• Civic Claim ID;

• exact claim text;

• claim class;

• public-value domain;

• beneficiary population;

• geographic scope;

• programme period;

• baseline;

• comparator;

• measurement method;

• evidence source;

• attribution method;

• uncertainty;

• confounders;

• reviewer;

• conflict-of-interest status;

• privacy and safeguarding status;

• public-facing permitted wording;

• prohibited wording;

• maturity status;

• next gate;

• expiry or review date.

Claims shall expire, downgrade, or return to review where methods change, data quality deteriorates, programme conditions change, evidence is contradicted, or external validity is exceeded.

**Separation from PRU and RAP**

Public-value evidence may inform contextual analysis, risk assessment, procurement, policy, philanthropy, grant evaluation, or community decision-making.

It may not enter PRU value or RAP capital-facing analysis unless:

1.  the benefit is separately measurable;

2.  the methodology is formally specified;

3.  the evidence is sufficiently mature;

4.  no-double-counting is controlled;

5.  the value pathway is legally recognised;

6.  the beneficiary and payer relationship is defined;

7.  financial relevance is not inferred from public benefit alone;

8.  the Claims Register permits the use;

9.  governance and legal review approve the use.

Public-value indicators shall have zero automatic weight in PRU valuation.

The default rule is:

public_value_weight_in_PRU = 0.

Any deviation requires a distinct, versioned, evidence-supported, governance-approved, legally reviewed methodology.

**Public Value Integrity Invariant**

Prometheus may recognise value without pricing it.

It may measure benefit without converting it into an asset.

It may report outcomes without claiming causation.

It may support public policy without certifying political success.

It may model avoided cost without presenting it as realised saving.

It may document social benefit without converting people into data products.

It may support capital formation without allowing capital to appropriate public value.

The constitutional rule is:

activities are recorded;

outputs are counted;

outcomes are measured;

causation is tested;

public value is reviewed;

financial meaning is separate;

no benefit becomes value merely because the narrative is compelling.

# Public-Land One Human Ecosystem Protocol

The Public-Land One Human Ecosystem Protocol governs any Prometheus-compatible One Human Ecosystem, food forest, nursery, regenerative garden, ecological corridor, water-retention landscape, productive commons, or related regenerative infrastructure deployed on land that is publicly owned, publicly controlled, institutionally held, charitably held, communally held, licensed for public benefit, or subject to public-access obligations.

For the purposes of this Protocol, public-land deployment may include land held or controlled by:

• municipalities;

• counties or regional authorities;

• public agencies;

• schools and educational institutions;

• universities;

• public-health institutions;

• correctional institutions;

• housing authorities;

• transport or infrastructure authorities;

• water or watershed authorities;

• churches or religious institutions providing public-benefit access;

• charitable foundations;

• Community Land Trusts;

• cooperatives;

• indigenous or traditional authorities;

• community organisations;

• private owners granting defined public-benefit use.

The use of the term “public land” within Prometheus does not determine legal ownership, public-law classification, access rights, immunity, title, possession, easement, concession, tenancy, licence, usufruct, maintenance liability, or public-procurement status.

These matters are governed only by the applicable jurisdiction and valid legal instruments.

**Public-Land Boundary**

A Public-Land OHE does not, by itself, create:

• ownership;

• tenancy;

• adverse possession;

• easement;

• usufruct;

• exclusive access;

• harvesting rights;

• commercial rights;

• governance rights;

• development rights;

• water rights;

• data ownership;

• carbon rights;

• biodiversity rights;

• ecological-credit rights;

• PRU rights;

• RAP rights;

• token rights;

• revenue rights;

• collateral;

• compensation rights;

• renewal rights;

• permanent occupancy.

Planting, maintaining, financing, measuring, volunteering, designing, donating, operating, or participating in a Public-Land OHE does not create any such right unless a valid legal instrument explicitly establishes it.

The controlling rule is:

public use does not equal ownership;

stewardship does not equal possession;

maintenance does not equal title;

funding does not equal control;

participation does not equal entitlement;

ecological improvement does not equal private appropriation;

Prometheus compatibility does not equal land admission.

**Public-Land Activation Sequence**

A Public-Land OHE may progress only through the following sequence:

public or community need

→ land and authority identification

→ title and control review

→ authorised-use pathway

→ stakeholder and community process

→ site baseline

→ legal, environmental, safety, access, and operational review

→ regenerative design

→ stewardship and operator assignment

→ maintenance and funding plan

→ evidence and Claims Register plan

→ written implementation instrument

→ installation approval

→ pilot implementation

→ monitoring and review

→ continuation, adaptation, replication, suspension, or decommissioning decision.

No stage may be bypassed because:

• the land appears unused;

• the project has political support;

• volunteers are available;

• plants have been donated;

• funds have been raised;

• public demand exists;

• the design is ecologically sound;

• the intervention is reversible;

• the project is temporary;

• the operator has previously managed similar land.

A later approval does not retroactively legitimise an unauthorised intervention.

**Public-Land OHE Readiness States**

Prometheus establishes a Public-Land OHE readiness ladder.

**PL-OHE-0 — Proposed**

A site, public need, political proposal, community idea, institutional request, or preliminary design has been identified.

No land-use, implementation, ecological-performance, or public-benefit claim is permitted.

**PL-OHE-1 — Custodian Identified**

The apparent owner, controlling authority, institutional custodian, land manager, or competent public body has been identified.

Identification does not constitute authorisation.

**PL-OHE-2 — Legal-Use Pathway Identified**

The required licence, concession, agreement, lease, permit, procurement route, partnership, easement, public-benefit arrangement, or other lawful pathway has been identified.

No site work may begin until the applicable written authority is effective.

**PL-OHE-3 — Dossier Complete**

The site baseline, design, operating model, maintenance plan, risk assessment, public-access plan, evidence plan, data-governance plan, funding plan, produce-use plan, and Claims Register seed entries are complete.

No improvement claim is permitted before baseline.

**PL-OHE-4 — Authorised Pilot**

The project has received the applicable written approval and may begin within the defined scope, period, budget, site boundary, access rules, and stewardship responsibilities.

Claims are limited to implementation and observation.

**PL-OHE-5 — Operationally Stable**

The deployment has completed at least one defined operating cycle with maintenance continuity, evidence completeness, incident review, plant-survival review, water-performance review, budget review, and stakeholder review.

Operational stability is not equivalent to ecological validation.

**PL-OHE-6 — Evidence-Supported Public Infrastructure**

The deployment has sufficient repeated evidence to support defined ecological, productive, public-value, operational, or educational claims within the approved scope.

Only gate-cleared claims may be used publicly.

**PL-OHE-7 — Long-Term Stewardship Admitted**

A durable legal, operational, maintenance, budget, governance, public-access, evidence, and continuity arrangement has been admitted for the defined period and use.

Long-term admission does not create permanent private ownership, universal replication readiness, or investor rights.

The PL-OHE ladder is distinct from:

• CTD civic deployment maturity;

• PVM public-value maturity;

• PRU-R unit maturity;

• RAP-M market maturity;

• legal-wrapper status;

• funding status;

• political support;

• scientific evidence maturity;

• runtime admission.

No ladder may substitute for another.

**Mandatory Public-Land OHE Dossier**

Before implementation, each Public-Land OHE shall maintain a versioned dossier containing, where applicable:

1.  site identifier;

2.  geospatial boundary;

3.  landowner or controlling custodian;

4.  competent approving authority;

5.  title, licence, concession, lease, agreement, permit, or use-right reference;

6.  authorised use;

7.  duration;

8.  renewal conditions;

9.  termination conditions;

10. reversion conditions;

11. public-access status;

12. accessibility requirements;

13. existing utilities;

14. water source and water-use authority;

15. soil baseline;

16. contamination or prior-use review;

17. drainage and flood review;

18. fire-risk review;

19. ecological baseline;

20. existing habitat constraints;

21. protected-species or heritage constraints where applicable;

22. design plan;

23. species plan;

24. invasive-species controls;

25. maintenance plan;

26. seasonal operating calendar;

27. operator and steward assignments;

28. safeguarding and public-safety plan;

29. food-safety and produce-use plan;

30. volunteer-management plan;

31. equipment and tool-custody plan;

32. waste and biomass-management plan;

33. incident-response plan;

34. liability and insurance review;

35. procurement and expenditure pathway;

36. data-governance plan;

37. MRV plan;

38. public Claims Register entries;

39. public communications boundary;

40. budget and continuity plan;

41. decommissioning or transition plan;

42. decision records;

43. conflict-of-interest disclosures.

The dossier shall distinguish confirmed facts, assumptions, pending approvals, design decisions, risks, conditions, and unresolved matters.

A visually complete dossier is not an admitted dossier if the underlying permissions, evidence, assignments, or resources are absent.

**Site Admissibility**

A Public-Land OHE shall not be admitted merely because land is available.

Site admissibility shall consider:

• legal availability;

• community relevance;

• public-benefit relevance;

• soil condition;

• contamination risk;

• water availability;

• hydrological function;

• climate and microclimate;

• topography;

• access;

• accessibility;

• vandalism or misuse risk;

• fire exposure;

• flooding;

• public safety;

• maintenance feasibility;

• staffing or operator capacity;

• procurement constraints;

• neighbouring land use;

• existing ecological value;

• heritage value;

• infrastructure conflicts;

• long-term continuity.

An ecologically degraded area may be suitable for regeneration but require a higher remediation, safety, legal, or cost threshold.

An ecologically valuable unmanaged area shall not be treated as vacant or underperforming merely because it lacks visible human production.

Prometheus shall not replace existing habitat, spontaneous ecological succession, culturally significant landscapes, or valuable public open space with productive planting without a justified ecological and public-interest analysis.

**Design and Ecological Integrity**

The regenerative design shall be site-specific.

It may integrate:

• syntropic agroforestry;

• food-forest design;

• Keyline or hydrological design;

• rainwater harvesting;

• soil restoration;

• biomass cycling;

• perennial crops;

• native or climate-adapted species;

• biodiversity habitat;

• nursery production;

• public education;

• community food production.

The design shall disclose:

• expected functions;

• expected yields;

• anticipated succession;

• water demand;

• management intensity;

• establishment period;

• potential failure modes;

• species risks;

• public-access impacts;

• maintenance burden;

• ecological trade-offs.

No species, structure, earthwork, pond, water diversion, irrigation system, access path, fence, lighting system, storage unit, sign, building element, or permanent installation may be introduced without the applicable approval.

**Public Access and Inclusion**

Public ownership does not automatically require unrestricted access.

Access conditions shall be defined according to:

• safety;

• safeguarding;

• ecological protection;

• operating hours;

• accessibility;

• harvesting rules;

• maintenance periods;

• school or institutional use;

• protected habitat;

• event management;

• equipment security;

• seasonal conditions.

Where access is intended to be public or community-based, participation rules should be transparent, proportionate, non-discriminatory, and consistent with the legal and institutional purpose of the site.

Access to food, plants, education, land, programmes, volunteer roles, or public benefits shall not be conditioned on:

• political support;

• party membership;

• campaign participation;

• religious conformity;

• investor status;

• token ownership;

• financial contribution;

• data surrender beyond what is necessary and lawful;

• ideological agreement unrelated to the legitimate programme purpose.

**Stewardship and Operator Responsibility**

Every Public-Land OHE shall identify a responsible operator or stewardship body.

Essential responsibilities shall not depend indefinitely on informal volunteer goodwill.

The operator shall be accountable for:

• implementation;

• maintenance;

• water management;

• plant health;

• public safety;

• equipment;

• records;

• evidence;

• communications;

• incident escalation;

• budget use;

• stakeholder reporting;

• end-of-term transition.

Volunteer participation may supplement but shall not replace responsible assignment for critical functions.

Prometheus shall distinguish:

• land custodian;

• legal authority;

• programme sponsor;

• designer;

• operator;

• steward;

• volunteer;

• beneficiary;

• funder;

• reviewer.

No role shall be interpreted as controlling all others.

**Maintenance Continuity**

A Public-Land OHE is not considered successful because it has been planted or inaugurated.

The maintenance plan shall define:

• watering;

• irrigation inspection;

• pruning;

• succession management;

• biomass return;

• soil cover;

• invasive-species management;

• pest and disease response;

• replacement planting;

• harvesting;

• pathway maintenance;

• public-safety inspections;

• tool storage;

• waste removal;

• signage;

• data collection;

• seasonal labour;

• absence cover;

• drought response;

• storm response;

• vandalism response;

• budget continuity.

Failure to maintain shall trigger review, remediation, suspension, redesign, transfer, or decommissioning.

Plant mortality, neglected infrastructure, unsafe access, uncontrolled invasives, water failure, repeated complaints, incomplete evidence, or absent operators shall be treated as operational signals, not hidden as reputational risks.

**Food, Harvest, and Produce-Use Boundary**

Where food or plant products are generated, the deployment shall define:

• who may harvest;

• what may be harvested;

• when harvesting is permitted;

• whether produce is free, donated, sold, processed, used institutionally, or reserved for programme participants;

• food-safety responsibilities;

• traceability requirements;

• handling and storage;

• allergen and contamination risks;

• commercial or procurement requirements;

• benefit-allocation rules;

• prohibited appropriation;

• surplus management.

Publicly accessible produce is not automatically ownerless.

Community participation does not automatically create equal harvesting rights.

Operator custody does not automatically create commercial ownership.

Donated plants do not automatically entitle donors to future produce, data, publicity, ecological credits, PRU value, or governance authority.

Food access and distribution claims shall distinguish food produced, harvested, distributed, received, consumed, wasted, or lost.

**Water and Environmental Resource Boundary**

The deployment shall identify the source, legality, availability, cost, quality, and continuity of water.

Rainwater harvesting, stormwater diversion, wells, surface water, public water, greywater, treated water, or irrigation infrastructure shall be used only within the applicable legal, environmental, health, safety, and technical boundaries.

A Public-Land OHE shall not claim water resilience merely because water-retention structures exist.

Water performance shall be evaluated through defined indicators such as:

• infiltration;

• soil moisture persistence;

• runoff reduction;

• irrigation demand;

• storage reliability;

• erosion control;

• water quality;

• seasonal sufficiency.

Hydrological interventions shall not transfer flooding, erosion, salinity, contamination, or drainage risk to neighbouring land or infrastructure.

**Data, MRV, and Public Reporting**

Public-Land OHE data shall be purpose-limited, proportionate, protected, and clearly governed.

The data plan shall distinguish:

• ecological data;

• operational data;

• public-expenditure data;

• volunteer data;

• participant data;

• school data;

• health-related data;

• geolocation data;

• imagery;

• incident data;

• political or civic data.

Ecological and operational records shall not be combined with voter profiling, political persuasion, commercial surveillance, social scoring, or unrelated behavioural analytics.

Public reporting shall disclose:

• claim status;

• data completeness;

• evidence period;

• methodology;

• uncertainty;

• unresolved risks;

• maintenance status;

• funding source;

• reviewer status;

• material incidents.

No dashboard or map creates legal authority, land rights, ecological truth, public value, PRU value, or political validation.

**Procurement, Donations, and Public Resources**

Public money, donated goods, volunteer labour, plants, equipment, professional services, sponsorship, grants, and philanthropic contributions shall be recorded according to the applicable legal, accounting, procurement, tax, charitable, public-resource, and conflict-of-interest requirements.

The project shall disclose material in-kind contributions and related-party transactions.

A donor, vendor, political sponsor, funder, operator, or technology provider shall not receive undisclosed control over:

• land;

• produce;

• data;

• branding;

• governance;

• ecological claims;

• procurement;

• future revenue;

• PRU or RAP status;

• public access;

• continuation or termination.

Naming, sponsorship, and communications rights shall remain proportionate and shall not convert a public-benefit asset into a proprietary political or commercial platform.

**Liability, Safety, and Incident Governance**

Every deployment shall maintain an incident and escalation framework covering, where applicable:

• personal injury;

• unsafe structures;

• water hazards;

• contaminated soil or produce;

• allergic reactions;

• tool or machinery incidents;

• fire;

• flooding;

• vandalism;

• theft;

• safeguarding concerns;

• data breach;

• harassment or conflict;

• animal interaction;

• pesticide or chemical exposure;

• infrastructure failure;

• unauthorised harvesting;

• food-safety events.

Incidents shall be documented, reviewed, escalated, and reflected in the risk register and Claims Register where material.

A positive public narrative shall not suppress adverse-event reporting.

**Termination, Reversion, and Decommissioning**

Every Public-Land OHE shall define what occurs when:

• the licence expires;

• the public authority withdraws approval;

• the operator fails;

• funding ends;

• maintenance becomes unsustainable;

• safety requirements are breached;

• land use changes;

• the project is relocated;

• community consent materially deteriorates;

• ecological harm is identified;

• the site becomes incompatible with public use.

The transition or decommissioning plan shall address:

• plant and infrastructure ownership;

• soil and land restoration;

• removal obligations;

• water systems;

• tools and equipment;

• stored data;

• records retention;

• produce;

• volunteer and beneficiary communication;

• outstanding liabilities;

• handover;

• continuation by another steward.

Decommissioning shall not erase the evidence record.

Failure and termination data are part of the institutional learning architecture.

**Public-Land Anti-Capture Invariant**

A Public-Land OHE exists to serve its admitted ecological, public, community, educational, productive, or institutional purpose.

It shall not be used to create an undisclosed pathway for:

• political patronage;

• campaign mobilisation;

• private land appropriation;

• exclusive commercial control;

• speculative land value capture;

• token-backed control;

• unreviewed ecological credits;

• private ownership of public evidence;

• governance capture;

• community displacement;

• public-subsidy extraction;

• reputational laundering;

• investor claims unsupported by rights or evidence.

The controlling principle is:

public land remains governed by public or legally admitted purpose;

community stewardship remains accountable;

ecological claims remain evidence-bound;

private benefit remains disclosed and bounded;

no actor may appropriate value merely because it planted, funded, measured, operated, or publicised the site.

**Public-Land OHE Admission Gate**

A Public-Land OHE may be described as operational only when:

• the competent authority is identified;

• the authorised use is documented;

• the site boundary is defined;

• baseline and risk review are complete;

• the design is approved;

• stewardship and maintenance roles are assigned;

• water and safety pathways are viable;

• the produce-use pathway is defined;

• the MRV and data plan are active;

• the funding and continuity plan are credible;

• incident and decommissioning procedures exist;

• Claims Register entries are created;

• unresolved limitations are disclosed.

It may be described as evidence-supported only when the relevant observations and outcomes have passed the applicable evidence and public-value gates.

It may be described as long-term regenerative infrastructure only when ecological performance, operational continuity, legal custody, maintenance capacity, public purpose, governance, and evidence have converged over the applicable period.

The lowest unresolved Public-Land OHE gate controls the permitted claim.

# School Food Forest and Child Safeguarding Protocol

The School Food Forest and Child Safeguarding Protocol governs every Prometheus-compatible One Human Ecosystem, food forest, regenerative garden, nursery, outdoor classroom, biodiversity area, water-harvesting system, ecological learning site, or related programme involving children, minors, students, schools, early-childhood settings, educational institutions, youth organisations, families, or child-facing community activities.

For the purposes of this Protocol, the meaning of child, minor, student, guardian, school, educator, safeguarding authority, consent, assent, special-category data, and educational duty shall be determined by applicable law and institutional policy.

Prometheus does not establish a universal legal age, educational status, safeguarding standard, or parental-authority rule.

The applicable jurisdiction, school authority, educational institution, safeguarding framework, and valid legal instruments govern those matters.

**Child-Protection Boundary**

A Prometheus-compatible educational deployment shall protect the child as:

• a rights-bearing person;

• a learner;

• a participant;

• a potential beneficiary;

• a developing individual;

• a member of a family and community;

• a person entitled to safety, dignity, privacy, inclusion, and age-appropriate participation.

A child shall not be treated as:

• an ecological data source by default;

• a behavioural dataset;

• a political audience;

• a marketing asset;

• an impact testimonial;

• a financial indicator;

• a governance-weighting input;

• a social-score subject;

• a biometric research object;

• a source of investor-facing evidence without appropriate safeguards.

The controlling rule is:

child welfare precedes programme delivery;

safeguarding precedes data collection;

education precedes promotion;

consent precedes optional participation;

evidence precedes outcome claims;

no programme objective overrides the dignity or safety of the child.

**Scope of the Protocol**

This Protocol applies where a Prometheus-compatible deployment involves one or more of the following:

• school-owned or school-controlled land;

• students participating during school hours;

• extracurricular youth activities;

• school food service;

• curriculum delivery;

• educational research;

• photographs, video, audio, or student-created media;

• student or family data;

• food harvesting, preparation, tasting, or distribution;

• tools, machinery, water features, animals, composting, soil, or biological materials;

• volunteers, visitors, contractors, researchers, political actors, funders, or external organisations interacting with minors;

• public events involving children;

• transport of students or produce;

• publication of student outcomes;

• digital platforms or AI systems processing educational or child-related information.

Where the deployment also uses public, municipal, charitable, religious, institutional, or community land, the Public-Land One Human Ecosystem Protocol applies concurrently.

The stricter applicable safeguarding, educational, public-land, privacy, food-safety, or legal requirement governs.

**School Food Forest Constitutional Purpose**

A School Food Forest may support:

• ecological literacy;

• soil and water education;

• nutrition and food-system learning;

• practical science;

• biodiversity observation;

• climate adaptation education;

• stewardship;

• cooperation;

• sensory and experiential learning;

• vocational skills;

• intergenerational learning;

• community participation;

• local food production;

• curriculum integration.

These purposes are programme objectives.

They are not automatically achieved outcomes.

The existence of a garden, food forest, nursery, outdoor classroom, or ecological programme does not prove:

• improved academic performance;

• improved attention;

• improved behaviour;

• improved physical health;

• improved mental health;

• reduced absenteeism;

• increased vegetable consumption;

• reduced social isolation;

• improved family food security;

• improved community cohesion.

Such outcomes require their own evidence, methodology, ethical review, Claims Register classification, and public-value maturity status.

**School Food Forest Readiness States**

Prometheus establishes a School Food Forest readiness ladder.

**SFF-0 — Educational Concept**

A school, family group, public institution, community, educator, or partner has proposed an educational regenerative deployment.

No operational, educational-outcome, health, nutritional, or public-value claim is permitted.

**SFF-1 — Institutional Sponsor Identified**

The responsible school, educational authority, property custodian, programme sponsor, and preliminary safeguarding authority have been identified.

Identification does not constitute permission.

**SFF-2 — Legal, Safeguarding, and Land-Use Pathway Identified**

The required school approvals, land-use permissions, safeguarding standards, insurance pathway, data-protection responsibilities, food-safety pathway, and operator obligations have been identified.

No child-facing activity may begin before the applicable controls are active.

**SFF-3 — Design and Safeguarding Dossier Complete**

The site design, curriculum relationship, child-safety assessment, accessibility plan, maintenance plan, produce-use plan, data-governance plan, consent requirements, operator roles, incident procedures, and Claims Register seed entries are complete.

No student-outcome claim is permitted before baseline or defined starting conditions.

**SFF-4 — Authorised Educational Pilot**

The educational pilot may begin within the approved scope, age group, timetable, staffing model, site boundary, supervision rules, safeguarding procedures, and data limitations.

Claims are restricted to implementation, participation, and recorded outputs.

**SFF-5 — Operationally Stable**

The programme has completed at least one defined educational and maintenance cycle, including school closure periods where applicable.

Safety, plant survival, maintenance, participation, food handling, incidents, accessibility, data use, and teacher workload have been reviewed.

Operational stability is not equivalent to educational effectiveness.

**SFF-6 — Evidence-Supported Educational Infrastructure**

Repeated evidence supports defined ecological, educational, productive, nutritional, participation, or public-value outcomes within the admitted scope.

Only Claims Register-approved wording may be used.

**SFF-7 — Independently Reviewed and Replication-Ready**

The programme has an independently reviewed evidence package, safeguarding record, curriculum model, operator handbook, maintenance history, legal and data-governance framework, cost structure, training pathway, risk register, failure record, and adaptation protocol.

Replication readiness does not eliminate the need for a new legal, safeguarding, ecological, curricular, and institutional review in each school or jurisdiction.

The SFF ladder is distinct from:

• PL-OHE readiness;

• CTD territorial maturity;

• PVM public-value maturity;

• PRU-R maturity;

• RAP-M maturity;

• scientific evidence maturity;

• funding status;

• political support;

• legal-wrapper status.

No ladder may substitute for another.

**Mandatory School Food Forest Dossier**

Before child-facing implementation, the programme shall maintain a versioned dossier containing, where applicable:

1.  site identifier and boundary;

2.  landowner and educational authority;

3.  responsible school or institution;

4.  authorised educational use;

5.  age groups and participant categories;

6.  programme schedule;

7.  curriculum relationship;

8.  educational objectives;

9.  safeguarding framework;
