10. designated safeguarding lead;

11. responsible teacher or educator;

12. operator and maintenance roles;

13. external partner roles;

14. volunteer roles;

15. supervision arrangements;

16. accessibility and inclusion plan;

17. soil and contamination review;

18. water-quality and water-safety review;

19. plant and species plan;

20. toxic, allergenic, invasive, irritant, thorned, or otherwise hazardous species controls;

21. tool and equipment plan;

22. machinery exclusion or control plan;

23. weather and heat-exposure plan;

24. first-aid and emergency plan;

25. incident and safeguarding reporting pathway;

26. food-safety plan;

27. produce-use and distribution rules;

28. allergen and dietary-requirement controls;

29. hygiene and handwashing arrangements;

30. photograph, video, and communications policy;

31. child and family data-governance plan;

32. consent and assent requirements;

33. research and evaluation boundary;

34. political and commercial neutrality boundary;

35. visitor and contractor controls;

36. transport arrangements where applicable;

37. maintenance during holidays and closures;

38. budget and resource continuity;

39. Claims Register entries;

40. review and termination procedures.

The dossier shall identify unresolved risks, pending approvals, excluded activities, and conditions that would require suspension.

**Safeguarding Governance**

Every child-facing deployment shall identify a safeguarding authority and escalation pathway consistent with applicable law and institutional policy.

The safeguarding framework shall define:

• who is responsible for child protection;

• who may supervise children;

• who may access the site;

• how visitors, volunteers, contractors, researchers, and external partners are authorised;

• applicable background checks or suitability checks;

• supervision ratios where required;

• visibility or two-adult practices where required;

• one-to-one interaction rules;

• transport rules;

• photography and recording rules;

• handling of disclosures or concerns;

• mandatory reporting duties;

• emergency contacts;

• incident documentation;

• suspension and exclusion procedures.

Prometheus does not replace statutory safeguarding systems.

A Prometheus governance circle, DAO, token vote, community decision, school club, parent group, donor, operator, AI system, or technical platform may not override the designated safeguarding authority.

Where safeguarding and programme continuity conflict, safeguarding controls.

**Child Consent, Guardian Consent, and Assent**

Participation conditions shall distinguish:

• activities that form part of the authorised educational curriculum;

• optional extracurricular participation;

• public events;

• food tasting;

• media participation;

• data collection;

• research participation;

• health-related assessment;

• photography, audio, or video recording;

• publication of student work;

• geolocation or digital-platform use.

Where guardian consent is legally or institutionally required, it shall be specific, informed, understandable, and revocable within the applicable limits.

Where child assent is appropriate, it shall be sought in age-appropriate language.

Refusal to participate in optional:

• research;

• photography;

• public communications;

• biometric collection;

• political communications;

• promotional activities;

• data collection beyond educational necessity

shall not result in punishment, loss of essential educational access, reduced status, or exclusion from unrelated programme benefits.

Consent to one activity does not constitute consent to all activities.

**Human Dignity and No-Scoring Rule**

No child-related physiological, biometric, psychological, emotional, behavioural, cognitive, social, attention, stress, health, academic, family, or participation data may enter:

• PRU valuation;

• RAP underwriting;

• investor reporting;

• token allocation;

• governance weighting;

• land access;

• food access;

• programme eligibility;

• public ranking;

• political profiling;

• commercial advertising profiles.

The technical rule remains:

human_state_weight = 0

for PRU value, RAP maturity, Conf_total, LegalGate, MRVGate, investor-facing claims, token rights, land rights, governance authority, and public-benefit eligibility.

Prometheus shall not assign children a:

• regeneration score;

• stewardship worth score;

• social-coherence score;

• ecological citizenship score;

• behavioural worth score;

• investor-readiness score;

• political-engagement score.

Learning progress may be assessed for legitimate educational purposes under applicable educational rules.

Educational assessment shall not be converted into a generalised human-value or governance score.

**Data Protection and Digital Safety**

Child-related data shall be purpose-limited, minimised, protected, access-controlled, and retained only for a defined period.

The programme shall distinguish:

• necessary educational records;

• safeguarding records;

• participation records;

• research data;

• ecological observations made by students;

• photographs and media;

• health or allergy information;

• geolocation;

• digital account information;

• public communications material.

The following practices are prohibited unless separately lawful, necessary, proportionate, reviewed, and explicitly admitted:

• facial recognition;

• emotion recognition;

• covert monitoring;

• continuous behavioural surveillance;

• political profiling;

• commercial advertising profiles;

• biometric ranking;

• automated eligibility decisions;

• public release of identifiable geolocation;

• combining child data with voter, donor, investor, token, or marketing databases;

• using AI outputs as safeguarding, disciplinary, medical, or psychological authority.

AI may assist educators or operators with administrative, ecological, or curricular tasks only within an approved and reviewable scope.

AI shall not autonomously determine a child’s wellbeing, risk status, aptitude, character, political orientation, educational value, or eligibility.

**Photography, Video, and Public Communications**

Images of children, student names, school identifiers, location data, schedules, personal stories, quotations, artwork, or programme outcomes shall be used only within the applicable consent, safeguarding, privacy, educational, and institutional boundaries.

A child’s image or testimony shall not be used to imply:

• verified ecological success;

• proven educational impact;

• clinical benefit;

• community transformation;

• political support;

• endorsement of a party, candidate, funder, investor, donor, token, company, or technology platform.

No child shall be pressured to produce:

• testimonial content;

• campaign content;

• promotional videos;

• donor messages;

• investor-facing narratives;

• political statements;

• emotional disclosure.

Where media participation is optional, an equivalent non-public educational pathway shall remain available.

**Political, Ideological, and Commercial Neutrality**

A School Food Forest may support civic education, ecological responsibility, community participation, and public-policy literacy.

It shall not be used to condition educational access or programme benefits on:

• support for a political party;

• support for a candidate;

• participation in a campaign;

• religious conformity;

• investor status;

• token ownership;

• donor status;

• commercial product endorsement;

• ideological agreement unrelated to legitimate educational objectives.

Parties, candidates, elected officials, funders, companies, foundations, charities, or advocacy organisations participating in public events shall remain subject to the school’s legal, safeguarding, neutrality, procurement, communications, and access rules.

Student data, family data, photographs, programme records, food-distribution records, volunteer records, or contact information shall not be transferred to political or campaign systems.

**Site and Physical Safety**

The site risk assessment shall consider:

• soil contamination;

• water contamination;

• sharp tools;

• machinery;

• irrigation systems;

• hoses and trip hazards;

• ponds or open water;

• retaining structures;

• unstable branches;

• thorned or toxic plants;

• poisonous fungi;

• allergenic species;

• insects;

• animal interactions;

• compost temperatures;

• manure;

• chemicals;

• sun exposure;

• heat;

• cold;

• storms;

• fire;

• flooding;

• slippery surfaces;

• accessibility;

• storage;

• unauthorised access.

Tools and activities shall be age-appropriate and supervised.

The programme shall define:

• permitted tools by age or competence;

• required protective equipment;

• safe storage;

• inspection;

• maintenance;

• first-aid arrangements;

• emergency stop procedures;

• weather-related suspension thresholds;

• hydration and shade access;

• hygiene procedures.

No child shall operate machinery, chemicals, irrigation infrastructure, elevated equipment, cutting tools, fire, or hazardous systems outside the applicable legal, institutional, age, training, and supervision requirements.

**Plant and Ecological Safety**

The species plan shall identify:

• edible species;

• non-edible species;

• toxic species;

• allergenic species;

• irritant species;

• invasive species;

• medicinal species;

• plants requiring specialist identification;

• plants unsuitable for unsupervised harvesting.

Children shall not be encouraged to taste or consume unidentified plants, fungi, berries, roots, seeds, or plant materials.

Educational interpretation shall distinguish between:

• edible;

• medicinal;

• ornamental;

• habitat-supporting;

• toxic;

• restricted-use plants.

Where pesticides, herbicides, fungicides, fertilisers, composts, manures, soil amendments, or biological controls are used, the programme shall document their identity, purpose, application, storage, exclusion period, and applicable safety controls.

A regenerative label does not remove chemical, biological, or toxicological risk.

**Food Safety and Produce Use**

Where students harvest, prepare, taste, cook, distribute, or consume produce, the programme shall define:

• authorised produce;

• harvesting supervision;

• washing;

• potable-water requirements;

• hand hygiene;

• storage;

• temperature control;

• contamination prevention;

• allergen management;

• dietary restrictions;

• kitchen or food-service integration;

• traceability;

• incident response;

• product withdrawal or recall where applicable.

The programme shall distinguish:

• produce grown;

• produce harvested;

• produce accepted by food service;

• produce distributed;

• produce consumed;

• produce composted or discarded.

Gross production does not establish student consumption, nutritional improvement, food security, or economic saving.

Food shall not be used as a reward for political, religious, promotional, research, or data participation.

**Curriculum and Educational Integrity**

A School Food Forest programme shall identify how activities relate to legitimate educational objectives.

Potential curriculum domains may include:

• biology;

• ecology;

• chemistry;

• geography;

• mathematics;

• food systems;

• nutrition;

• environmental science;

• technology;

• design;

• civic education;

• art;

• vocational education.

The programme shall not claim curriculum integration merely because activities occur during school hours.

Curriculum integration requires:

• defined learning objectives;

• age-appropriate activities;

• educator responsibility;

• accessible participation;

• assessment boundaries;

• safeguarding;

• timetable feasibility;

• teacher workload consideration;

• review of educational outcomes.

The food forest shall not displace essential educational provision without appropriate institutional approval.

**Inclusion and Accessibility**

The programme shall consider the participation of students with:

• physical disabilities;

• sensory disabilities;

• neurodivergence;

• allergies;

• chronic conditions;

• dietary restrictions;

• cultural or religious food requirements;

• language needs;

• economic barriers;

• safeguarding vulnerabilities.

Reasonable adjustments shall be identified where required.

Participation shall not depend on a family’s ability to:

• donate money;

• provide equipment;

• volunteer;

• purchase clothing;

• provide transport;

• consent to publicity;

• participate in fundraising;

• join an external organisation.

Where certain activities cannot be safely accessed, an equivalent educational pathway should be provided where feasible.

**Research and Evaluation Boundary**

Educational evaluation and research shall be distinguished.

Routine programme monitoring may include:

• attendance;

• activities completed;

• ecological observations;

• outputs;

• maintenance records;

• curriculum delivery;

• incidents.

Research involving children may require additional:

• ethics review;

• institutional approval;

• guardian consent;

• child assent;

• research protocol;

• data-protection assessment;

• registration;

• control or comparison design;

• adverse-event monitoring;

• publication rules.

Research participation shall not be disguised as ordinary education.

Children shall not be induced to participate through inappropriate pressure, essential-benefit conditioning, political messaging, public recognition, financial incentives, or academic penalty.

External researchers shall not assume access merely because the programme is public, charitable, educational, or Prometheus-compatible.

**Educational and Health Claims Boundary**

Claims concerning children shall follow the Public Value and Civic Claims Architecture.

The following claims require defined outcome evidence:

• improved learning;

• improved attention;

• improved memory;

• improved behaviour;

• improved wellbeing;

• improved diet;

• reduced obesity;

• reduced stress;

• reduced absenteeism;

• improved social cohesion;

• reduced bullying;

• improved mental health;

• improved physical health.

Claims concerning diagnosis, treatment, prevention, disease reversal, clinical improvement, or therapeutic efficacy require clinical-quality evidence and applicable professional, ethical, and legal review.

Permitted early-stage language may include:

• provides opportunities for outdoor learning;

• supports practical ecological education;

• increases access to gardening activities;

• creates opportunities for food-system learning;

• enables supervised contact with living systems;

• generates data for educational evaluation.

No child-facing programme may be promoted using stronger language than its evidence supports.

**Maintenance During Holidays and Institutional Closures**

School calendars create predictable continuity risks.

Every deployment shall define responsibility during:

• weekends;

• school holidays;

• summer closure;

• staff absence;

• examination periods;

• severe weather;

• emergency closure;

• institutional transition.

The continuity plan shall address:

• watering;

• irrigation failure;

• harvesting;

• plant care;

• food safety;

• security;

• access;

• animal care where applicable;

• monitoring;

• incident response;

• produce allocation.

A programme that depends entirely on students during term time without a closure-period maintenance plan is not operationally admitted.

**Teacher, Staff, and Operator Workload**

Prometheus shall not presume that teachers, school staff, parents, or volunteers possess unlimited time or specialist ecological competence.

The operating model shall disclose:

• staff time;

• preparation time;

• training;

• maintenance workload;

• supervision workload;

• recordkeeping;

• safeguarding duties;

• holiday responsibilities;

• external-operator costs.

Unfunded workload transfer shall be treated as an operational risk.

A project shall not be presented as low-cost by excluding teacher time, volunteer time, maintenance, replacement planting, data administration, safety compliance, or safeguarding.

**Incident, Complaint, and Suspension Pathway**

The programme shall maintain procedures for:

• accidents;

• safeguarding concerns;

• food-safety events;

• allergic reactions;

• privacy breaches;

• unauthorised photography;

• bullying or harassment;

• discrimination;

• inappropriate political activity;

• unsafe tools;

• toxic exposure;

• contaminated produce;

• water hazards;

• volunteer misconduct;

• operator failure;

• maintenance failure.

Children and families shall have an age-appropriate and accessible complaint pathway.

Material incidents shall trigger:

• immediate protection measures;

• documentation;

• notification where required;

• investigation;

• risk-register update;

• Claims Register review;

• remediation;

• suspension where necessary.

A positive programme narrative shall never suppress incident reporting.

**School Food Forest Admission Gate**

A School Food Forest may be described as operational only when:

• the competent school and land authorities are identified;

• authorised use is documented;

• safeguarding leadership is assigned;

• the site risk assessment is complete;

• supervision and visitor rules are active;

• the curriculum or educational-purpose boundary is defined;

• consent and data rules are established;

• plant, tool, water, weather, and food-safety controls are operational;

• accessibility has been reviewed;

• maintenance during school closures is assigned;

• incidents and complaints can be escalated;

• Claims Register entries exist;

• unresolved limitations are disclosed.

It may be described as educationally effective only when the relevant outcomes have passed the applicable evidence and public-value gates.

It may be described as replication-ready only when legal, safeguarding, educational, ecological, operational, maintenance, data, financial, and evidence requirements have been independently reviewed.

The lowest unresolved School Food Forest or child-safeguarding gate controls the permitted claim.

**Child Dignity Invariant**

Prometheus may invite children to learn from living systems.

It may not use children to legitimise unverified systems.

It may support ecological education.

It may not convert education into surveillance.

It may measure programme outcomes.

It may not measure human worth.

It may document participation.

It may not convert participation into political, commercial, investor, token, or governance value.

It may use technology to support learning and safety.

It may not grant technology authority over the child.

The constitutional rule is:

the child is a participant in regeneration;

never an instrument of extraction.

# Town Square and Community Hub Operating System

The Town Square and Community Hub Operating System governs any Prometheus-compatible civic space, public square, community hub, market, nursery, tool library, food-distribution point, training centre, gathering place, repair space, cooperative node, learning commons, stewardship centre, or territorial coordination site intended to support regenerative community life.

A Prometheus-compatible Town Square may be physical, distributed, temporary, mobile, seasonal, digital-assisted, or composed of several connected locations.

Its purpose is to provide a locally legitimate and publicly accountable infrastructure through which people may meet, learn, exchange, steward, produce, repair, distribute, deliberate, collaborate, and maintain shared regenerative capacity.

A Town Square is not merely an event venue.

It is a civic operating environment.

It shall therefore be designed and governed through explicit rules concerning:

• public purpose;

• access;

• stewardship;

• scheduling;

• safety;

• data;

• equipment;

• food;

• commerce;

• politics;

• finance;

• maintenance;

• conflict;

• evidence;

• continuity.

**Town Square Constitutional Boundary**

A Town Square or Community Hub does not, by itself, create:

• political authority;

• electoral legitimacy;

• legal representation of the community;

• ownership of public space;

• exclusive economic rights;

• community-wide consent;

• governance authority over non-participants;

• rights over food, tools, data, land, buildings, brands, methods, PRU, RAP, tokens, revenue, or public resources;

• certification of community cohesion;

• proof of resilience;

• proof of economic development;

• proof of reduced crime, loneliness, exclusion, or institutional distrust.

The controlling rule is:

gathering does not equal governance;

participation does not equal consent;

attendance does not equal impact;

visibility does not equal legitimacy;

activity does not equal continuity;

community language does not equal community ownership;

public space does not equal unrestricted use;

shared use does not equal unassigned responsibility.

**Purpose of the Town Square System**

A Prometheus-compatible Town Square may support:

• local food exchange;

• community markets;

• food and plant distribution;

• seed and plant libraries;

• regional nursery functions;

• tool libraries;

• repair and reuse;

• compost and biomass coordination;

• workshops;

• apprenticeships;

• ecological education;

• community meetings;

• local cultural events;

• intergenerational mentorship;

• public information;

• operator training;

• emergency coordination;

• stewardship circles;

• local procurement;

• cooperative enterprise;

• community research;

• public-value reporting.

Each function shall be separately defined.

The presence of several functions in one location does not automatically create integration, efficiency, resilience, or public value.

**Town Square Readiness States**

Prometheus establishes the following Town Square and Community Hub readiness ladder.

**TSH-0 — Civic Concept**

A town square, market, hub, commons, nursery, community centre, or related function has been proposed.

No operational, governance, public-value, commercial, resilience, or community-impact claim is permitted.

**TSH-1 — Community Need and Host Identified**

The intended community, territorial need, host organisation, site, and preliminary public purpose have been identified.

Identification does not create authority, representativeness, or permission.

**TSH-2 — Legal and Institutional Pathway Identified**

The applicable land-use, occupancy, event, market, food, public-access, insurance, licensing, procurement, safeguarding, health, safety, and data pathways have been identified.

No public-facing operation may begin before the required permissions are effective.

**TSH-3 — Operating Dossier Complete**

The governance model, operating calendar, site plan, access rules, vendor rules, food-safety rules, tool-custody system, volunteer model, public communications boundary, conflict pathway, maintenance plan, budget, evidence plan, and Claims Register entries are complete.

No community-impact claim is permitted before baseline or defined initial conditions.

**TSH-4 — Authorised Pilot Operation**

The hub may operate within its approved scope, schedule, site boundary, capacity, public purpose, staffing model, and risk controls.

Claims are restricted to implementation, participation, and recorded outputs.

**TSH-5 — Operationally Stable**

The hub has completed a defined operating cycle with evidence of:

• scheduling continuity;

• responsible staffing;

• maintenance;

• safety;

• access;

• vendor or participant compliance;

• equipment control;

• food-safety performance;

• financial control;

• incident management;

• community review.

Operational stability is not equivalent to community transformation or economic impact.

**TSH-6 — Evidence-Supported Civic Infrastructure**

Repeated evidence supports defined public-value, productive, educational, social, administrative, or resilience outcomes within the admitted scope.

Only approved Claims Register wording may be used.

**TSH-7 — Replication-Ready Community Infrastructure**

The hub has an independently reviewed operating model, governance record, cost structure, maintenance history, risk register, evidence package, training pathway, legal model, failure record, adaptation rules, and continuity plan.

Replication readiness does not eliminate local legal, community, cultural, ecological, or operational review.

The TSH ladder is distinct from:

• CTD maturity;

• PL-OHE maturity;

• SFF maturity;

• PVM public-value maturity;

• PRU-R maturity;

• RAP-M maturity;

• scientific evidence maturity;

• runtime release status;

• legal-wrapper status;

• political support;

• funding status.

No ladder may substitute for another.

**Mandatory Town Square Operating Dossier**

Before public operation, the hub shall maintain a versioned dossier containing, where applicable:

1.  site identifier;

2.  legal custodian;

3.  authorised use;

4.  operating entity;

5.  public purpose;

6.  target community;

7.  access rules;

8.  operating calendar;

9.  capacity limits;

10. site layout;

11. accessibility plan;

12. safeguarding responsibilities;

13. food-market rules;

14. food-safety controls;

15. nursery and plant-distribution rules;

16. seed-library rules;

17. tool-library rules;

18. equipment-custody rules;

19. vendor or producer admission rules;

20. pricing and fee rules;

21. donation rules;

22. volunteer roles;

23. staff and operator roles;

24. community governance structure;

25. political-neutrality controls;

26. commercial-sponsorship controls;

27. data-governance plan;

28. public communications policy;

29. complaints and conflict pathway;

30. emergency procedures;

31. incident reporting;

32. insurance and liability review;

33. procurement pathway;

34. financial-control procedures;

35. maintenance plan;

36. waste and sanitation plan;

37. utilities and water plan;

38. public-value indicators;

39. Claims Register entries;

40. continuity, suspension, and closure plan;

41. conflict-of-interest disclosures;

42. review cadence.

The dossier shall distinguish:

• public functions;

• commercial functions;

• political functions;

• charitable functions;

• educational functions;

• governance functions;

• research functions;

• Prometheus functions.

No function shall inherit the authority, legal status, data rights, funding permissions, or public legitimacy of another merely because they occur in the same place.

**Governance and Representation**

A Town Square shall not claim to represent the whole community unless a legally and procedurally valid representative mandate exists.

Community governance may include:

• open assemblies;

• stewardship circles;

• role-based operations;

• user councils;

• vendor councils;

• youth councils;

• elder councils;

• resident forums;

• public-institution participation;

• sociocratic circles;

• cooperative governance.

Each governance body shall define:

• scope;

• membership;

• decision rights;

• consent or voting method;

• quorum;

• conflict rules;

• delegated authority;

• escalation;

• review;

• removal;

• public decision records.

The following matters require heightened review:

• control of public assets;

• exclusion of participants;

• pricing;

• vendor access;

• tool access;

• food distribution;

• data use;

• political activity;

• commercial sponsorship;

• allocation of grants or public funds;

• long-term site use;

• changes to public purpose.

Tokens, donations, political status, volunteer hours, founder status, technical custody, property ownership, public office, or financial contributions shall not automatically determine constitutional or public-interest authority.

**Access and Inclusion**

Access rules shall be transparent, proportionate, and linked to the legitimate purpose and capacity of the hub.

Access may be limited where necessary for:

• safeguarding;

• safety;

• capacity;

• food hygiene;

• protected activities;

• equipment control;

• operational continuity;

• lawful membership structures;

• site restrictions;

• private or clinical services.

Access to basic community benefits shall not be conditioned on:

• political affiliation;

• campaign activity;

• party membership;

• religious conformity;

• investor status;

• token ownership;

• data surrender beyond lawful necessity;

• donations;

• publicity participation;

• ideological agreement unrelated to the programme purpose.

Where fees are charged, the fee structure, exemptions, subsidies, and use of revenue shall be disclosed.

Prometheus-compatible access shall not become a hidden paywall to public or charitable benefits.

**Market and Vendor Governance**

Where the Town Square hosts markets, vendors, producers, cooperatives, artisans, food providers, nurseries, or service providers, the operating rules shall define:

• admission criteria;

• product eligibility;

• food-safety requirements;

• licensing;

• insurance;

• pricing transparency;

• stall allocation;

• conflicts of interest;

• local-producer preference where lawful;

• waste rules;

• packaging rules;

• prohibited products;

• political merchandise;

• sponsorship;

• dispute resolution;

• payment and settlement pathways;

• vendor data.

The hub shall distinguish:

• public-benefit distribution;

• commercial sale;

• cooperative exchange;

• barter;

• donation;

• emergency provision;

• educational demonstration.

A community market does not automatically prove local economic development, affordability, food security, or fair access.

Market claims require evidence on:

• vendors participating;

• sales;

• repeat participation;

• local sourcing;

• affordability;

• consumer reach;

• producer retention;

• seasonal continuity;

• economic leakage;

• operating costs.

**Food Distribution and Community Benefit**

Where food is donated, distributed, sold, exchanged, or prepared, the hub shall define:

• eligible recipients where applicable;

• distribution method;

• food-safety controls;

• storage;

• traceability;

• perishability;

• handling;

• allergens;

• waste;

• recalls;

• emergency procedures;

• data collected from beneficiaries;

• dignity and non-discrimination standards.

Food access shall not be conditioned on political support, campaign activity, religious participation, publicity, research participation, or unnecessary data disclosure.

The hub shall distinguish:

• food received;

• food stored;

• food distributed;

• food collected;

• food consumed;

• food wasted;

• unmet demand.

Distribution volume does not alone establish food security, nutritional improvement, or social impact.

**Nursery, Seed Library, and Plant Distribution**

Where plants, seeds, cuttings, slips, trees, or nursery materials are distributed, the system shall define:

• source;

• phytosanitary status;

• species identity;

• invasive-species controls;

• recipient guidance;

• distribution records;

• propagation rights;

• licensing or protected-variety constraints;

• survival monitoring where applicable;

• political and commercial neutrality;

• donor recognition boundaries;

• no-guarantee language.

Plant distribution does not establish successful cultivation.

Plant survival, productive establishment, ecological suitability, recipient capacity, water availability, and follow-up support are separate outcomes.

A donated plant shall not create political obligation, commercial endorsement, data entitlement, governance authority, PRU rights, RAP rights, future revenue rights, or ownership of the recipient’s land or produce.

**Tool Library and Shared Equipment**

Where tools or equipment are shared, the system shall define:

• asset ownership;

• custody;

• eligibility;

• booking;

• deposits;

• fees;

• user training;

• age restrictions;

• protective equipment;

• inspection;

• maintenance;

• cleaning;

• loss;

• damage;

• insurance;

• prohibited use;

• incident reporting;

• replacement;

• retirement.

Equipment access does not create ownership.

Volunteer repair does not create title unless formally agreed.

A tool library shall not be described as economically efficient without accounting for:

• staff time;

• maintenance;

• replacement;

• storage;

• insurance;

• training;

• loss;

• utilisation rates.

**Education, Training, and Mentorship**

The hub may host:

• ecological training;

• food-system education;

• repair skills;

• governance training;

• youth programmes;

• elder mentorship;

• vocational pathways;

• operator apprenticeships;

• digital-literacy programmes;

• public-policy education.

Training claims shall distinguish:

• attendance;

• completion;

• demonstrated competence;

• certification;

• employment outcomes;

• stewardship outcomes;

• long-term application.

A workshop delivered is an output.

It is not proof of competence, employment, behaviour change, or community resilience.

Where minors participate, the School Food Forest and Child Safeguarding Protocol applies.

**Political and Civic Activity Boundary**

A Town Square may host lawful civic dialogue, candidate forums, public-policy discussion, voter education, public meetings, or community deliberation where permitted.

Such activity shall remain explicitly separated from:

• food access;

• tool access;

• school participation;

• plant distribution;

• public benefits;

• Prometheus scoring;

• MRV records;

• public-value admission;

• governance eligibility;

• community-service eligibility.

No participant shall be required to support a party, candidate, policy position, campaign, referendum, religion, donor, funder, or sponsor in order to access unrelated benefits.

Where partisan or electoral activity is allowed:

• the responsible entity shall be identified;

• applicable campaign, venue, finance, disclaimer, reporting, and equal-access rules shall be observed;

• campaign data shall remain separated from operational data;

• costs and in-kind contributions shall be recorded;

• Prometheus shall not certify political claims;

• no political actor shall gain control over the hub’s ecological, community, or evidentiary records.

**Public Communications and Community Narrative**

The hub may communicate:

• activities;

• schedules;

• opportunities;

• approved outputs;

• evidence-supported outcomes;

• maintenance needs;

• risks;

• incidents;

• public-value status;

• open decisions.

It shall not present:

• attendance as cohesion;

• testimonials as causal proof;

• political support as legitimacy;

• donations as impact;

• market activity as economic transformation;

• events as institutional continuity;

• digital engagement as community participation;

• branding as community ownership.

Community stories may supplement evidence.

They shall not substitute for it.

**Data Governance**

The hub shall define distinct treatment for:

• participant data;

• beneficiary data;

• vendor data;

• volunteer data;

• donor data;

• staff data;

• safeguarding data;

• ecological data;

• financial data;

• political data;

• research data;

• imagery;

• geolocation.

Operational data shall not be combined by default with:

• voter files;

• donor-profiling systems;

• political targeting;

• commercial surveillance;

• biometric scoring;

• investor marketing;

• unrelated advertising.

No civic participation history, food-access record, tool-borrowing record, training history, volunteer activity, or attendance pattern may be used to infer political affiliation, financial reliability, moral worth, governance eligibility, or social status.

**Finance, Treasury, and Revenue Control**

The Town Square shall maintain transparent financial controls appropriate to its scale and legal form.

The system shall identify:

• revenue sources;

• grants;

• donations;

• public funds;

• fees;

• vendor charges;

• sponsorship;

• in-kind contributions;

• restricted funds;

• expenditure authority;

• bank or treasury custody;

• reconciliation;

• audit or review requirements;

• conflicts of interest;

• related-party transactions.

No donor, political sponsor, investor, vendor, operator, founder, or platform provider shall receive undisclosed control over:

• programming;

• vendor access;

• participant data;

• community governance;

• ecological claims;

• public-value claims;

• public assets;

• food distribution;

• tool allocation;

• PRU or RAP status.

Financial sustainability shall not be claimed without including maintenance, staffing, utilities, insurance, administration, replacement, compliance, and closure costs.

**Safety, Sanitation, and Incident Governance**

The hub shall maintain controls appropriate to its activities, including:

• crowd safety;

• fire;

• electrical systems;

• temporary structures;

• weather;

• food preparation;

• water;

• sanitation;

• waste;

• tools;

• machinery;

• traffic;

• children;

• vulnerable adults;

• harassment;

• theft;

• conflict;

• data breach;

• medical emergencies.

Material incidents shall be documented and reviewed.

A positive civic narrative shall not suppress:

• injuries;

• food-safety failures;

• financial irregularities;

• harassment;

• exclusion;

• data misuse;

• vendor complaints;

• safeguarding concerns;

• maintenance failures;

• political misuse.

**Conflict Resolution and Community Harm**

The hub shall maintain an accessible process for:

• complaints;

• interpersonal conflict;

• discrimination;

• harassment;

• vendor disputes;

• governance disputes;

• resource allocation disputes;

• political disputes;

• data concerns;

• safety concerns;

• stewardship failures.

The process shall distinguish:

• informal resolution;

• facilitated dialogue;

• operational decision;

• safeguarding escalation;

• legal escalation;

• governance review;

• suspension;

• removal.

Community harmony shall not be presumed.

Conflict records, where lawful and proportionate, are part of institutional learning.

Restorative approaches may be used but shall not replace legal, safeguarding, employment, criminal, or public-authority obligations.

**Maintenance and Continuity**

The hub shall define responsibility for:

• opening and closing;

• cleaning;

• utilities;

• repairs;

• equipment;

• signage;

• waste;

• storage;

• access control;

• emergency systems;

• digital systems;

• food systems;

• records;

• volunteer coordination;

• staffing;

• absence cover;

• insurance;

• seasonal closure;

• succession.

A hub dependent entirely on one founder, one volunteer, one donor, one political administration, one grant, one vendor, or one technical platform shall disclose the concentration risk.

Continuity planning shall include:

• role succession;

• data custody;

• financial handover;

• key and credential custody;

• asset inventory;

• emergency closure;

• temporary suspension;

• operator replacement;

• community communication.

**Town Square Public-Value Boundary**

The Town Square may generate public value, but such value shall be classified through the Public Value and Civic Claims Architecture.

Potential claims include:

• improved food access;

• increased local trade;

• increased participation;

• reduced social isolation;

• increased trust;

• improved skills;

• strengthened emergency capacity;

• improved institutional coordination;

• reduced waste;

• increased reuse;

• improved access to tools.

Each claim requires a defined method.

Participation, attendance, volume, or visibility do not automatically establish these outcomes.

The default financial rule remains:

town_square_public_value_weight_in_PRU = 0.

Any financial translation requires a separate evidence-supported, legally recognised, governance-approved pathway.

**Suspension, Closure, and Asset Transition**

The hub shall define what occurs when:

• the site becomes unavailable;

• funding ends;

• the operator fails;

• public authority is withdrawn;

• safety deteriorates;

• governance becomes captured;

• financial irregularity occurs;

• community support materially declines;

• political misuse occurs;

• maintenance becomes unsustainable;

• legal status changes.

The closure or transition plan shall address:

• assets;

• tools;

• food;

• plants;

• seeds;

• data;

• records;

• restricted funds;

• debts;

• vendor deposits;

• staff;

• volunteers;

• beneficiaries;

• keys;

• credentials;

• branding;

• ongoing commitments;

• public communications.

Closure shall not erase the evidence, incident, or financial record.

**Town Square Anti-Capture Invariant**

A Prometheus-compatible Town Square exists to serve its admitted civic, ecological, educational, productive, cultural, and public purpose.

It shall not become an undisclosed mechanism for:

• political capture;

• commercial monopoly;

• donor control;

• investor control;

• founder control;

• religious coercion;

• community surveillance;

• voter recruitment;

• data extraction;

• exclusionary gatekeeping;

• public-asset privatisation;

• token-based authority;

• reputational laundering;

• unverified impact claims.

The controlling principle is:

community infrastructure shall remain accountable to its lawful and admitted purpose;

participation shall remain dignified;

data shall remain bounded;

politics shall remain separated from benefits;

commerce shall remain transparent;

governance shall remain reviewable;

no actor may convert civic stewardship into unreviewed sovereignty.

**Town Square Admission Gate**

A Town Square or Community Hub may be described as operational only when:

• the responsible entity and site authority are identified;

• authorised use is documented;

• governance and decision rights are defined;

• public-access rules are active;

• safety, safeguarding, food, sanitation, and equipment controls are operational;

• finance and treasury controls are established;

• data governance is active;

• political and commercial boundaries are documented;

• maintenance and continuity responsibilities are assigned;

• complaints and incidents can be escalated;

• Claims Register entries exist;

• unresolved limitations are disclosed.

It may be described as public-value-generating only when the relevant outcomes have passed the applicable PVM gates.

It may be described as replication-ready only when its legal, operational, governance, financial, data, maintenance, public-value, and failure records have been independently reviewed.

The lowest unresolved Town Square gate controls the permitted claim.

**Civic Infrastructure Invariant**

Prometheus may help communities gather.

It may not presume that gathering creates trust.

It may support markets.

It may not presume that exchange creates fairness.

It may support public dialogue.

It may not convert dialogue into political authority.

It may support shared tools and resources.

It may not leave responsibility unassigned.

It may document participation.

It may not convert participation into social, financial, political, or governance worth.

The constitutional rule is:

a Town Square becomes regenerative infrastructure only when access, stewardship, maintenance, safety, evidence, accountability, and continuity converge.

# Legal Commons and Stewardship Custody Stack

The Legal Commons and Stewardship Custody Stack defines the legal-structuring boundary through which land, buildings, ecological infrastructure, community assets, public-benefit resources, equipment, data, operational rights, revenues, obligations, and long-term stewardship may be held, used, governed, protected, transferred, or terminated.

Prometheus does not prescribe one universal legal form.

The appropriate structure depends on:

• jurisdiction;

• land status;

• public or private ownership;

• community purpose;

• tax treatment;

• financing;

• liability;

• duration;

• public-access obligations;

• beneficiary class;

• governance model;

• succession requirements;

• regulatory status;

• indigenous, customary, or traditional rights;

• intended economic activities.

Potential legal structures may include, where legally available and appropriate:

• Community Land Trusts;

• charitable trusts;

• public-benefit corporations;

• cooperatives;

• mutual-benefit entities;

• foundations;

• nonprofit entities;

• public authorities;

• municipal corporations;

• land banks;

• stewardship associations;

• conservation easements;

• public concessions;

• licences;

• leases;

• usufruct-like arrangements;

• management agreements;

• public-private agreements;

• community-benefit agreements;

• restrictive covenants;

• asset locks;

• special-purpose entities;

• regulated investment or financing vehicles;

• contractual stewardship arrangements.

Listing a structure does not constitute legal approval, suitability, enforceability, tax efficiency, regulatory admission, or recommendation.

Every implementation requires jurisdiction-specific professional review.

**Legal Meaning Boundary**

Prometheus distinguishes among:

• canonical meaning;

• ecological function;

• operational responsibility;

• governance participation;

• contractual authority;

• ownership;

• possession;

• beneficial use;

• economic entitlement;

• public authority;

• fiduciary responsibility;

• financial exposure;

• enforceable legal rights.

These categories shall not be collapsed.

An OHE is an ecological and operational definition.

A PRU is an analytical interface.

A RAP is a portfolio-analysis structure.

A governance circle is a decision body within its admitted scope.

A token may provide defined utility where legally admitted.

A dashboard records or displays information.

None of these creates title, tenancy, security interest, public authority, revenue entitlement, beneficial ownership, fiduciary status, voting rights in a legal entity, or investor protection unless an applicable legal instrument explicitly creates that right.

The controlling rule is:

canon defines meaning;

ecology defines living function;

governance defines admitted decision process;

contracts define obligations;

law defines enforceable rights;

no digital or narrative representation substitutes for law.

**Rights and Responsibilities Map**

Every material Prometheus-compatible deployment shall maintain a versioned Rights and Responsibilities Map.

The map shall identify, where applicable:

1.  legal owner of land;

2.  legal owner of buildings;

3.  owner of permanent improvements;

4.  owner of removable equipment;

5.  holder of possession;

6.  holder of access rights;

7.  holder of public-use authority;

8.  holder of operational authority;

9.  maintenance responsible party;

10. ecological stewardship responsible party;

11. water-right holder;

12. harvesting-right holder;

13. produce owner;

14. nursery-stock owner;

15. revenue recipient;

16. revenue-sharing obligations;

17. public-benefit obligations;

18. data controller;

19. data processor;

20. evidence custodian;

21. intellectual-property holder;

22. brand and communications rights;

23. insurance responsible party;

24. tax responsible party;

25. regulatory responsible party;

26. employer or contractor relationships;

27. lender rights;

28. security interests;

29. investor rights where applicable;

30. public-authority powers;

31. beneficiary class;

32. governance rights;

33. veto or reserved powers;

34. dispute-resolution pathway;

35. termination rights;

36. renewal rights;

37. transfer restrictions;

38. insolvency consequences;

39. reversion rights;

40. decommissioning obligations.

The map shall distinguish:

• existing rights;

• proposed rights;

• conditional rights;

• disputed rights;

• unverified assumptions;

• prohibited interpretations.

No material may describe rights as existing where they are only proposed, expected, politically supported, technologically represented, or subject to future legal review.

**Legal Commons Readiness States**

Prometheus establishes the following Legal Commons and Stewardship Custody readiness ladder.

**LCS-0 — Stewardship Intention**

A community, institution, landowner, municipality, operator, donor, or project intends to preserve regenerative or public-benefit use.

No enforceable long-term custody, access, transfer, or anti-capture claim is permitted.

**LCS-1 — Parties and Asset Boundary Identified**

The relevant parties, assets, land parcels, infrastructure, uses, beneficiaries, and preliminary responsibilities have been identified.

Identification does not establish legal rights.

**LCS-2 — Legal Options Analysed**

Potential legal forms, contractual pathways, public-law mechanisms, tax implications, regulatory constraints, liability issues, and transfer restrictions have been analysed.

No structure may be represented as adopted or enforceable.

**LCS-3 — Rights Map and Draft Structure Complete**

The Rights and Responsibilities Map, governance relationship, asset schedule, use restrictions, revenue rules, maintenance duties, data rights, transfer controls, default rules, termination provisions, and draft legal instruments are complete.

Drafting does not create rights.

**LCS-4 — Legally Executed Structure**

The applicable legal instruments have been validly executed, approved, registered, authorised, or made effective as required.

Claims are limited to the actual rights and obligations contained in those instruments.

**LCS-5 — Operationally Effective Custody**

The structure is operating in practice with active governance, accounting, insurance, maintenance, compliance, recordkeeping, and enforcement capacity.

Legal existence alone does not establish operational effectiveness.

**LCS-6 — Independently Reviewed and Stress-Tested**

The structure has undergone applicable independent legal, tax, governance, insolvency, change-of-control, liability, public-benefit, and conflict-of-interest review.

Material weaknesses, residual risks, and jurisdictional limitations are disclosed.

**LCS-7 — Durable Stewardship Custody**

The structure demonstrates effective continuity across a defined period, including leadership succession, funding change, dispute, operator transition, political change, or other material stress.

Durable custody does not imply permanence beyond law, universal enforceability, financial safety, or immunity from public authority.

The LCS ladder is distinct from:

• CTD maturity;

• PL-OHE readiness;

• SFF readiness;

• TSH readiness;

• PVM public-value maturity;

• PRU-R maturity;

• RAP-M maturity;

• funding status;

• title status;

• market maturity;

• runtime maturity.

No ladder may substitute for another.

**Legal Commons Stack Layers**

The Legal Commons and Stewardship Custody Stack shall distinguish at least the following layers.

**Layer 1 — Title and Asset Ownership**

This layer identifies legal ownership of:

• land;

• buildings;

• permanent improvements;

• equipment;

• stored materials;

• nursery stock;

• harvested products;

• intellectual property;

• digital infrastructure;

• financial assets.

Ownership shall be evidenced through applicable title, registry, contract, invoice, assignment, statute, public record, or other legally recognised documentation.

Ecological contribution does not create title.

Volunteer labour does not create title unless agreed.

Funding does not automatically create title.

Planting trees does not automatically create ownership of trees, land, produce, credits, data, or future value.

**Layer 2 — Use, Access, and Possession**

This layer defines who may:

• enter;

• occupy;

• cultivate;

• maintain;

• harvest;

• operate;

• host events;

• exclude others;

• use utilities;

• store equipment;

• collect data;

• provide services.

Use rights may arise through:

• licence;

• lease;

• concession;

• management agreement;

• easement;

• public access right;

• usufruct-like right;

• statutory authority;

• community agreement recognised by law.

Access shall not be inferred from participation, proximity, historical use, public ownership, donation, or digital membership.

**Layer 3 — Stewardship and Maintenance Obligations**

This layer defines affirmative duties concerning:

• ecological management;

• soil protection;

• water management;

• biodiversity;

• public safety;

• repairs;

• maintenance;

• replacement planting;

• reporting;

• insurance;

• regulatory compliance;

• recordkeeping;

• incident response;

• decommissioning.

A stewardship right without enforceable maintenance responsibility may create unmanaged risk.

A maintenance duty without resources or authority may be structurally impossible.

The legal instrument shall align duty, authority, resources, expertise, and accountability.

**Layer 4 — Governance and Reserved Powers**

This layer defines the relationship between:

• legal entity governance;

• Prometheus governance;

• community participation;

• public authority;

• landowner rights;

• operator autonomy;

• funder controls;

• lender protections;

• beneficiary rights.

Reserved powers may be necessary for:

• constitutional purpose;

• asset disposal;

• change of use;

• debt incurrence;

• amendment of stewardship restrictions;

• transfer of control;

• dissolution;

• major ecological disturbance;

• related-party transactions;

• PRU or RAP use;

• token or instrument issuance;

• data transfer.

No reserved power shall be described as community governance unless the community actually holds and can exercise it under the applicable legal framework.

**Layer 5 — Benefit and Economic Rights**

This layer defines:

• produce ownership;

• food-distribution priority;

• service revenue;

• nursery revenue;

• lease income;

• event income;

• grant restrictions;

• public subsidy;

• contractual payments;

• surplus allocation;

• reinvestment;

• dividends or patronage where lawful;

• investor returns where legally admitted.

Public or ecological benefit does not automatically create a private economic entitlement.

A contributor shall not receive undisclosed rights to future value merely because that contributor provided:

• capital;

• plants;

• data;

• technology;

• labour;

• design;

• political support;

• publicity;

• land access;

• professional services.

All material economic rights shall be explicit, legally valid, disclosed, and linked to a defined source.

**Layer 6 — Data, Evidence, and Knowledge Custody**

This layer defines rights and responsibilities concerning:

• raw ecological data;

• operational records;

• laboratory results;

• imagery;

• geolocation;

• public-value evidence;

• Claims Register records;

• software;

• schemas;

• methods;

• educational materials;

• community knowledge;

• traditional knowledge;

• personal data.

The legal structure shall distinguish:

• custody;

• access;

• control;

• authorship;

• licence;

• confidentiality;

• publication;

• portability;

• retention;

• deletion;

• transfer;

• public-interest access.

Data custody does not create ownership of ecological reality.

Technical hosting does not create methodological authority.

Funding data collection does not create unrestricted rights to exploit the data.

Community or traditional knowledge shall not be appropriated through broad contractual, platform, investment, or intellectual-property clauses.

**Layer 7 — Continuity, Reversion, and Asset Lock**

This layer protects the admitted purpose across:

• transfer;

• change of control;

• insolvency;

• dissolution;

• death;

• incapacity;

• political transition;

• operator failure;

• funder withdrawal;

• merger;

• sale;

• foreclosure;

• abandonment.

Potential controls may include:

• asset locks;

• transfer restrictions;

• rights of first refusal;

• public-benefit covenants;

• conservation easements;

• reversion clauses;

• successor qualification;

• stewardship succession;

• dissolution clauses;

• restricted-asset transfer;

• beneficiary-enforcement rights;

• independent protector or custodian roles.

The availability and enforceability of each control depend on law.

No contractual clause shall be represented as irreversible, perpetual, bankruptcy-proof, foreclosure-proof, or immune from public authority without a valid legal opinion.

**Community Land Trust Boundary**

A Community Land Trust may provide a useful structure for separating long-term land custody from operational or residential use.

However, the term Community Land Trust shall not be used merely as branding.

A Prometheus-compatible Community Land Trust pathway shall identify:

• legal entity;

• land title;

• geographic or beneficiary community;

• board composition;

• resident or user representation;

• public representation where applicable;

• stewardship obligations;

• transfer restrictions;

• ground-lease or use arrangements;

• affordability or public-benefit mechanism;

• asset-lock provisions;

• conflict-of-interest rules;

• dissolution provisions;

• enforcement capacity;

• funding model.

A trust or nonprofit controlled solely by founders, investors, vendors, donors, political actors, or landowners shall not be described as community-controlled without substantiation.

Community representation shall be real, legally recognised, procedurally accessible, and protected against capture.

**Cooperative Boundary**

A cooperative may support shared ownership, employment, production, services, purchasing, marketing, or stewardship.

The legal instrument shall distinguish:

• member class;

• membership eligibility;

• voting rights;

• capital contributions;

• patronage;

• surplus distribution;

• reserves;

• withdrawal;

• transfer;

• employment rights;

• asset ownership;

• land rights;

• dissolution.

A cooperative structure does not automatically create ecological integrity, fairness, community benefit, or anti-capture protection.

One-member-one-vote does not resolve all concentration risks where financial dependency, employment control, information asymmetry, or founder custody remain unaddressed.

**Public Concession and Licence Boundary**

Where a public authority grants use of land or infrastructure, the instrument shall define:

• competent authority;

• legal basis;

• term;

• permitted use;

• public purpose;

• access conditions;

• fees;

• maintenance;

• improvements;

• produce or revenue;

• data;

• procurement;

• insurance;

• liability;

• assignment;

• renewal;

• termination;

• reversion;

• public oversight.

A concession, memorandum, letter of support, council resolution, or political commitment shall not be described as permanent ownership or irrevocable stewardship.

The legal effect depends on the instrument and applicable public law.

**Conservation and Stewardship Covenant Boundary**

Conservation easements, restrictive covenants, stewardship covenants, or similar mechanisms may protect ecological or public-benefit use.

The instrument shall identify:

• protected values;

• prohibited uses;

• permitted uses;

• monitoring party;

• enforcement party;

• amendment process;

• baseline documentation;

• remedy;

• duration;

• transfer effect;

• public access, if any;

• interaction with debt and foreclosure.

A covenant without monitoring and enforcement capacity may provide limited protection.

A covenant shall not be treated as a substitute for operating competence, funding, maintenance, MRV, or community legitimacy.

**Asset Lock and Anti-Privatisation Controls**

Where public, charitable, donated, or community resources contribute materially to an asset, the legal structure should evaluate mechanisms preventing undisclosed private appropriation.

Potential controls include:

• restricted disposal;

• public-benefit use requirements;

• independent valuation;

• related-party approval;

• open tender where required;

• reinvestment obligations;

• beneficiary notice;

• supermajority approval;

• public-authority consent;

• community consent within its lawful scope;

• transfer to an equivalent-purpose entity upon dissolution.

An asset lock shall not be described as absolute.

The legal opinion shall examine:

• statutory basis;

• creditor priority;

• insolvency;

• foreclosure;

• taxation;

• eminent domain;

• regulatory intervention;

• enforcement standing;

• amendment risk.

**Capital and Security Interest Boundary**

Capital providers may require:

• repayment rights;

• security interests;

• covenants;

• step-in rights;

• information rights;

• control rights;

• foreclosure rights;

• revenue priority.

These rights can conflict with stewardship and commons objectives.

Before accepting capital, the structure shall disclose:

• assets encumbered;

• remedies available to the capital provider;

• circumstances of enforcement;

• impact on public access;

• impact on ecological use;

• impact on community governance;

• impact on asset lock;

• impact on continuity;

• refinancing risk;

• cross-default risk.

No asset may be described as permanently protected where a lender or creditor may lawfully seize, sell, control, or alter it under unresolved conditions.

Capital shall not receive disguised constitutional authority through debt covenants, technical custody, treasury control, or default remedies.

**Change-of-Control and Beneficial Ownership**

The structure shall identify:

• legal controllers;

• beneficial owners;

• voting controllers;

• appointing authorities;

• key-person dependencies;

• treasury controllers;

• data controllers;

• technical administrators;

• veto holders;

• successor rights.

Changes in control shall trigger review where they may affect:

• public purpose;

• community representation;

• ecological stewardship;

• data;

• operator independence;

• conflicts;

• PRU or RAP use;

• token or financial representation;

• land use;

• asset disposal.

A nominally nonprofit, cooperative, public-benefit, or community entity may still be captured through appointment rights, contracts, debt, employment, platform dependency, or information asymmetry.

Substantive control shall be assessed, not merely legal labels.

**Indigenous, Customary, and Traditional Rights**

Prometheus shall not presume that formal title exhausts all legitimate territorial rights.

Where relevant, legal and governance review shall consider:

• indigenous rights;

• customary tenure;

• traditional use;

• sacred or cultural sites;

• community rights;

• ancestral knowledge;

• treaty rights;

• access and harvesting practices;

• free, prior, and informed consent where applicable.

A state title, private deed, concession, investment agreement, data licence, or technological registry shall not be used to erase or appropriate rights requiring separate recognition.

Traditional ecological knowledge shall not be extracted, tokenised, commercialised, published, or incorporated into financial claims without legitimate authority, consent, benefit-sharing, and legal review.

**Conflict of Interest and Related-Party Transactions**

The legal structure shall maintain a conflicts register covering:

• founders;

• directors;

• officers;

• trustees;

• public officials;

• landowners;

• operators;

• vendors;

• consultants;

• investors;

• donors;

• political actors;

• relatives and associated entities.

Material related-party transactions shall disclose:

• relationship;

• terms;

• pricing basis;

• decision process;

• abstentions;

• independent review;

• benefit to the deployment;

• alternatives considered.

Community, nonprofit, cooperative, or ecological language shall not shield self-dealing from scrutiny.

**Default, Breach, and Enforcement**

Legal structures shall define what occurs when a party:

• fails to maintain;

• misuses funds;

• breaches public-purpose obligations;

• falsifies evidence;

• misuses data;

• changes land use;

• obstructs access;

• creates ecological harm;

• violates safeguarding;

• fails to insure;

• becomes insolvent;

• transfers control improperly;

• uses the project for unauthorised political, commercial, or financial purposes.

Potential remedies may include:

• notice;

• cure period;

• corrective action;

• suspension;

• removal of operator;

• loss of access;

• repayment;

• damages;

• injunction;

• transfer of custody;

• termination;

• reversion;

• regulatory referral;

• Claims Register downgrade.

A right without a remedy may be operationally weak.

A remedy without an authorised enforcing party may be ineffective.

**Dispute Resolution**

The legal stack shall define an appropriate dispute pathway.

Potential stages may include:

• operational review;

• facilitated dialogue;

• mediation;

• expert determination;

• governance review;

• arbitration where lawful and appropriate;

• judicial remedy;

• public-authority review.

Dispute processes shall not prevent urgent safeguarding, environmental, regulatory, employment, criminal, or public-law action.

Community mediation shall not be used to conceal coercion, harassment, corruption, ecological damage, public-resource misuse, or legal violations.

**Insolvency and Failure Boundary**

Every material structure shall analyse what occurs if:

• the operating entity fails;

• the landholding entity fails;

• a lender enforces;

• grants are reclaimed;

• insurance lapses;

• directors resign;

• a key operator disappears;

• the municipality changes policy;

• revenue collapses;

• litigation occurs.

The analysis shall identify:

• asset priority;

• creditor rights;

• land custody;

• equipment ownership;

• data custody;

• unfinished obligations;

• produce and inventory;

• public access;

• environmental liabilities;

• staff and contractor claims;

• continuity options.

No stewardship structure shall be described as durable without a documented failure pathway.

**Legal Commons Mandatory Dossier**

Before a deployment is described as legally structured, the dossier shall contain, where applicable:

1.  jurisdiction;

2.  asset schedule;

3.  land-title evidence;

4.  entity documents;

5.  beneficial-ownership record;

6.  Rights and Responsibilities Map;

7.  governance documents;

8.  legal-use instrument;

9.  stewardship obligations;

10. public-benefit obligations;

11. asset-lock provisions;

12. transfer restrictions;

13. reserved powers;

14. data and evidence provisions;

15. intellectual-property provisions;

16. produce and revenue rules;

17. financing documents;

18. security interests;

19. insurance;

20. tax analysis;

21. regulatory analysis;

22. safeguarding obligations;

23. environmental obligations;

24. conflicts register;

25. related-party controls;

26. succession provisions;

27. default and remedies;

28. dispute-resolution process;

29. termination;

30. reversion;

31. insolvency analysis;

32. decommissioning obligations;

33. legal opinion or review status;

34. Claims Register alignment;

35. unresolved legal risks.

A complete folder is not equivalent to a valid legal structure.

Documents must be effective, consistent, executed where required, and operationally implemented.

**Legal Opinion Gate**

No Prometheus-compatible deployment may be described as:

• legally protected;

• permanently protected;

• community-owned;

• community-controlled;

• asset-locked;

• bankruptcy-proof;

• foreclosure-proof;

• irrevocable;

• perpetual;

• legally decentralised;

• investor-protected;

• public-benefit secured;

• land-rights secured;

• tokenised ownership;

• enforceable commons;

unless the precise claim is supported by applicable law, executed instruments, factual control, and competent legal review.

The legal opinion shall identify:

• scope;

• jurisdiction;

• assumptions;

• documents reviewed;

• unresolved risks;

• enforceability limits;

• regulatory dependencies;

• required actions;

• expiry or review trigger.

Legal review does not validate ecological performance, public value, market demand, financial return, or governance quality.

**Legal Commons Anti-Capture Invariant**

The purpose of the Legal Commons and Stewardship Custody Stack is not to make commons language legally decorative.

Its purpose is to align:

• title;

• use;

• stewardship;

• benefit;

• governance;

• data;

• capital;

• succession;

• remedies.

No founder, landowner, investor, lender, donor, public official, operator, vendor, platform provider, trustee, director, political organisation, or technical custodian may obtain undisclosed or unreviewed control over a Prometheus-compatible commons through:

• title;

• debt;

• appointment rights;

• veto rights;

• data custody;

• treasury custody;

• infrastructure custody;

• intellectual property;

• employment dependency;

• vendor lock-in;

• political authority;

• emergency powers;

• token concentration;

• informational asymmetry.

The controlling principle is:

rights shall be explicit;

duties shall be enforceable;

control shall be disclosed;

benefit shall be bounded;

failure shall be anticipated;

stewardship shall survive only through valid institutions, not through narrative.

**Legal Commons Admission Gate**

A Prometheus-compatible commons or stewardship structure may be described as legally structured only when:

• the relevant assets and parties are identified;

• title and use rights are evidenced;

• governance and reserved powers are documented;

• stewardship and maintenance duties are enforceable;

• benefit and economic rights are explicit;

• data and evidence custody are defined;

• capital and creditor rights are disclosed;

• conflicts and related-party transactions are controlled;

• succession, default, insolvency, termination, and reversion are addressed;

• applicable instruments are validly executed;

• professional legal review is complete within the stated scope;

• unresolved limitations are disclosed;

• the Claims Register is aligned.

It may be described as durable stewardship custody only after operational effectiveness and continuity under material stress have been demonstrated.

The lowest unresolved legal, custody, governance, insolvency, or enforcement gate controls the permitted claim.

**Stewardship Custody Invariant**

Prometheus may inspire stewardship.

It cannot create title by inspiration.

It may coordinate community governance.

It cannot create legal authority by participation alone.

It may document ecological value.

It cannot create ownership of that value without law.

It may support commons.

It cannot protect commons without enforceable custody, duties, remedies, and succession.

The constitutional rule is:

stewardship becomes durable when purpose, rights, responsibility, control, continuity, and law converge.

**Civic Data and Electoral Compliance Protocol**

The Civic Data and Electoral Compliance Protocol governs every Prometheus-compatible deployment, partnership, event, system, database, application, communications pathway, public-benefit programme, political interaction, or community initiative in which civic activity may intersect with electoral, campaign, referendum, lobbying, public-office, party, voter, donor, or political-preference data.

Prometheus is not a voter database, campaign-management platform, electoral persuasion engine, political micro-targeting system, candidate-ranking system, party-membership system, public-opinion manipulation system, or source of political eligibility.

Prometheus may support lawful civic participation, public consultation, nonpartisan education, public-policy analysis, community deliberation, evidence-based policy design, and transparent public reporting.

Such functions remain subject to political neutrality, human dignity, purpose limitation, data minimisation, lawful authority, public-resource protection, and jurisdiction-specific legal review.

The controlling rule is:

civic participation ≠ electoral participation;

programme participation ≠ political support;

public-benefit eligibility ≠ voter eligibility;

ecological data ≠ voter data;

community stewardship ≠ campaign activity;

policy evidence ≠ candidate endorsement;

public communication ≠ electoral persuasion;

technical capability ≠ lawful authority.

**Scope of the Protocol**

This Protocol applies where a Prometheus-compatible actor interacts with:

• political parties;

• candidates;

• campaign committees;

• referendum committees;

• elected officials;

• public administrations;

• government departments;

• political action organisations;

• lobbying organisations;

• advocacy organisations;

• voter-registration initiatives;

• election-related events;

• candidate forums;

• public-policy campaigns;

• political fundraising;

• campaign volunteering;

• political advertising;

• issue advertising;

• political research;

• voter or donor databases;

• campaign analytics;

• election-related AI systems;

• gifts, plants, food, services, venues, transport, labour, equipment, data, or digital infrastructure provided in a political context.

The applicable jurisdiction determines whether an activity is electoral, political, charitable, educational, lobbying, public-administration, campaign-finance, or otherwise regulated.

Prometheus terminology does not determine legal classification.

**Data-Class Separation**

Every deployment shall classify data before collection, storage, sharing, analysis, or use.

The minimum classes are:

**Class E — Ecological and MRV Data**

This may include:

• soil;

• water;

• biodiversity;

• biomass;

• climate;

• species;

• land management;

• geospatial evidence;

• laboratory records;

• sensor records;

• imagery;

• operational ecological events.

Class E data shall not be used to infer political preferences, voter behaviour, ideological orientation, or electoral susceptibility.

**Class O — Operational and Stewardship Data**

This may include:

• maintenance;

• assignments;

• equipment;

• training;

• work records;

• volunteer scheduling;

• incident reporting;

• programme delivery;

• contribution records.

Class O data shall not become political-engagement or campaign-reliability data.

**Class C — Civic Participation Data**

This may include:

• attendance at public meetings;

• participation in stewardship circles;

• community consultations;

• policy workshops;

• nonpartisan educational events;

• public comments;

• programme feedback.

Civic participation shall not be interpreted as support for a candidate, party, ideology, referendum, or political programme.

**Class B — Beneficiary and Public-Service Data**

This may include:

• food distribution;

• plant distribution;

• tool access;

• educational access;

• public-benefit eligibility;

• assistance received;

• programme use.

Beneficiary records shall not be used for voter recruitment, political targeting, persuasion, fundraising, or party membership.

**Class P — Political and Electoral Data**

This may include:

• party affiliation;

• voting intention;

• voter-registration status;

• candidate support;

• campaign participation;

• political donations;

• referendum position;

• canvassing records;

• political communications preferences;

• political-event attendance where legally classified as such.

Class P data shall remain outside the Prometheus core evidence, MRV, PRU, RAP, governance, public-benefit, and stewardship architecture.

**Class S — Sensitive and Protected Personal Data**

This may include data concerning:

• children;

• health;

• disability;

• biometrics;

• ethnicity;

• religion;

• sexuality;

• political opinions;

• precise location;

• financial hardship;

• incarceration;

• immigration;

• safeguarding;

• family circumstances.

Class S data require heightened necessity, minimisation, security, retention, access, consent, legal, and ethical controls.

Political opinion data shall not be inferred from ecological, civic, educational, religious, cultural, geographical, consumption, donation, or participation data.

**Electoral Firewall**

Prometheus shall maintain a substantive firewall between regenerative infrastructure and electoral infrastructure.

The firewall shall separate, where applicable:

• legal entities;

• governance bodies;

• personnel roles;

• contracts;

• bank accounts;

• treasuries;

• budgets;

• fundraising;

• websites;

• repositories;

• applications;

• databases;

• mailing lists;

• authentication systems;

• analytics;

• cloud environments;

• devices;

• advertising accounts;

• volunteer management;

• communications;

• data controllers;

• service providers;

• decision records.

A different brand, folder, wallet, domain, account, dashboard, or legal name does not prove substantive separation.

Separation shall be demonstrated through access controls, purposes, contracts, financial records, role assignments, data architecture, technical isolation, governance decisions, and operational practice.

**Prohibited Data Combinations**

The following combinations are prohibited by default:

• ecological MRV data with voter records;

• beneficiary data with political-targeting data;

• food or plant distribution data with voter outreach;

• school or child data with political databases;

• volunteer stewardship records with campaign-volunteer records;

• landowner data with political persuasion profiles;

• tool-library use with campaign engagement;

• Town Square attendance with voter-susceptibility analysis;

• Claims Register data with candidate or party scoring;

• PRU or RAP records with voter, donor, or campaign profiles;

• token activity with political-preference inference;

• geolocation or imagery with electoral targeting;

• health, disability, religious, cultural, or economic vulnerability data with campaign analytics.

A person’s participation in a Prometheus-compatible programme shall not be used to infer that the person:

• supports regeneration as a political ideology;

• supports a particular party or candidate;

• is persuadable;

• is likely to vote;

• belongs to a particular political constituency;

• should receive targeted political messaging;

• should be excluded from a benefit or governance process.

**Purpose Limitation**

Every data-processing activity shall have a defined purpose.

Permitted purposes may include:

• ecological observation;

• maintenance;

• programme administration;

• public-benefit delivery;

• safeguarding;

• operational safety;

• research under an admitted protocol;

• public-value evaluation;

• nonpartisan civic consultation;

• legally required reporting.

Data collected for one purpose shall not be reused for electoral, campaign, lobbying, commercial, investor, donor, or surveillance purposes without an independently lawful basis, a separate review, appropriate notice, and the relevant Prometheus admission.

Broad or bundled consent shall not be used to erase purpose boundaries.

Consent to receive a plant is not consent to political communication.

Consent to attend a workshop is not consent to campaign contact.

Consent to ecological monitoring is not consent to voter profiling.

Consent to public-benefit services is not consent to fundraising.

**No Benefit-for-Political-Support Rule**

No person shall be offered, denied, prioritised, delayed, ranked, rewarded, or penalised in relation to:

• food;

• plants;

• seeds;

• tools;

• education;

• land access;

• public services;

• community programmes;

• financial assistance;

• training;

• employment;

• volunteer opportunities;

• ecological benefits;

• public infrastructure;

on the basis of:

• voting behaviour;

• promise to vote;

• abstention;

• party membership;

• candidate support;

• campaign volunteering;

• political donation;

• attendance at a political event;

• publication of a political testimonial;

• consent to political contact.
