A public or charitable benefit shall remain unconditional with respect to unrelated political behaviour.

Where a benefit is distributed during an electoral or political event, the separation between benefit and political activity shall be explicit, documented, and operationally enforceable.

**Civic Education and Electoral Activity Boundary**

Prometheus may support nonpartisan civic education concerning:

• institutions;

• public policy;

• ecological evidence;

• public budgets;

• land stewardship;

• community participation;

• lawful voting procedures;

• public consultation;

• policy alternatives.

Prometheus shall not describe political advocacy, electoral persuasion, candidate promotion, opposition activity, or campaign mobilisation as neutral civic education.

Where candidate forums or political debates occur, the responsible entity shall define:

• participation criteria;

• equal-access or fairness requirements where applicable;

• moderator independence;

• permitted communications;

• event costs;

• data collection;

• recording and publication;

• campaign-finance treatment;

• public-resource use;

• safeguarding;

• disclaimers.

Prometheus does not certify candidates, parties, manifestos, referenda, political promises, or campaign claims.

**Political Claim Boundary**

A political or policy claim shall remain distinct from a Prometheus-verified outcome.

The following do not establish programme effectiveness:

• inclusion in a political manifesto;

• endorsement by a candidate;

• passage of legislation;

• appropriation of public funds;

• adoption by a council;

• inclusion in an administrative programme;

• media coverage;

• public popularity;

• electoral success.

Political targets shall be classified as policy objectives or programme theories until the applicable outputs, outcomes, attribution, evidence, review, and Claims Register gates are passed.

A political actor may cite Prometheus evidence only within the scope, maturity, limitations, and permitted wording of the relevant claim.

No political communication may remove uncertainty, comparator limitations, pilot status, unresolved gates, or non-offering boundaries.

**Contributions, Donations, and In-Kind Support**

Where money, goods, services, plants, food, land, venues, labour, transport, equipment, software, data, advertising, professional services, or other benefits are provided in connection with political or electoral activity, the responsible entity shall determine their applicable legal and reporting treatment.

The compliance record shall identify:

• provider;

• recipient;

• item or service;

• fair or applicable value;

• date;

• purpose;

• restrictions;

• responsible entity;

• authorisation;

• reporting status;

• conflict of interest;

• whether public, charitable, corporate, individual, or campaign resources were involved.

Prometheus shall not assume that a contribution is legally irrelevant because it is:

• ecological;

• donated;

• volunteer-provided;

• low-cost;

• produced by a nursery;

• distributed for community benefit;

• delivered through a digital platform;

• not expressed in money.

**Public-Resource Protection**

Public land, public staff, schools, public equipment, public vehicles, public data, public communications systems, public funds, grants, charitable resources, and tax-advantaged assets shall not be used for unauthorised electoral or partisan purposes.

Every deployment involving public or charitable resources shall define:

• authorised purpose;

• eligible users;

• cost allocation;

• staff time;

• venue use;

• branding;

• data use;

• communications;

• political access;

• reimbursement where applicable;

• audit trail.

A public official’s participation does not convert a public-benefit event into a lawful campaign event.

A campaign’s presence does not transfer public-benefit data or resources to that campaign.

**Voter and Donor Data Prohibition**

Prometheus core systems shall not maintain voter files, voter scores, persuasion scores, turnout models, donor-capacity scores, party-membership profiles, or candidate-support rankings.

Where an independent political actor lawfully operates such systems, those systems shall remain external to Prometheus.

No Prometheus operator, node, repository, dashboard, Holochain-compatible record, AI model, application interface, data room, or identity system shall expose Prometheus participant data to political targeting.

The following shall not determine access to Prometheus:

• voter status;

• donation history;

• party status;

• political ideology;

• campaign support;

• political influence;

• elected office.

**AI and Political Profiling**

AI shall not be used within Prometheus to:

• infer political opinions;

• predict voting behaviour;

• identify persuadable individuals;

• generate voter scores;

• select beneficiaries for political influence;

• create personalised political pressure;

• exploit fear, vulnerability, health, financial hardship, religion, ethnicity, age, or community relationships;

• produce deepfake political endorsements;

• simulate participant support;

• manipulate public consultation;

• autonomously moderate political eligibility;

• certify political truth.

AI may assist with neutral administrative tasks, public-document organisation, evidence comparison, meeting transcription, translation, or lawful public-policy analysis only within a reviewable and purpose-limited scope.

AI outputs concerning political or electoral matters shall be labelled as assistive, non-authoritative, review-required, and incapable of creating legal or evidentiary status.

**Digital Identity Boundary**

A Prometheus identity, credential, wallet, role, membership, contribution record, reputation record, stewardship record, or governance status shall not become:

• voter identification;

• proof of political affiliation;

• political passport;

• voting qualification;

• public-election credential;

• campaign-access credential;

• eligibility for public benefits;

• compulsory civic identity.

Prometheus may support bounded governance identity within its own admitted processes.

It does not create public electoral identity or authority.

**Civic Consultation Integrity**

Where Prometheus supports public consultation, the system shall disclose:

• who commissioned it;

• who may participate;

• how participants were recruited;

• whether participation is representative;

• how duplicate participation is controlled;

• how minority or marginalised views are protected;

• how data are used;

• how decisions are made;

• what authority the consultation possesses;

• whether results are binding, advisory, exploratory, or informational.

Participation volume shall not be presented as representative democratic consent without a valid methodology and mandate.

A digital poll is not an election.

A community meeting is not universal consent.

A token vote is not a public referendum.

An AI summary is not the voice of the community.

**Electoral Compliance Readiness States**

Prometheus establishes the following Civic Data and Electoral Compliance readiness ladder.

**CDEC-0 — No Political Interaction**

No political, electoral, candidate, referendum, lobbying, or voter-data interaction is planned.

Normal civic and data governance still applies.

**CDEC-1 — Potential Interaction Identified**

A political actor, elected official, campaign, public-policy initiative, referendum, or electoral event may interact with the deployment.

No political data transfer, benefit linkage, campaign use, or electoral claim is permitted.

**CDEC-2 — Classification and Jurisdiction Identified**

The responsible entities, jurisdictions, activity type, campaign status, public-resource context, charitable restrictions, and preliminary compliance responsibilities are identified.

Identification does not constitute legal clearance.

**CDEC-3 — Firewall and Compliance Dossier Complete**

Entity, finance, data, personnel, communications, public-resource, donation, vendor, AI, retention, and incident boundaries are documented.

No regulated interaction may occur before the applicable authorisations are effective.

**CDEC-4 — Legally Reviewed Limited Interaction**

A defined interaction may proceed within the reviewed scope, purpose, period, entities, data, resources, communications, and reporting obligations.

No wider political or data use may be inferred.

**CDEC-5 — Operationally Verified Separation**

Access logs, financial records, data flows, personnel roles, public-resource records, communications, and incident reports demonstrate substantive separation.

Operational separation is not permanent legal clearance.

**CDEC-6 — Independently Audited Compliance**

The relevant political, electoral, privacy, public-resource, charitable, technical, and financial boundaries have undergone independent review.

Residual risks and limitations are disclosed.

**CDEC-7 — Mature Civic-Neutral Infrastructure**

The deployment has demonstrated sustained party independence, data separation, public-resource protection, compliance continuity, incident response, and resistance to political capture across a defined period or political transition.

The CDEC ladder is distinct from:

• political support;

• electoral success;

• CTD maturity;

• PVM maturity;

• legal-wrapper status;

• PRU-R maturity;

• RAP-M maturity;

• runtime maturity;

• data-security maturity.

No ladder may substitute for another.

**Mandatory Civic and Electoral Compliance Dossier**

Where political or electoral interaction exists, the dossier shall contain, where applicable:

1.  responsible legal entities;

2.  activity classification;

3.  jurisdictions;

4.  political or electoral actors;

5.  public or charitable entities involved;

6.  purpose statement;

7.  data-classification map;

8.  data-flow diagram;

9.  lawful-basis or authority analysis;

10. firewall architecture;

11. personnel-role separation;

12. database separation;

13. access-control matrix;

14. finance and treasury separation;

15. cost-allocation methodology;

16. public-resource inventory;

17. contribution and in-kind record;

18. vendor and contractor review;

19. communications and disclaimer rules;

20. beneficiary non-conditioning controls;

21. voter and donor-data prohibition;

22. AI-use boundary;

23. political-event protocol;

24. retention and deletion schedule;

25. data-subject request pathway;

26. consent and preference records where applicable;

27. incident-response plan;

28. complaint and escalation pathway;

29. legal review;

30. governance decision;

31. Claims Register entries;

32. audit and review cadence;

33. unresolved risks.

**Retention and Deletion**

Political, civic, beneficiary, volunteer, event, communications, and participant data shall not be retained indefinitely by default.

The retention schedule shall identify:

• purpose;

• data category;

• responsible entity;

• retention period;

• legal hold;

• archive status;

• deletion or anonymisation method;

• portability or access rights where applicable.

Records necessary for legal compliance, audit, safeguarding, financial reporting, or institutional accountability may require preservation.

Preservation shall not authorise unrelated political reuse.

**Breach, Misuse, and Incident Response**

Material incidents may include:

• unauthorised transfer to a campaign;

• political profiling;

• benefit-for-support conduct;

• improper voter contact;

• public-resource misuse;

• undisclosed in-kind contribution;

• campaign use of school or child data;

• combining ecological and voter data;

• political micro-targeting;

• AI-generated false endorsement;

• unauthorised political messaging;

• misleading claims of Prometheus endorsement;

• unlawful access;

• security breach;

• retention beyond purpose.

The incident response shall include, where applicable:

• immediate containment;

• access suspension;

• preservation of evidence;

• data-flow review;

• affected-party protection;

• legal and regulatory notification;

• public correction;

• deletion or quarantine;

• Claims Register update;

• governance review;

• partner suspension;

• canonical compatibility downgrade;

• remedial controls;

• independent investigation.

A political or reputational interest shall not delay containment or disclosure where disclosure is required.

**Partner Compatibility and Suspension**

A political, civic, public, charitable, or campaign-related partner may be classified as:

• mission-compatible;

• civic partner;

• policy-engagement partner;

• limited political-interface partner;

• compliance-reviewed partner;

• suspended;

• non-admissible;

• incompatible.

Compatibility is limited to the reviewed scope.

It does not constitute endorsement of the partner’s leadership, candidates, programme, ideology, communications, donors, affiliates, or future activity.

A partner shall be suspended or downgraded where it:

• uses Prometheus data for political targeting;

• conditions benefits on political behaviour;

• misrepresents Prometheus endorsement;

• suppresses evidence or limitations;

• combines civic and campaign databases;

• misuses public resources;

• violates safeguarding or privacy;

• refuses audit or correction;

• uses regenerative claims to conceal political or financial extraction.

**Electoral Compliance Gate**

A Prometheus-compatible political or electoral interaction may proceed only when:

• the participating entities are identified;

• the activity and jurisdiction are classified;

• the political-independence boundary is preserved;

• data classes and purposes are defined;

• campaign and Prometheus systems are substantively separated;

• beneficiaries are protected from political conditioning;

• public and charitable resources are controlled;

• contributions and in-kind support are recorded;

• voter, donor, child, health, beneficiary, and ecological data remain appropriately separated;

• AI use is bounded;

• retention and incident procedures are active;

• applicable legal review is complete;

• governance has admitted the defined interaction;

• Claims Register entries exist;

• unresolved risks are disclosed.

No interaction may be described as compliant, nonpartisan, data-safe, campaign-independent, or publicly authorised beyond the scope supported by evidence and legal review.

The lowest unresolved civic-data, privacy, campaign-finance, public-resource, electoral, safeguarding, or legal gate controls the permitted interaction.

# Civic Data and Electoral Integrity Invariant

Prometheus may support civic participation.

It may not convert participation into political surveillance.

It may support public policy.

It may not certify political promises.

It may distribute plants, food, knowledge, tools, and opportunity.

It may not exchange benefits for political support.

It may interact with political actors.

It may not become their data infrastructure.

It may use AI to organise information.

It may not use AI to manipulate political behaviour.

It may preserve civic memory.

It may not transform civic memory into a voter profile.

The constitutional rule is:

regeneration serves life;

civic infrastructure serves participation;

elections remain governed by law;

no political objective authorises extraction from people, data, public resources, or community trust.

**Sweet Potato Campaign and Civic Plant-Distribution Protocol**

The Sweet Potato Campaign and Civic Plant-Distribution Protocol governs any Prometheus-compatible initiative through which sweet-potato slips, cuttings, tubers, plants, seeds, trees, nursery stock, propagation material, cultivation guidance, tools, compost, soil amendments, food, or related regenerative resources are gifted, distributed, subsidised, exchanged, or delivered in a civic, public-benefit, community, political, electoral, educational, charitable, or campaign-adjacent context.

The Protocol applies whether the initiative is described as:

• a campaign;

• a civic action;

• a public-benefit programme;

• a community distribution;

• a nursery programme;

• a food-security initiative;

• a voter-engagement event;

• a stewardship invitation;

• a school or family programme;

• a political demonstration;

• a charitable donation;

• an educational activity;

• a regenerative deployment pathway.

The name given to the initiative does not determine its legal, political, charitable, electoral, phytosanitary, tax, public-resource, food-safety, or data-protection classification.

Applicable law and factual operation govern.

**Constitutional Purpose**

A civic plant-distribution initiative may support:

• household productive capacity;

• food-system learning;

• practical stewardship;

• nursery development;

• local propagation;

• community participation;

• school and family education;

• small-scale food access;

• regenerative awareness;

• decentralised demonstration;

• future OHE development.

These are programme purposes.

The distribution of a plant does not by itself demonstrate:

• successful cultivation;

• food production;

• food consumption;

• food security;

• nutrition improvement;

• household resilience;

• economic saving;

• political support;

• community transformation;

• ecological regeneration;

• PRU eligibility;

• public value.

The controlling distinction is:

plant distributed ≠ plant established;

plant established ≠ crop produced;

crop produced ≠ crop consumed;

crop consumed ≠ food security;

programme participation ≠ political support;

gift ≠ vote;

distribution ≠ impact;

publicity ≠ verification.

**Unconditional-Gift Rule**

Any plant, slip, cutting, seed, tuber, food item, tool, educational material, or related benefit distributed through a Prometheus-compatible civic initiative shall be provided without requiring or implying a political exchange.

The recipient shall not be required to:

• vote;

• register to vote;

• promise to vote;

• vote for or against a candidate;

• support or oppose a party;

• support or oppose a referendum;

• become a party member;

• volunteer for a campaign;

• make a political donation;

• display a political sign;

• provide a political endorsement;

• attend a political meeting;

• participate in canvassing;

• create campaign content;

• provide political-preference data;

• consent to political contact;

• publish a testimonial;

• post on social media;

• provide unnecessary personal information.

No recipient shall be denied, deprioritised, delayed, embarrassed, ranked, or excluded because the recipient refuses any unrelated political, communications, research, media, or data request.

The unconditional nature of the gift shall be clear in:

• scripts;

• event signage;

• volunteer training;

• registration forms;

• digital interfaces;

• follow-up communications;

• public materials;

• recipient guidance.

The constitutional rule is:

the plant is freely given within the programme’s legitimate eligibility and capacity rules;

no political obligation attaches to it.

**Political and Civic Classification**

Before distribution, the responsible entity shall determine whether the initiative is operated by or connected to:

• a political party;

• a candidate committee;

• a campaign committee;

• a referendum committee;

• an elected official;

• a public administration;

• a charity;

• a school;

• a religious institution;

• a foundation;

• a cooperative;

• a community organisation;

• an independent civic entity;

• a commercial sponsor;

• a Prometheus-compatible operator.

The classification shall identify:

• the actual provider of the benefit;

• the legal owner of the plant material;

• the entity paying for propagation, transport, storage, events, labour, media, and digital systems;

• the entity receiving political or reputational benefit;

• the entity controlling recipient data;

• the entity responsible for legal and financial reporting.

A civic label shall not conceal a campaign expenditure.

A charitable label shall not conceal partisan activity.

A political label shall not automatically convert every ecological or educational activity into campaign activity.

The applicable treatment requires factual and jurisdiction-specific review.

**Sweet Potato Campaign Readiness States**

Prometheus establishes the following readiness ladder.

**SPC-0 — Conceptual Campaign**

The distribution idea, message, intended community, or political concept has been proposed.

No claim concerning legality, food security, production, impact, reach, or electoral neutrality is permitted.

**SPC-1 — Responsible Entity and Programme Purpose Identified**

The provider, organiser, funder, nursery source, intended recipients, geographic scope, and preliminary programme purpose have been identified.

Identification does not constitute authorisation or compliance.

**SPC-2 — Legal, Electoral, Plant-Health, and Data Pathways Identified**

The applicable political, electoral, charitable, tax, public-resource, phytosanitary, nursery, transport, food, data, child-safeguarding, and public-event requirements have been identified.

No distribution may begin until applicable controls are active.

**SPC-3 — Distribution Dossier Complete**

The material source, quantities, valuation, logistics, eligibility, unconditional-gift controls, recipient guidance, data boundary, volunteer training, communications rules, incident pathway, follow-up method, and Claims Register entries are complete.

No success or impact claim is permitted.

**SPC-4 — Authorised Limited Distribution**

A defined distribution may proceed within the reviewed entity, jurisdiction, geography, quantity, event, purpose, budget, data, communications, and political boundary.

Claims are limited to verified distribution outputs.

**SPC-5 — Operationally Verified Distribution**

The programme can reconcile material received, propagated, distributed, lost, damaged, recalled, retained, or undistributed, together with costs, providers, recipients where lawfully necessary, complaints, incidents, and follow-up boundaries.

Operational verification is not evidence of successful cultivation.

**SPC-6 — Evidence-Supported Civic Plant Programme**

Repeated and appropriately sampled evidence supports defined outcomes such as plant survival, cultivation adoption, production, household use, educational benefit, nursery development, or local propagation capacity.

Only Claims Register-approved wording may be used.

**SPC-7 — Independently Reviewed Replication Package**

The programme has an independently reviewed legal, electoral, plant-health, logistical, financial, data, educational, operational, evidence, risk, and replication package.

Replication readiness does not eliminate the need for jurisdictional, climatic, varietal, phytosanitary, cultural, and institutional adaptation.

The SPC ladder is distinct from:

• political support;

• electoral success;

• CDEC compliance maturity;

• CTD maturity;

• PVM maturity;

• PL-OHE readiness;

• SFF readiness;

• PRU-R maturity;

• RAP-M maturity;

• nursery certification;

• phytosanitary status;

• funding status.

No ladder may substitute for another.

**Mandatory Campaign and Distribution Dossier**

Before distribution, the responsible entity shall maintain a versioned dossier containing, where applicable:

1.  programme name;

2.  responsible legal entity;

3.  political or civic classification;

4.  jurisdiction;

5.  programme purpose;

6.  target geography;

7.  recipient eligibility, if any;

8.  plant-material type;

9.  botanical and varietal identification;

10. propagation source;

11. supplier or nursery;

12. ownership of material;

13. phytosanitary status;

14. invasive-species review;

15. intellectual-property or protected-variety restrictions;

16. quantity acquired or propagated;

17. quantity available;

18. unit valuation;

19. total acquisition or production cost;

20. labour and volunteer-cost treatment;

21. transport and storage cost;

22. public-resource use;

23. donation and in-kind record;

24. distribution locations and dates;

25. storage requirements;

26. packaging and labelling;

27. recipient cultivation guidance;

28. climatic and seasonal suitability;

29. food-safety and non-guarantee language;

30. unconditional-gift statement;

31. volunteer script and training;

32. political and communications boundary;

33. data-collection plan;

34. consent and media rules;

35. child-safeguarding controls;

36. complaints and incident pathway;

37. recall or quarantine pathway;

38. outcome-measurement plan;

39. Claims Register entries;

40. financial and electoral reporting status;

41. legal review status;

42. unresolved risks.

The dossier shall distinguish verified information, assumptions, pending matters, excluded claims, and conditions requiring suspension.

**Plant Material Identity and Provenance**

Every batch of material shall have sufficient identification and provenance for its intended use.

The record should include, where applicable:

• common name;

• botanical name;

• cultivar or variety;

• lot or batch identifier;

• supplier;

• propagation date;

• origin;

• generation or propagation method;

• storage history;

• visible-health inspection;

• phytosanitary documentation;

• treatment history;

• known restrictions;

• climate suitability;

• intended planting period.

A sweet-potato slip shall not be treated as a generic, universally suitable, pathogen-free, high-yield, or climate-resilient plant merely because it appears healthy.

Recipient materials shall clearly distinguish:

• verified varietal characteristics;

• supplier statements;

• general cultivation guidance;

• site-dependent expectations;

• unresolved uncertainty.

**Plant Health, Biosecurity, and Phytosanitary Boundary**

Plant distribution can spread pests, diseases, pathogens, invasive organisms, contaminated soil, or unsuitable genetic material.

The programme shall therefore evaluate, where applicable:

• pest and disease status;

• regulated plant movement;

• state, regional, national, or cross-border restrictions;

• nursery requirements;

• quarantine requirements;

• soil or growing-medium restrictions;

• sweet-potato weevils or other relevant pests;

• viral, fungal, bacterial, or nematode risks;

• cleaning and sanitation;

• storage separation;

• batch traceability;

• recall and quarantine.

No material shall be distributed where the responsible programme knows or reasonably suspects that it is:

• diseased;

• infested;

• contaminated;

• misidentified;

• legally restricted;

• unsuitable for the intended movement;

• likely to create material ecological harm.

A regenerative purpose does not reduce biosecurity obligations.

**Geographic, Climatic, and Cultivation Suitability**

Recipient guidance shall identify that cultivation outcomes depend on factors including:

• climate;

• frost;

• planting date;

• soil temperature;

• rainfall;

• irrigation;

• soil drainage;

• photoperiod;

• variety;

• pest pressure;

• planting density;

• management;

• harvest timing;

• storage.

The programme shall not promise:

• a guaranteed crop;

• a guaranteed yield;

• a fixed household-food contribution;

• universal survival;

• economic savings;

• nutritional outcomes;

• climate resilience;

• independence from external food systems.

Where distribution covers different climate zones, guidance shall be locally adapted or clearly bounded as general information.

**Logistics and Chain of Custody**

The programme shall track, where applicable:

• material acquired;

• material propagated;

• material received from donors;

• material stored;

• material transferred between nodes;

• material distributed;

• material damaged;

• material expired or no longer viable;

• material quarantined;

• material recalled;

• material retained for propagation;

• material composted or destroyed.

The system shall define:

• storage conditions;

• maximum storage duration;

• watering or care before distribution;

• packing;

• transport;

• event inventory;

• custody handover;

• end-of-event reconciliation;

• surplus treatment.

The count “distributed” shall refer only to material actually transferred to a recipient or authorised destination.

Material delivered to an event, displayed, reserved, or left unattended shall not be counted as successfully distributed.

**Recipient Eligibility and Fair Access**

Where quantities are limited, the programme shall define transparent allocation rules.

Potential criteria may include:

• geography;

• programme capacity;

• household interest;

• school or community participation;

• cultivation feasibility;

• first-come allocation;

• lottery;

• priority populations under a lawful public-benefit programme;

• nursery or demonstration purpose.

Eligibility criteria shall not use:

• party membership;

• voting history;

• political support;

• campaign donation;

• donor status;

• token ownership;

• publicity consent;

• ideological conformity;

• unnecessary personal data.

Where a programme claims equitable access, it shall evaluate material barriers including:

• transport;

• language;

• disability;

• planting space;

• water access;

• cultivation knowledge;

• digital access;

• distribution time;

• economic capacity to maintain the plant.

Equal distribution does not necessarily create equal capacity to establish a crop.

**Recipient Data and Privacy**

The minimum necessary data shall be collected.

A simple distribution may require no personal data beyond aggregate counting.

Where follow-up, legal reporting, plant recall, safeguarding, or programme evaluation requires additional information, the purpose and retention period shall be explicit.

Recipient data shall not be used for:

• voter registration;

• voter profiling;

• campaign persuasion;

• political fundraising;

• party membership;

• donor prospecting;

• commercial advertising;

• investor marketing;

• token distribution;

• unrelated surveillance.

A recipient’s address, phone number, email, photograph, garden location, crop result, or family information shall not be transferred to a political actor merely because that actor sponsored, promoted, or attended the distribution.

Consent to cultivation follow-up does not constitute consent to political communication.

**Political Communications Boundary**

Political or electoral communications shall remain clearly separable from:

• receipt of the plant;

• cultivation instructions;

• plant-health notices;

• recall communications;

• safeguarding messages;

• programme evaluation;

• food-safety information.

Where a political organisation conducts the distribution, materials shall still make clear that:

• receipt is unconditional;

• voting is not required;

• political support is not required;

• personal testimony is optional;

• political contact preferences are separate;

• the plant is not payment or consideration for political activity.

Recipients shall not be placed under social or reputational pressure to express gratitude to a candidate, party, donor, or elected official.

**Media, Video, and Testimonial Consent**

A recipient may voluntarily share:

• planting photographs;

• cultivation updates;

• harvest results;

• educational experiences;

• testimonials.

Such participation shall be optional and separately consented.

Refusal shall not affect access to plants, future programmes, cultivation support, public benefits, or community status.

A video, photograph, quotation, or social-media post shall not be used to imply:

• political endorsement;

• verified food security;

• generalised programme success;

• guaranteed yield;

• health benefit;

• PRU value;

• national scalability.

The programme shall distinguish:

• documentary evidence;

• personal story;

• communications material;

• research data;

• campaign content.

One category shall not be silently converted into another.

**Children and School Distribution**

Where children or schools participate, the School Food Forest and Child Safeguarding Protocol applies.

No child shall be required or encouraged to:

• endorse a political actor;

• appear in campaign communications;

• reveal family political views;

• provide a testimonial;

• share a home location;

• connect school participation with political support.

Plant-distribution data involving minors shall remain separate from political, donor, commercial, or campaign systems.

A plant supplied to a school does not demonstrate curriculum impact, household food production, improved diet, or political support.

**Public and Charitable Resource Boundary**

Where the initiative uses:

• public land;

• school property;

• public staff;

• municipal vehicles;

• public nurseries;

• public funds;

• charitable funds;

• tax-advantaged resources;

• donated labour;

• institutional mailing lists;

• public communications systems;

the authorised purpose, cost allocation, staff time, resource use, reporting obligations, branding, and political restrictions shall be documented.

No public or charitable resource shall be treated as free merely because no invoice is issued.

Unrecorded staff time, vehicle use, venue use, plant material, software, data access, or communications support may remain economically and legally material.

**Contributions and In-Kind Valuation**

The responsible entity shall record material contributions, including:

• plant material;

• propagation services;

• nursery space;

• soil and growing media;

• containers;

• labels;

• transport;

• storage;

• venue;

• tools;

• staff time;

• professional services;

• software;

• advertising;

• photography;

• communications;

• volunteer labour where legally relevant.

The record shall identify:

• provider;

• recipient entity;

• valuation method;

• political, charitable, public, or commercial status;

• restriction;

• date;

• reporting status.

Prometheus does not determine the applicable campaign-finance, tax, charitable, procurement, or accounting treatment.

Competent jurisdiction-specific review is required.

**Volunteer Governance**

Volunteers shall receive training appropriate to their roles.

Training shall cover:

• unconditional-gift rule;

• prohibited political pressure;

• data minimisation;

• recipient dignity;

• plant identification;

• handling;

• storage;

• health and safety;

• complaint escalation;

• media consent;

• child safeguarding where applicable;

• inventory control.

Volunteers shall not:

• record political preferences;

• ask how a recipient intends to vote;

• condition distribution on political conversation;

• add recipients to political mailing lists without separate lawful consent;

• claim guaranteed cultivation outcomes;

• provide undisclosed professional agricultural, medical, legal, or financial advice;

• pressure recipients to produce campaign content.

**Cultivation Guidance and Support**

The recipient should receive clear, accessible cultivation guidance appropriate to the material and geography.

Guidance may include:

• planting period;

• soil preparation;

• spacing;

• water;

• frost protection;

• weed control;

• pest observation;

• harvest timing;

• storage;

• propagation;

• food handling;

• sources of further assistance.

The programme shall identify whether support is:

• general educational information;

• locally adapted guidance;

• professional agronomic support;

• community peer support;

• unavailable after distribution.

The absence of follow-up support shall be disclosed where material to expected success.

**Outcome Measurement**

The programme shall distinguish:

**Output indicators**

• slips propagated;

• slips distributed;

• recipients reached;

• schools or communities reached;

• guidance materials delivered;

• volunteers trained;

• events conducted.

**Establishment indicators**

• recipient-reported planting;

• plant survival;

• area cultivated;

• follow-up participation;

• replacement required.

**Productive indicators**

• harvest occurrence;

• quantity harvested;

• edible quantity;

• propagation material retained;

• seasonal continuity.

**Use and public-value indicators**

• household consumption;

• sharing or donation;

• school use;

• educational use;

• local propagation;

• perceived barriers;

• maintenance burden.

No claim may skip from distribution outputs directly to food-security, health, economic, ecological, or community-impact conclusions.

Outcome collection shall be proportionate and shall not convert the gift into a surveillance obligation.

**Claims Register Language**

Early-stage permitted language may include:

• sweet-potato slips were distributed;

• recipients received cultivation guidance;

• the initiative is testing decentralised household propagation;

• follow-up is voluntary;

• cultivation outcomes remain site-dependent;

• food-security effects have not yet been established.

Prohibited or restricted language without sufficient evidence includes:

• every family becomes food-secure;

• one plant ends hunger;

• guaranteed harvest;

• universal climate resilience;

• proven health improvement;

• self-sufficient households;

• X-fold public return;

• voter-supported regenerative mandate;

• national programme validated by distribution count.

**Failure, Adverse Outcomes, and Learning**

The programme shall record material failures including:

• low plant survival;

• transport damage;

• unsuitable timing;

• disease;

• pest spread;

• incorrect identification;

• recipient inability to plant;

• lack of water;

• insufficient guidance;

• complaint;

• political pressure;

• unauthorised data use;

• inventory discrepancy;

• food-safety issue;

• reputational misuse.

Failure records shall inform:

• guidance updates;

• source selection;

• varietal selection;

• timing;

• storage;

• volunteer training;

• Claims Register status;

• future distribution.

Failure shall not be erased merely because the initiative has symbolic, civic, or political value.

**Recall, Quarantine, and Suspension**

The programme shall maintain a pathway for suspending distribution and, where reasonably feasible, notifying recipients if material is later suspected to be:

• diseased;

• infested;

• contaminated;

• misidentified;

• legally restricted;

• unsuitable for movement;

• connected to a material safety risk.

The response may include:

• batch quarantine;

• distribution suspension;

• recipient notice;

• disposal guidance;

• regulator or nursery notification;

• supplier review;

• evidence preservation;

• public correction;

• Claims Register update.

A political calendar or communications objective shall not delay a necessary plant-health or safety response.

**Sweet Potato Campaign Admission Gate**

A Prometheus-compatible civic plant-distribution programme may be described as operational only when:

• the responsible legal entity is identified;

• the civic, political, charitable, public, or educational classification is documented;

• the unconditional-gift rule is operational;

• plant identity and source are documented;

• applicable plant-health and movement requirements are reviewed;

• quantities and material custody can be reconciled;

• contribution and public-resource records are maintained;

• recipient data are minimised and separated from political systems;

• volunteer scripts and training are active;

• media and testimonial consent is separate;

• cultivation guidance is available;

• complaints, quarantine, recall, and incident pathways exist;

• Claims Register entries are active;

• unresolved risks are disclosed.

It may be described as evidence-supported only when establishment, production, use, or public-value outcomes have passed their applicable evidence gates.

It may be described as replication-ready only when its legal, electoral, plant-health, logistical, data, operational, cost, failure, and evidence systems have been independently reviewed.

The lowest unresolved electoral, plant-health, public-resource, data, safeguarding, operational, or evidence gate controls the permitted claim.

**Civic Gift Invariant**

Prometheus may use a plant to invite participation in regeneration.

It may not use a plant to purchase political loyalty.

It may distribute productive capacity.

It may not promise productivity it has not measured.

It may record distribution.

It may not call distribution food security.

It may invite recipients to share their experience.

It may not make testimony a condition of benefit.

It may support a political mission.

It may not convert a civic gift into voter extraction.

The constitutional rule is:

the gift remains unconditional;

the data remain bounded;

the plant remains biologically and legally traceable;

the claim remains no stronger than the evidence.

**Regenerative Propagation and Civic Distribution Protocol**

The Regenerative Propagation and Civic Distribution Protocol governs every Prometheus-compatible initiative through which seeds, slips, cuttings, tubers, seedlings, trees, nursery stock, propagation materials, cultivation guidance, tools, compost, soil amendments, food-producing plants, or related regenerative resources are propagated, gifted, distributed, subsidised, exchanged, or delivered for a civic, ecological, educational, charitable, community, public-benefit, institutional, or politically adjacent purpose.

This Protocol establishes the general canonical architecture.

A programme centred on a particular species, territory, institution, or public-facing initiative is an implementation of this Protocol and does not replace or redefine it.

The applicable hierarchy is:

# Regenerative Propagation and Civic Distribution Protocol

→ Civic Plant Distribution Programme\
→ species-, territory-, or institution-specific pilot\
→ public-facing campaign or communications name.

Accordingly, an externally named “Sweet Potato Campaign” may be classified within Prometheus as a:

Sweet Potato Civic Propagation Pilot.

The external communications name does not determine the initiative’s canonical, legal, evidentiary, ecological, electoral, phytosanitary, financial, or governance status.

**Constitutional Purpose**

Regenerative propagation and civic distribution may support:

• household productive capacity;

• local nursery development;

• decentralised propagation;

• food-system learning;

• ecological literacy;

• practical stewardship;

• school and family education;

• community participation;

• local food production;

• species and cultivar conservation;

• climate-adapted planting;

• future One Human Ecosystem development;

• distributed territorial resilience.

These are legitimate programme purposes.

They are not automatically achieved outcomes.

The propagation or distribution of plant material does not by itself demonstrate:

• successful establishment;

• crop production;

• household consumption;

• food security;

• nutritional improvement;

• household resilience;

• ecological regeneration;

• economic saving;

• public value;

• community transformation;

• political support;

• PRU eligibility;

• RAP maturity;

• financial value.

The controlling distinction is:

material propagated ≠ material distributed;

material distributed ≠ material planted;

material planted ≠ plant established;

plant established ≠ productive output;

productive output ≠ consumption;

consumption ≠ food security;

programme participation ≠ political support;

distribution ≠ verified impact.

**Scope**

This Protocol applies whether an initiative is described as:

• civic plant distribution;

• community nursery programme;

• seed or plant library;

• public-benefit propagation programme;

• family food-resilience programme;

• school planting programme;

• regenerative-gardening initiative;

• community food-production pilot;

• political or public-policy demonstration;

• charitable distribution;

• municipal plant programme;

• public-facing campaign;

• stewardship invitation;

• One Human Ecosystem activation pathway.

The name assigned to the initiative does not determine its legal classification.

Applicable law, institutional authority, factual operation, funding source, data use, public-resource use, and political context govern that classification.

**Unconditional-Benefit Rule**

Any plant, seed, slip, cutting, tuber, tree, tool, cultivation material, food item, educational resource, or related benefit distributed through a Prometheus-compatible programme shall be provided without requiring or implying an unrelated political, commercial, financial, religious, reputational, or data exchange.

A recipient shall not be required to:

• vote;

• register to vote;

• promise to vote;

• support or oppose a candidate;

• support or oppose a political party;

• support or oppose a referendum;

• become a member of a political, religious, commercial, or donor organisation;

• volunteer for a campaign;

• make a political or charitable donation;

• display political or commercial branding;

• attend a political event;

• create promotional or campaign content;

• provide a testimonial;

• publish on social media;

• provide political-preference data;

• consent to political communications;

• consent to unrelated commercial communications;

• surrender unnecessary personal data.

No recipient shall be delayed, deprioritised, ranked, penalised, excluded, or publicly embarrassed for refusing an unrelated political, media, research, communications, or data request.

The unconditional nature of the benefit shall be expressed consistently in:

• event scripts;

• signage;

• digital interfaces;

• registration forms;

• volunteer training;

• recipient guidance;

• follow-up communications;

• public materials.

The constitutional rule is:

the regenerative resource is provided within legitimate programme eligibility and capacity rules;

no unrelated obligation attaches to it.

**Programme Classification**

Before propagation or distribution begins, the responsible entity shall identify whether the initiative is operated, funded, sponsored, promoted, hosted, or materially supported by:

• a community organisation;

• a cooperative;

• a nursery;

• a school;

• a university;

• a municipality;

• a public agency;

• a charity;

• a foundation;

• a religious institution;

• a commercial entity;

• a political party;

• a candidate or campaign committee;

• an elected official;

• a public administration;

• a Prometheus-compatible operator.

The classification shall identify:

• the actual provider of the material;

• the legal owner of the material;

• the entity funding propagation, acquisition, storage, transport, events, labour, media, and digital systems;

• the entity controlling recipient data;

• the entity receiving political, commercial, institutional, or reputational benefit;

• the entity responsible for legal, tax, accounting, procurement, charitable, electoral, phytosanitary, and data-protection obligations.

A civic label shall not conceal political activity.

A charitable label shall not conceal partisan activity.

A political label shall not automatically convert all ecological, educational, or community functions into campaign activity.

A Prometheus label shall not create legal admission.

**Regenerative Propagation and Distribution Readiness States**

Prometheus establishes the following readiness ladder.

**RPCD-0 — Conceptual Initiative**

A propagation, distribution, nursery, educational, civic, or public-facing initiative has been proposed.

No claim concerning compliance, establishment, production, public value, ecological performance, food security, or replication is permitted.

**RPCD-1 — Responsible Entity and Purpose Identified**

The responsible entity, programme purpose, intended beneficiaries, geography, propagation source, and preliminary material type have been identified.

Identification does not constitute approval, compliance, or operational readiness.

**RPCD-2 — Legal, Biological, Institutional, and Data Pathways Identified**

Applicable nursery, phytosanitary, biosecurity, plant-movement, food-safety, public-resource, educational, safeguarding, electoral, charitable, tax, data, and public-event requirements have been identified.

No propagation or distribution may begin until applicable controls are active.

**RPCD-3 — Programme Dossier Complete**

Material sources, batch identity, quantities, valuation, propagation methods, logistics, eligibility rules, unconditional-benefit controls, recipient guidance, volunteer training, data boundaries, public communications, incident procedures, and Claims Register entries are complete.

No outcome or impact claim is permitted.

**RPCD-4 — Authorised Limited Pilot**

A defined pilot may proceed within its approved entity, geography, quantity, species, season, recipients, purpose, budget, data, communications, and legal boundary.

Claims are limited to implementation and verified outputs.

**RPCD-5 — Operationally Verified Programme**

The programme can reconcile material acquired, propagated, stored, transferred, distributed, damaged, quarantined, recalled, retained, composted, or destroyed, together with programme costs, incidents, complaints, and data use.

Operational verification is not evidence of successful establishment or public value.

**RPCD-6 — Evidence-Supported Propagation and Distribution System**

Repeated and appropriately sampled evidence supports defined outcomes such as:

• survival;

• establishment;

• cultivation adoption;

• harvest;

• local propagation;

• educational use;

• household use;

• nursery development;

• territorial distribution capacity.

Only Claims Register-approved wording may be used.

**RPCD-7 — Independently Reviewed Replication Package**

The programme has an independently reviewed legal, biological, phytosanitary, operational, logistical, financial, data, educational, safeguarding, evidence, risk, and adaptation package.

Replication readiness does not remove the need for local ecological, climatic, legal, cultural, institutional, and biosecurity review.

The RPCD ladder is distinct from:

• CTD maturity;

• PVM public-value maturity;

• PL-OHE readiness;

• SFF readiness;

• CDEC compliance maturity;

• PRU-R maturity;

• RAP-M maturity;

• nursery certification;

• phytosanitary certification;

• political support;

• funding status.

No ladder may substitute for another.

**Mandatory Regenerative Propagation and Distribution Dossier**

Before implementation, the responsible entity shall maintain a versioned dossier containing, where applicable:

1.  programme identifier;

2.  responsible legal entity;

3.  operating partners;

4.  civic, educational, charitable, public, commercial, or political classification;

5.  jurisdiction;

6.  programme purpose;

7.  target geography;

8.  intended recipients;

9.  eligibility or allocation rules;

10. plant-material category;

11. common and botanical name;

12. cultivar, variety, accession, or genetic line where known;

13. batch or lot identifier;

14. propagation source;

15. nursery or supplier;

16. ownership of material;

17. propagation method;

18. propagation date;

19. plant-health status;

20. phytosanitary status;

21. invasive-species review;

22. protected-variety or licensing restrictions;

23. quantity acquired;

24. quantity propagated;

25. quantity available;

26. quantity distributed;

27. unit and total valuation;

28. labour-cost treatment;

29. volunteer-labour treatment;

30. transport and storage costs;

31. public or charitable resources used;

32. donation and in-kind records;

33. distribution locations and dates;

34. storage and viability requirements;

35. packaging and labelling;

36. recipient cultivation guidance;

37. climatic and seasonal suitability;

38. water and maintenance assumptions;

39. non-guarantee language;

40. unconditional-benefit statement;

41. volunteer scripts and training;

42. political and communications boundaries;

43. data-collection plan;

44. consent and media rules;

45. child-safeguarding controls;

46. complaints and incident procedures;

47. quarantine and recall pathway;

48. outcome-measurement plan;

49. Claims Register entries;

50. financial, public-resource, charitable, tax, or electoral reporting status;

51. legal and institutional review status;

52. unresolved risks.

The dossier shall distinguish:

• verified facts;

• supplier representations;

• programme assumptions;

• site-dependent expectations;

• pending approvals;

• excluded claims;

• conditions requiring suspension.

**Plant Identity and Provenance**

Each batch of material shall have sufficient identity and provenance for its intended use.

The record should include, where applicable:

• common name;

• botanical name;

• variety or cultivar;

• lot or batch;

• supplier or propagation node;

• geographic origin;

• propagation date;

• propagation method;

• generation;

• storage history;

• inspection status;

• treatment history;

• known restrictions;

• climate suitability;

• intended planting period.

Visual appearance alone does not establish:

• correct identity;

• varietal purity;

• pathogen-free status;

• legal movement status;

• climate suitability;

• expected yield;

• resilience;

• nutritional value;

• regenerative performance.

Recipient-facing materials shall distinguish verified characteristics from general guidance and site-dependent expectations.

**Biosecurity and Plant-Health Boundary**

Propagation and distribution can spread:

• regulated pests;

• plant diseases;

• viruses;

• fungi;

• bacteria;

• nematodes;

• invasive species;

• contaminated soil;

• unsuitable genetic material;

• resistant weeds;

• hazardous biological materials.

The programme shall evaluate, where applicable:

• nursery requirements;

• plant-movement restrictions;

• cross-border restrictions;

• regional quarantine;

• batch traceability;

• inspection;

• sanitation;

• cleaning;

• storage separation;

• growing-medium restrictions;

• treatment requirements;

• recall procedures;

• destruction procedures.

No material shall be distributed where the responsible programme knows or reasonably suspects that it is:

• diseased;

• infested;

• contaminated;

• materially misidentified;

• unlawfully moved;

• subject to unresolved quarantine;

• likely to create material ecological, agricultural, or public harm.

A regenerative purpose does not reduce biosecurity obligations.

**Ecological and Geographic Suitability**

Planting outcomes depend on:

• climate;

• frost;

• heat;

• planting season;

• soil temperature;

• rainfall;

• irrigation;

• drainage;

• salinity;

• photoperiod;

• elevation;

• variety;

• pest pressure;

• disease pressure;

• planting density;

• management;

• harvest timing;

• storage;

• local ecological relationships.

The programme shall not promise:

• universal survival;

• guaranteed establishment;

• guaranteed yield;

• household self-sufficiency;

• food security;

• economic savings;

• climatic resilience;

• reduced input dependency;

• ecological restoration;

• therapeutic or nutritional outcomes.

Where distribution crosses ecological or climatic zones, guidance shall be locally adapted or explicitly identified as general and non-site-specific.

**Propagation Node Governance**

A propagation node may include:

• a community nursery;

• school nursery;

• municipal nursery;

• private nursery;

• farm;

• greenhouse;

• household propagation site;

• distributed volunteer network;

• research institution;

• OHE nursery component.

Each node shall define:

• responsible operator;

• source material;

• propagation method;

• water source;

• sanitation;

• batch identity;

• material custody;

• contamination controls;

• inspection;

• loss reporting;

• transfer records;

• volunteer access;

• data responsibilities.

A decentralised network shall not be described as traceable merely because participants use a common application, spreadsheet, label, QR code, ledger, or distributed record.

Traceability requires alignment between physical material, batch identity, custody, records, and review.

**Logistics and Chain of Custody**

The programme shall track material through its relevant states:

source material\
→ propagation\
→ growing and maintenance\
→ inspection\
→ storage\
→ transfer\
→ distribution\
→ recipient establishment\
→ follow-up where applicable.

The record shall distinguish material:

• acquired;

• propagated;

• received from donors;

• stored;

• transferred;

• distributed;

• reserved;

• damaged;

• lost;

• no longer viable;

• quarantined;

• recalled;

• retained for further propagation;

• composted or destroyed.

“Distributed” shall refer only to material actually transferred to a recipient or authorised destination.

Material displayed, transported to an event, reserved, advertised, or remaining undistributed shall not be counted as successful distribution.

**Recipient Eligibility and Fair Access**

Where programme capacity is limited, allocation rules shall be transparent.

Potential criteria may include:

• geographic scope;

• ecological suitability;

• programme capacity;

• cultivation feasibility;

• educational purpose;

• demonstration purpose;

• public-benefit eligibility;

• first-come allocation;

• lottery;

• community or institutional priority defined by a lawful programme.

Eligibility shall not depend on:

• political affiliation;

• voting behaviour;

• campaign support;

• party membership;

• political donation;

• donor status;

• investor status;

• token ownership;

• publicity consent;

• ideological conformity;

• unnecessary data disclosure.

Where equity is claimed, the programme shall consider barriers including:

• lack of planting space;

• lack of water;

• disability;

• language;

• transport;

• digital exclusion;

• lack of cultivation knowledge;

• unsuitable climate;

• cost of continued maintenance.

Equal distribution does not necessarily create equal capacity to establish or benefit from plant material.

**Recipient Data and Privacy**

The programme shall collect the minimum data necessary for its legitimate purpose.

Some distributions may require only aggregate counts.

Additional personal information may be justified only for defined purposes such as:

• legal reporting;

• batch recall;

• safeguarding;

• institutional administration;

• voluntary follow-up;

• programme evaluation;

• technical support.

Recipient information shall not be used for:

• voter profiling;

• political persuasion;

• campaign fundraising;

• party membership;

• donor prospecting;

• unrelated commercial advertising;

• investor marketing;

• token allocation;

• social scoring;

• unrelated surveillance.

A recipient’s name, address, telephone number, email, photograph, land location, cultivation outcome, or family information shall not be transferred to a political, commercial, donor, or investment actor merely because that actor supported, funded, sponsored, promoted, or attended the programme.

Consent to cultivation follow-up is not consent to political or commercial communication.

**Political and Public-Communications Boundary**

Where a political party, candidate, elected official, administration, or public-policy organisation is connected to the programme, the Civic Neutrality and Political Independence Boundary and the Civic Data and Electoral Compliance Protocol apply.

Communications shall make clear that:

• receipt is unconditional;

• voting is not required;

• political support is not required;

• campaign participation is not required;

• testimony is optional;

• publicity is optional;

• political-contact consent is separate;

• cultivation outcomes are not guaranteed.

Recipients shall not be placed under political, social, economic, or reputational pressure to express gratitude, loyalty, endorsement, or support.

Prometheus does not certify the political actor, campaign, manifesto, or public promise associated with an initiative.

**Media, Testimonial, and Storytelling Boundary**

A recipient may voluntarily provide:

• photographs;

• cultivation updates;

• harvest information;

• educational reflections;

• programme feedback;

• testimonials.

Participation shall be optional and separately consented.

Refusal shall not affect:

• access to plant material;

• future eligibility;

• cultivation support;

• public benefits;

• educational access;

• community status.

A photograph, video, quotation, testimonial, or social-media post shall not be used to imply:

• verified establishment;

• generalised food security;

• universal programme success;

• guaranteed production;

• political endorsement;

• clinical or health benefit;

• PRU value;

• RAP maturity;

• national scalability.

Personal stories may illustrate experience.

They do not substitute for evidence.

**Children and Educational Settings**

Where children, schools, youth programmes, or educational institutions participate, the School Food Forest and Child Safeguarding Protocol applies.

No child shall be required or encouraged to:

• endorse a political actor;

• appear in campaign communications;

• reveal family political preferences;

• publish a testimonial;

• share home geolocation;

• connect educational participation with political support;

• provide unnecessary personal or behavioural data.

Plant material delivered to a school does not establish:

• curriculum integration;

• educational effectiveness;

• household food production;

• dietary improvement;

• community impact.

**Public and Charitable Resource Boundary**

Where the programme uses public or charitable resources, including:

• public land;

• school property;

• public staff;

• municipal vehicles;

• public nurseries;

• public funds;

• charitable funds;

• tax-advantaged resources;

• institutional mailing lists;

• volunteer labour;

• donated venues;

• public communications systems;

the programme shall document:

• authorised purpose;

• cost allocation;

• staff time;

• resource custody;

• reporting obligations;

• branding;

• communications;

• political limitations;

• procurement or charitable restrictions.

A resource is not economically or legally immaterial merely because no invoice is issued.

**Contributions and In-Kind Valuation**

The programme shall record material contributions, including:

• seeds;

• slips;

• cuttings;

• tubers;

• trees;

• nursery stock;

• propagation services;

• growing media;

• containers;

• water;

• energy;

• nursery space;

• transport;

• storage;

• venues;

• tools;

• staff time;

• professional services;

• software;

• communications;

• photography;

• advertising.

The record shall identify:

• provider;

• recipient entity;

• value or valuation method;

• purpose;

• restrictions;

• date;

• public, charitable, political, commercial, or volunteer status;

• reporting requirements.

Prometheus does not determine the applicable accounting, tax, electoral, procurement, or charitable treatment.

Jurisdiction-specific review remains necessary.

**Volunteer Governance**

Volunteers shall receive training proportionate to their role.

Training shall cover, where applicable:

• unconditional-benefit rule;

• political neutrality;

• recipient dignity;

• data minimisation;

• plant identity;

• plant handling;

• sanitation;

• storage;

• inventory;

• cultivation guidance limits;

• media consent;

• safeguarding;

• complaint escalation;

• incident reporting.

Volunteers shall not:

• request political support;

• ask how a recipient intends to vote;

• condition distribution on political dialogue;

• add recipients to political or commercial mailing lists without separate lawful consent;

• promise yield, food security, economic savings, health outcomes, or ecological performance;

• provide undisclosed professional agricultural, legal, medical, financial, or political advice.

**Cultivation Guidance and Support**

Recipient guidance should address, where relevant:

• planting time;

• propagation method;

• soil preparation;

• spacing;

• water;

• fertility;

• frost or heat protection;

• weed management;

• pest observation;

• disease observation;

• harvesting;

• storage;

• future propagation;

• food handling;

• sources of further support.

The programme shall identify whether guidance is:

• general educational information;

• locally adapted guidance;

• peer support;

• professional agronomic guidance;

• unavailable after distribution.

Where programme success materially depends on follow-up support, its absence shall be disclosed.

**Outcome Architecture**

The programme shall distinguish four levels of programme evidence.

**Output Indicators**

• material propagated;

• material distributed;

• recipients reached;

• schools, households, or organisations reached;

• volunteers trained;

• guidance materials delivered;

• events conducted.

**Establishment Indicators**

• material planted;

• survival;

• establishment period;

• area cultivated;

• replacement required;

• recipient follow-up.

**Productive Indicators**

• harvest occurrence;

• harvested quantity;

• edible quantity;

• seed or propagation material retained;

• seasonal continuity;

• crop loss.

**Use and Public-Value Indicators**

• household consumption;

• school use;

• sharing;

• donation;

• sale where applicable;

• local re-propagation;

• cultivation barriers;

• educational use;

• maintenance burden;

• territorial nursery capacity.

No claim may move directly from distribution outputs to food security, nutritional improvement, economic saving, ecological regeneration, political legitimacy, or public value.

**Claims Register Language**

Permitted early-stage language may include:

• plant material was propagated;

• plant material was distributed;

• recipients received cultivation guidance;

• the initiative is testing decentralised propagation;

• follow-up is voluntary;

• cultivation outcomes are site-dependent;

• establishment and food-security outcomes remain unverified.

Restricted or prohibited language without sufficient evidence includes:

• one plant ends hunger;

• every household becomes food-secure;

• guaranteed harvest;

• guaranteed savings;

• universal climate resilience;

• verified national impact;

• proven community transformation;

• self-sufficient household;

• guaranteed health improvement;

• campaign success validated by distribution count.

**Failure, Adverse Outcomes, and Learning**

The programme shall record material failures, including:

• low viability;

• low survival;

• unsuitable timing;

• unsuitable climate;

• transport damage;

• storage failure;

• disease;

• pest spread;

• contamination;

• misidentification;

• recipient inability to plant;

• lack of water;

• inadequate guidance;

• unauthorised political pressure;

• unauthorised data use;

• inventory discrepancy;

• complaint;

• food-safety issue;

• safeguarding issue.

Failure records shall inform:

• sourcing;

• propagation;

• varietal selection;

• timing;

• storage;

• logistics;

• volunteer training;

• recipient guidance;

• Claims Register maturity;

• replication decisions.

Failure shall not be excluded merely because the initiative carries civic, symbolic, charitable, or political value.

**Quarantine, Recall, and Suspension**

The programme shall maintain a pathway for suspending propagation or distribution where material is suspected to be:

• diseased;

• infested;

• contaminated;

• misidentified;

• legally restricted;

• ecologically unsuitable;

• unsafe for movement or use.

The response may include:

• batch quarantine;

• propagation suspension;

• distribution suspension;

• recipient notification;

• disposal guidance;

• supplier review;

• regulatory notification;

• public correction;

• Claims Register update;

• evidence preservation.

A political calendar, funding deadline, public event, communications objective, or reputational concern shall not delay a necessary safety or biosecurity response.

**Specific Implementation: Sweet Potato Civic Propagation Pilot**

A Sweet Potato Civic Propagation Pilot is a species-specific implementation of this Protocol.

It may test:

• slip propagation;

• local nursery capacity;

• household planting;

• school participation;

• decentralised multiplication;

• harvest;

• food use;

• recipient learning;

• territorial adaptation.

The public-facing name “Sweet Potato Campaign” may be retained by an external organisation for its own communications, provided that Prometheus records classify the initiative as a Sweet Potato Civic Propagation Pilot and apply all relevant:

• evidence;

• political-neutrality;

• unconditional-benefit;

• data;

• biosecurity;

• public-resource;

• safeguarding;

• logistical;

• Claims Register controls.

The public-facing communications name does not increase maturity or evidentiary status.

**Regenerative Propagation and Civic Distribution Admission Gate**

A programme may be described as operational only when:

• the responsible entity is identified;

• programme classification is documented;

• material identity and provenance are recorded;

• biological, phytosanitary, and movement risks are reviewed;

• the unconditional-benefit rule is operational;

• material quantities and custody can be reconciled;

• recipient eligibility and data collection are bounded;

• political, commercial, charitable, and public-resource relationships are disclosed;

• volunteer training is active;

• cultivation guidance is available;

• complaints, quarantine, recall, and incident procedures exist;

• Claims Register entries are active;

• unresolved risks are disclosed.

It may be described as evidence-supported only when establishment, production, use, or public-value outcomes have passed their respective evidence gates.

It may be described as replication-ready only when the legal, biological, phytosanitary, operational, logistical, data, safeguarding, financial, evidence, and failure systems have been independently reviewed.

The lowest unresolved biological, legal, electoral, safeguarding, public-resource, data, operational, or evidence gate controls the permitted claim.

**Regenerative Distribution Invariant**

Prometheus may propagate biological capacity.

It may not promise outcomes it has not measured.

It may distribute regenerative resources.

It may not purchase political loyalty.

It may record material distribution.

It may not call distribution food security.

It may invite participation.

It may not make testimony, publicity, data surrender, political support, or commercial affiliation a condition of benefit.

It may support species-specific pilots.

It shall preserve a species-neutral canonical architecture.

The constitutional rule is:

the benefit remains unconditional;

the material remains identifiable and traceable;

the data remain bounded;

the public claim remains no stronger than the evidence.

# Civic and Territorial Genesis Pilot Framework

The Civic and Territorial Genesis Pilot Framework governs the first bounded, pre-registered, evidence-generating deployment through which Prometheus tests whether its civic, ecological, operational, governance, public-value, legal, and institutional architecture can function coherently under real territorial conditions.

The Pilot is not a ceremonial demonstration.

It is not a marketing site.

It is not proof that Prometheus is validated.

It is not a political programme evaluation by declaration.

It is not an investment product.

It is not a pathway for converting civic participation, public expenditure, ecological activity, or community enthusiasm into automatic PRU value, RAP maturity, financial value, or political legitimacy.

The Pilot exists to test whether defined interventions produce measurable, reviewable, and sufficiently attributable outcomes while preserving:

• ecological integrity;

• evidence quality;

• community legitimacy;

• human dignity;

• legal compliance;

• safeguarding;

• political neutrality;

• operational continuity;

• maintenance capacity;

• public-resource protection;

• governance accountability;

• anti-capture boundaries.

The controlling rule is:

pilot activity is not pilot success;

pilot completion is not validation;

observed change is not causal proof;

local evidence is not universal evidence;

public value is not financial value;

successful implementation is not replication readiness;

no claim may exceed the lowest unresolved pilot gate.

**Pilot Purpose**

The Civic and Territorial Genesis Pilot shall test whether a bounded Prometheus-compatible territorial deployment can:

1.  establish legally and institutionally authorised regenerative infrastructure;

2.  generate complete baseline and longitudinal evidence;

3.  maintain assigned stewardship and operational continuity;

4.  improve defined ecological indicators relative to baseline and, where feasible, comparators;

5.  generate measurable productive, educational, civic, institutional, or public-value outcomes;

6.  preserve political neutrality and participant dignity;

7.  operate within safeguarding, food-safety, data, biosecurity, public-land, and legal boundaries;

8.  maintain transparent governance, finance, risk, incident, and Claims Register records;

9.  identify failure modes, trade-offs, costs, limitations, and external dependencies;

10. determine whether the deployment is suitable for continuation, adaptation, suspension, or replication.

The Pilot shall not be designed to maximise the number of positive claims.

It shall be designed to maximise learning quality, evidence integrity, and decision usefulness.

**Pilot Architecture**

The Genesis Pilot may contain one or more modules.

The minimum recommended integrated architecture comprises:

**Module A — Public-Land or Community OHE**

A legally authorised regenerative site integrating, where appropriate:

• soil restoration;

• water management;

• biomass cycling;

• productive perennial systems;

• biodiversity habitat;

• food-forest or syntropic design;

• public or community stewardship;

• ecological MRV.

This module tests biophysical performance, legal-use viability, stewardship assignment, maintenance continuity, and public-land operating discipline.

**Module B — School Food Forest or Educational Regenerative Site**

A child-safe and institutionally authorised educational deployment integrating:

• ecological learning;

• age-appropriate stewardship;

• curriculum-related activities;

• food-system education;

• supervised planting and observation;

• safeguarding;

• data minimisation;

• maintenance during institutional closures.

This module tests educational feasibility, teacher and operator workload, child safeguarding, participation, maintenance continuity, and appropriately bounded educational outcomes.

**Module C — Town Square or Community Regenerative Hub**

A civic operating node integrating a defined subset of:

• community meetings;

• local markets;

• public information;

• training;

• nursery activities;

• tool or seed libraries;

• food distribution;

• stewardship coordination;

• intergenerational learning;

• public-value reporting.

This module tests access, governance, operational continuity, resource custody, political neutrality, community participation, conflict resolution, and public accountability.

**Module D — Regenerative Propagation and Civic Distribution**

A traceable propagation and distribution system for seeds, slips, cuttings, seedlings, trees, nursery stock, or other regenerative materials.

This module tests:

• biological provenance;

• biosecurity;

• propagation-node capacity;

• distribution logistics;

• unconditional-benefit controls;

• recipient guidance;

• establishment;

• local re-propagation;

• data boundaries;

• public-value outcomes.

A Sweet Potato Civic Propagation Pilot may be included as a species-specific implementation of this module.

**Module E — Legal Commons and Stewardship Custody**

A legally reviewed structure supporting land use, asset custody, stewardship obligations, public benefit, community participation, data governance, continuity, transfer, termination, and anti-capture protections.

This module tests whether legal structure, governance, operations, maintenance, and factual control remain aligned.

**Module F — Civic Evidence and Public-Value System**

A Claims Register, evidence repository, public-value methodology, data-governance framework, public reporting process, and independent-review pathway.

This module tests whether programme claims remain traceable, bounded, appropriately worded, and separable from financial, political, or promotional narratives.

Not every pilot must contain every module.

The selected architecture shall be justified by the territorial need, site, institutions, population, resources, risk, and research questions.

A smaller coherent pilot is preferable to a larger deployment whose evidence, maintenance, or governance cannot be sustained.

**Pilot Unit of Analysis**

The Pilot shall explicitly define its unit or units of analysis.

Potential units include:

• plot;

• OHE;

• school site;

• household;

• propagation node;

• community hub;

• participant cohort;

• institution;

• neighbourhood;

• watershed sub-unit;

• territorial cluster.

The unit of implementation and the unit of analysis may differ.

For example:

• plants may be distributed to households, while the analytical unit is the propagation cohort;

• students may participate in activities, while the analytical unit is the school programme;

• several OHEs may form one territorial cluster, while ecological analysis remains site-specific.

The Pilot shall not aggregate incompatible units merely to create a stronger narrative.

**Pilot Hypothesis Register**

Every material Pilot claim shall be pre-registered as a hypothesis, programme theory, output target, exploratory question, or descriptive indicator.

The minimum Civic and Territorial Pilot Hypothesis Register should consider the following domains.

**CTH-1 — Ecological Function**

Defined regenerative management improves one or more ecological indicators relative to baseline and, where feasible, a comparator.

Potential indicators include:

• infiltration;

• soil cover;

• soil organic matter;

• aggregate stability;

• soil moisture persistence;

• erosion;

• biomass return;

• vegetation structure;

• functional biodiversity.

A planting event is not ecological improvement.

**CTH-2 — Water Function**

Defined landscape and management interventions improve water retention, reduce runoff or erosion, or reduce irrigation dependency after accounting for rainfall, season, soil, and management.

Water structures alone do not demonstrate water resilience.

**CTH-3 — Productive and Food-System Capacity**

The deployment produces measurable and usable food, nursery stock, propagation material, or productive services within the defined period and conditions.

Production shall be distinguished from:

• harvest;

• distribution;

• consumption;

• nutritional value;

• food security;

• revenue.

**CTH-4 — Operational Continuity**

The deployment can maintain defined ecological, safety, evidence, maintenance, staffing, and governance functions across seasons, absences, institutional closures, funding changes, and routine disturbances.

Initial implementation does not demonstrate continuity.

**CTH-5 — Stewardship Capacity**
