Assisted regeneration may include facilitation, role reset, conflict review, purpose clarification, rhythm redesign, evidence review, or temporary pause.

This is maintenance of the social organism.

Not failure.

**14. Membership and Participation Pathway**

Participation should develop through stages.

| **Stage** | **Meaning** | **Rights and limits** |
|----|----|----|
| Observer | Learns the culture and attends selected spaces. | No decision authority. |
| Participant | Contributes to activities and rituals. | Limited voice; no full governance authority. |
| Member | Accepts the Access Covenant and role responsibilities. | May participate in ordinary governance. |
| Role holder | Holds a temporary elected or assigned role. | Has specific mandate and accountability. |
| Custodian | Protects purpose, memory, integrity, or learning. | Service role; no ownership. |
| Elder / mentor | Supports continuity through experience. | Advisory authority only unless otherwise mandated. |

Belonging matures through participation.

Authority matures through demonstrated responsibility.

No person should receive structural authority merely because they are charismatic, wealthy, technical, early, loud, socially central, token-rich, or close to founders.

**15. Anti-Capture and Power-Separation Safeguards**

A Prometheus-compatible community should protect itself from capture.

Capture may arise through founder dominance, charisma, capital, technical access, token concentration, control of memory, control of conflict processes, control of onboarding, spiritual pressure, ideological pressure, or social dependency.

| **Capture risk** | **Safeguard** |
|----|----|
| Founder dominance | Role rotation, consent governance, documented mandates. |
| Charismatic control | Separation between purpose, conflict, memory, and access roles. |
| Investor capture | Separation between capital, governance, PRU value, and constitutional authority. |
| Technical capture | Access controls, audit logs, shared infrastructure knowledge, replaceable components. |
| Clique formation | Transparent onboarding, relationship weaving, protected dissent. |
| Memory capture | Decision log, rotating scribe, reviewable archive. |
| Conflict suppression | Formal conflict protocol and protected dissent. |
| Token capture | Scope-limited governance rights and anti-whale controls where applicable. |
| AI authority drift | Explicit distinction between AI support, recommendation, authorization, and execution. |

A community is healthy when no person or faction controls purpose, memory, conflict, access, capital, and technical infrastructure at the same time.

That concentration is the red flag.

**16. Relationship with Prometheus Constitution**

A Tribe, circle, or community may govern its own operations.

It may not amend the Prometheus constitution unless it enters the constitutional amendment pathway.

A community may interpret its own purpose.

It may not redefine PRU, RAP, MRV, TRBK, external human-learning research appendix, tokenization, or Access Covenant outside canonical definitions.

A community may create rituals.

It may not create coercive belonging.

A community may coordinate resources.

It may not convert participation into financial entitlement without legal structure.

A community may use AI tools.

It may not allow AI output to become governance authority.

A community may welcome capital.

It may not allow capital to command life.

A community may evolve.

It may not drift away from the foundational articles and Eternity Clauses when acting inside Prometheus.

**17. Specific Interoperability with Tribeke**

Tribeke may be treated as one specific expression of the broader Prometheus human-governance interoperability model.

Tribeke contributes the language of Tribe, freedom, belonging, training, community activation, relationship energy, and tokenized utility.

Prometheus contributes constitutional kernel discipline, MRV, PRU/RAP architecture, evidence-before-value, capital-readiness boundaries, ecological verification, and institutional trust.

The relationship should follow sovereign alignment.

TRBK remains Tribeke’s token layer.

PRU remains Prometheus’ verified regenerative performance layer.

RAP remains Prometheus’ portfolio aggregation layer.

MRV remains Prometheus’ evidence discipline.

The Access Covenant remains Prometheus’ entry condition.

Tribeke governance remains Tribeke governance.

Prometheus governance remains Prometheus governance.

Where the two systems collaborate, they may create a powerful shared field.

Where they diverge, they should restore boundaries respectfully and continue without confusion.

The strongest partnership is federation, not fusion.

**18. Final Principle**

**The final principle of Prometheus human governance is:**

no one governs alone, and no one disappears into the group.

The individual remains visible.

The circle remains responsible.

The community remains sovereign.

The constitution remains upstream.

Evidence remains the discipline.

The living system remains the source.

Prometheus does not build one community to dominate others.

It builds an architecture through which many communities can meet, resonate, decide, learn, repair, differentiate, and regenerate without losing their soul.

This is the functional synthesis of tribe, sociocracy, commons governance, community learning, psychological safety, self-determination, evolutionary purpose, and Prometheus constitutional discipline.

It is human governance with sovereign alignment.

**6: The Access Covenant — Conditions for Entry**

Before any entity — whether an individual, community, company, foundation, trust, investment fund, bioregional network, public agency, digital agent, infrastructure provider, or future institutional participant — may access, participate in, govern, finance, extend, automate, or benefit from the h•eart•h Prometheus ecosystem, it must enter into the Access Covenant.

The Access Covenant is the constitutional entry protocol of Prometheus.

It is not a mere click-through agreement.

It is not branding language.

It is not symbolic consent.

It is the threshold through which participation becomes constitutionally legible.

**The Covenant establishes that every Prometheus-facing relationship must remain aligned with:**

• the foundational articles;\
• the Eternity Clauses;\
• the universal human interoperability model;\
• the living-field principle;\
• evidence-before-value discipline;\
• human dignity;\
• ecological integrity;\
• voluntary participation;\
• governance accountability;\
• source-layer non-bypassability;\
• regenerative fidelity.

Access is conditional.

Participation is conditional.

Governance is conditional.

Capital access is conditional.

Token-mediated participation, where legally and technically available, is conditional.

No entity enters Prometheus merely because it holds capital, technical capacity, governance influence, institutional status, token exposure, data access, community status, religious authority, cultural authority, scientific authority, or model capability.

Every participant enters through covenant.

The Access Covenant defines the threshold between outside interest and inside participation.

Its purpose is to ensure that every actor understands the basic constitutional truth of Prometheus:

the ecosystem exists to serve life, evidence, dignity, governance, ecological integrity, and regenerative coherence.

It does not exist to provide a new interface for extraction, domination, greenwashing, speculative value inflation, tokenomic confusion, or institutional capture.

The Covenant is therefore the first operational expression of Prometheus’ constitutional architecture.

It translates meaning into entry.

It translates entry into responsibility.

It translates responsibility into reviewable participation.

**6.1 The Three Irrevocable Affirmations**

The Access Covenant is grounded in three affirmations.

These affirmations do not create the Eternity Clauses. They recognize them.

They establish that any participant entering Prometheus accepts that the constitutional source layer precedes ordinary governance, token logic, investor demand, operational convenience, community pressure, model output, institutional status, or market opportunity.

The affirmations should be presented in full before acceptance.

Acceptance should be recorded through the appropriate pathway for the participant type: digital, legal, governance-based, or combined.

For individuals and ordinary ecosystem participants, this may involve a signed digital acknowledgment.

For legal entities, funds, public agencies, foundations, trusts, companies, capital-market participants, or infrastructure providers, this should involve both digital acknowledgment and an enforceable legal access agreement, where appropriate.

The Three Affirmations are the constitutional minimum. They are not optional language.

They are the human, institutional, and technical doorway into Prometheus.

**6.1.1 First Affirmation — Recognition of living-system reality**

“I recognize that the principles encoded in the Eternity Clauses of the h•eart•h Prometheus constitution are not inventions of human agreement, market preference, token logic, governance majority, institutional power, community pressure, or model output.

They are codifications of living-system constraints that pre-exist any human institution.

They are discovered, not created.

They are observed, not voted into existence.

They are honored by governance, not subordinated to it.

I recognize that soil, water, biodiversity, climate, planetary boundaries, human dignity, ecological integrity, and the regenerative capacity of life form the living foundation of any durable economy.”

**6.1.2 Second Affirmation — Voluntary Submission**

“I voluntarily submit my participation in Prometheus to these principles as non-negotiable conditions of access.

I understand that no action I take, no vote I cast, no consensus I join, no power I accumulate, no capital I deploy, no token I hold, no model I operate, no community role I receive, and no governance position I occupy can legitimate any violation of these principles.

Any attempt to bypass, weaken, invert, dilute, obscure, or violate the foundational articles, the Eternity Clauses, the Access Covenant, or the constitutional source layer is invalid within Prometheus protected pathways.

**It is outside the valid Prometheus pathway.”**

**6.1.3 Third Affirmation — Stewardship Commitment**

“I accept stewardship responsibility for the principles I have recognized and to which I have voluntarily submitted.

This includes the duty to preserve constitutional meaning, resist semantic erosion, avoid extraction under regenerative language, respect evidence discipline, protect human dignity, preserve ecological integrity, and transmit these principles intact to future participants.

I understand that access to Prometheus is conditioned on faithful participation.

A violation of this Covenant may result in review, limitation, corrective action, suspension, exclusion, quarantine, loss of privileges, or other proportionate responses according to the applicable governance, legal, technical, and constitutional procedures.”

**6.2 Covenant Acceptance and Record Architecture**

Acceptance of the Access Covenant should be recorded in a manner appropriate to the participant type and implementation stage.

The objective is not theatrical immutability.

The objective is verifiable constitutional traceability.

**A mature implementation may include:**

• canonical Covenant text version;\
• hash of the canonical Covenant text;\
• timestamp of acceptance;\
• participant identity or entity identity;\
• authority of the signer, where relevant;\
• the full text or reference version of the Three Affirmations;\
• digital signature or equivalent authentication;\
• legal agreement reference, where applicable;\
• applicable jurisdiction, where applicable;\
• participant category;\
• scope of access granted;\
• governance rights, if any;\
• financial rights, if any;\
• data-access permissions, if any;\
• renewal status;\
• review status;\
• revocation, suspension, restriction, or limitation status, where applicable.

Where Prometheus is implemented through Holochain-compatible infrastructure, the acceptance event may be recorded as a signed entry on the participant’s source chain.

A reference, hash, or availability record may be published to the appropriate distributed layer according to the hApp design, privacy rules, and validation logic.

**The system should preserve the distinction between:**

• constitutional meaning;\
• technical record;\
• legal agreement;\
• governance status;\
• operational permission;\
• token or credential status;\
• investor-facing status;\
• MRV admissibility status.

These layers may interoperate.

They must not be collapsed.

A digital record does not replace legal documentation where legal rights are involved.

A legal document does not replace constitutional alignment.

A governance status does not create PRU value.

A token credential does not create investor entitlement.

A community role does not create Prometheus authority unless the relevant Prometheus pathway explicitly grants it. The record architecture exists to make participation traceable without confusing the meaning of participation.

**6.3 Cryptographic and Technical Enforcement**

The Access Covenant should be supported by cryptographic and technical controls where feasible.

**These controls may include:**

• signed acceptance events;\
• canonical text hashing;\
• source-chain records;\
• DHT publication where appropriate;\
• versioned Covenant references;\
• validation checks;\
• permission gates;\
• access-control lists;\
• role-based authorization;\
• revocation or suspension records;\
• audit logs;\
• governance-review flags;\
• policy-engine constraints;\
• test suites for Covenant-dependent operations;\
• canonical release manifests;\
• emergency-pause procedures for high-impact pathways.

In a mature Holochain-compatible implementation, sensitive operations may check whether a participant has a valid Covenant status before permitting access to:

• governance actions;\
• role-specific operations;\
• PRU-related workflows;\
• RAP-related workflows;\
• MRV submission or validation;\
• investor-facing data rooms;\
• token-mediated participation;\
• protected APIs;\
• institutional dashboards;\
• capital-readiness workflows;\
• other protected functions.

However, technical enforcement should be described with implementation discipline.

Prometheus should not claim that every enforcement mechanism is automatic until it is actually implemented, tested, audited, and governed.

**The correct principle is:**

where implemented, Covenant-dependent operations should be technically gated;

where not yet implemented, Covenant compliance should be governed through procedural, legal, institutional, and governance review.

This preserves credibility while keeping the constitutional architecture strong.

The Covenant is not complete merely because it is written.

It becomes operational when it is reflected in code, permissions, records, review pathways, and institutional behavior.

**6.4 Legal Integration for Institutional and Capital Participation**

For institutions, legal entities, investment funds, foundations, trusts, public agencies, companies, SPVs, DAOs, infrastructure providers, or other formal participants, the Access Covenant should be integrated through a two-layer structure.

**Digital layer**

An authorized representative may digitally acknowledge the Covenant through the Prometheus hApp or equivalent interface.

**The digital layer may create:**

• a signed record;\
• hash reference;\
• Covenant version reference;\
• timestamp;\
• access-status record;\
• representative identity reference;\
• role or authority reference;\
• technical permission state.

**Legal layer**

The entity should also execute a separate Access Agreement, participation agreement, governance agreement, fund-side letter, subscription document, operating agreement, partnership agreement, data-sharing agreement, service agreement, or other legally appropriate instrument that incorporates the Access Covenant by reference. The legal instrument should clarify:

• authority of the signer;\
• applicable jurisdiction;\
• participant category;\
• scope of permitted participation;\
• governance rights, if any;\
• financial rights, if any;\
• data access, if any;\
• confidentiality obligations;\
• Covenant obligations;\
• consequences of breach;\
• dispute-resolution pathway;\
• limitation, suspension, or termination conditions;\
• relationship between digital records and legal instruments;\
• relationship between participation and any token, credential, PRU, RAP, MRV, or investor-facing status.

This is especially important for capital-market actors.

No investor, fund, noteholder, token participant, philanthropic vehicle, family office, institutional counterparty, or public-sector partner should rely on the Access Covenant alone as a substitute for enforceable legal documentation.

The Covenant is constitutional infrastructure.

Legal instruments create enforceable rights and obligations in specific jurisdictions.

Both layers are needed.

The digital layer creates traceability.

The legal layer creates enforceability.

The constitutional layer defines meaning.

The governance layer preserves alignment.

**6.5 User Interface and Acceptance Flow**

The Prometheus interface should make Covenant acceptance deliberate, explicit, and reviewable.

**A valid acceptance flow may include:**

| **Step** | **Function** | **Control purpose** |
|----|----|----|
| **Covenant presentation** | Participant is shown the current canonical Covenant text. | Prevents hidden or implied acceptance. |
| **Version disclosure** | The applicable Covenant version and hash are displayed. | Preserves canonical traceability. |
| **Three Affirmations** | Each affirmation is presented separately and accepted actively. | Prevents passive consent. |
| **Scope disclosure** | Participant sees what access, rights, roles, limits, or restrictions apply. | Prevents misunderstanding of participation status. |
| **Boundary notice** | Participant is informed that the Covenant does not create PRU value, land rights, investor rights, or token entitlement by itself. | Prevents category collapse. |
| **Risk and review notice** | Participant understands that sensitive actions may require governance, MRV, legal, compliance, or technical review. | Preserves review discipline. |
| **Digital signature or authentication** | Participant confirms acceptance through appropriate identity or authorization mechanism. | Creates verifiable acceptance. |
| **Legal agreement, if applicable** | Legal entities execute additional legal documentation. | Creates jurisdiction-specific enforceability. |
| **Receipt and record** | Participant receives a receipt or record reference. | Supports auditability and future review. |

The interface should avoid manipulative design.

Participants should not be rushed into constitutional acceptance through dark patterns, default consent, hidden terms, emotional pressure, misleading access claims, scarcity tactics, or symbolic belonging pressure.

The Covenant must be entered consciously.

A system that protects dignity must make its threshold dignified.

**6.6 Periodic Reaffirmation and Renewal**

The Access Covenant should not be treated as a one-time ritual that becomes meaningless over time.

Participants may be required to periodically reaffirm the Covenant.

**Periodic reaffirmation serves several purposes:**

• maintaining constitutional awareness;\
• confirming continuing alignment;\
• updating participants to canonical text changes;\
• confirming authority of institutional representatives;\
• identifying dormant accounts;\
• reviewing scope of access;\
• preventing stale privileges;\
• refreshing governance hygiene;\
• preserving the link between participation and responsibility.

A mature implementation may require annual reaffirmation, or another interval defined by governance.

**High-impact participants may require more frequent review. Examples include:**

• capital allocators;\
• institutional partners;\
• governance-role holders;\
• MRV validators;\
• data-room users;\
• token-mediated governance participants;\
• infrastructure providers;\
• technical administrators;\
• public-facing representatives.

Failure to reaffirm within a defined grace period should not automatically become punitive unless the relevant governance and technical rules so provide.

**The default response should be proportionate:**

• reminder;\
• request for reaffirmation;\
• temporary limitation of certain rights;\
• restricted governance participation;\
• review-required status;\
• suspension of sensitive operations until reaffirmation;\
• escalation if combined with other risk indicators.

Reaffirmation preserves constitutional continuity without turning governance into a trapdoor.

The purpose is not punishment.

The purpose is coherence over time.

**6.7 Covenant Immutability and Version Discipline**

The Access Covenant is constitutionally protected.

Its substance must remain aligned with the foundational articles and the Eternity Clauses.

It cannot be amended, repealed, weakened, inverted, diluted, obscured, or bypassed through ordinary governance, token vote, investor demand, emergency procedure, legal wrapper, model output, autonomous agent action, community pressure, or operational convenience.

**However, implementation language may evolve. The Covenant may require:**

• clarifying language;\
• translation;\
• jurisdiction-specific legal adaptation;\
• accessibility improvements;\
• interface improvements;\
• technical implementation updates;\
• versioning;\
• canonical release management;\
• cryptographic anchoring;\
• audit trail;\
• legal review;\
• governance review;\
• public explanatory notes.

This distinction is essential.

The constitutional substance is protected.

The implementation form may evolve.

A translation may change words without changing meaning.

A jurisdictional adaptation may adjust legal form without weakening the Covenant.

A technical implementation may improve enforcement without altering constitutional substance.

An accessibility improvement may make the Covenant easier to understand without making it less binding.

Any material change must pass through constitutional review, versioned approval, canonical release discipline, and, where appropriate, legal review.

No change is valid if it weakens the Access Covenant’s function as a source-layer entry condition.

The Covenant may become clearer.

It may not become weaker.

It may become more accessible.

It may not become more capturable.

It may evolve in form.

It may not betray its source.

**6.8 Covenant Violation Status**

A violation or attempted bypass of the Access Covenant must be handled through a proportionate and reviewable pathway.

The purpose is to preserve constitutional integrity while avoiding arbitrary punishment.

Depending on severity, intent, evidence, repetition, harm, role, legal context, and applicable governance rules, the status may be:

| **Status** | **Meaning** |
|----|----|
| **Review required** | A potential issue requires governance, legal, technical, ecological, MRV, or constitutional review. |
| Warning / corrective action | Minor or inadvertent breach can be corrected through education, clarification, or corrective action. |
| **Restricted status** | Certain rights, roles, accesses, or operations are temporarily limited. |
| **Suspended status** | Sensitive access is paused pending review, corrective action, or decision. |
| **Non-admissible status** | The participant’s output, claim, or action cannot enter PRU, RAP, governance, token, investor-facing, or public-reporting pathways. |
| **Quarantined status** | The pathway is isolated due to serious risk, capture attempt, manipulation, legal exposure, ecological harm, or constitutional breach. |
| **Excluded status** | The participant is removed from defined forms of participation according to governance and legal procedures. |
| **External legal escalation** | Serious misconduct may require referral to competent legal, regulatory, fiduciary, or enforcement authorities. |

**Violation handling should follow core principles:**

• proportionality;\
• evidence;\
• reviewability;\
• right to respond where appropriate;\
• protection of the community field;\
• protection of the constitutional source layer;\
• protection of vulnerable participants;\
• protection of ecological and investor-facing integrity;\
• preservation of due process.

Prometheus may use automated monitoring, anomaly detection, routing, and review triggers where implemented. However, enforcement depends on the applicable governance, legal, MRV, technical, or constitutional review pathway. No automatic enforcement should be implied unless a reviewed technical and legal mechanism expressly exists.

This structure preserves the immune function of Prometheus without overclaiming automatic enforcement.

The system should be firm where the constitution is threatened and precise where proportionality is required.

The Covenant is a gate, but it is also a teacher.

It must protect the system without becoming arbitrary.

**6.9 Final Covenant Principle**

The Access Covenant exists to ensure that Prometheus remains open without becoming capturable.

It allows participation, but not extraction.

It allows governance, but not constitutional inversion.

It allows capital, but not sovereignty over life.

It allows intelligence, but not authority without evidence and review.

It allows community, but not coercive belonging.

It allows pluralism, but not harm disguised as culture.

It allows tokens, where appropriate, but not semantic confusion between access, value, governance, ownership, or investor rights.

It allows evolution, but not drift.

The Covenant is therefore the threshold guardian of Prometheus.

It does not ask participants to worship the system.

It asks them to recognize the living foundation that the system exists to protect.

No actor is above the Covenant.

No token is above the Covenant.

No capital structure is above the Covenant.

No AI model is above the Covenant.

No governance majority is above the Covenant.

No community status is above the Covenant.

No institutional power is above the Covenant.

The Covenant is the gate through which participation becomes legitimate.

**It is the first promise of Prometheus:**

to remain open to life,\
disciplined by evidence,\
grounded in dignity,\
protected by governance,\
and faithful to the living field from which all value arises.

**7. Eligibility Criteria by Entity Type**

The Access Covenant applies to every participant.

However, different entity types create different responsibilities, risk profiles, evidence requirements, legal considerations, and governance implications.

For this reason, Prometheus defines eligibility criteria by entity type.

These criteria do not exist to create exclusionary gatekeeping.

They exist to ensure that each participant enters the ecosystem through an appropriate pathway, with a clear scope of access, a defined responsibility profile, and a reviewable relationship to the constitutional source layer.

Eligibility is therefore not a social rank.

It is a governed participation status.

A participant may be welcomed into Prometheus only when its role, rights, responsibilities, evidence obligations, governance boundaries, and risk exposure are sufficiently clear.

**Eligibility should be assessed through a combination of:**

• Access Covenant acceptance;\
• identity or authority verification, where appropriate;\
• legal documentation, where relevant;\
• governance review;\
• technical onboarding;\
• evidence of alignment;\
• risk classification;\
• scope definition;\
• periodic review.

Verification may use digital records, signed source-chain entries, hash references, legal documents, governance records, off-chain documentation, public registries, or other appropriate evidence depending on implementation stage and participant type.

Prometheus should not claim that every eligibility condition is automatically enforced on-chain unless the relevant technical infrastructure has been implemented, tested, audited, and governed.

**The correct principle is:**

eligibility must be reviewable, proportionate, source-linked, and constitutionally aligned.

**7.1 Individuals**

Individuals may participate as learners, stewards, contributors, governance participants, technical contributors, community members, researchers, operators, mentors, role holders, or other approved participants.

**Minimum criteria may include:**

• legal capacity or age of majority in the relevant jurisdiction, where legally required;\
• acknowledgment of the Access Covenant;\
• completion of an orientation module or equivalent onboarding process;\
• acceptance of the relevant role boundaries;\
• understanding that participation does not create financial entitlement, land rights, PRU value, investor rights, or governance authority beyond the applicable scope;\
• no unresolved exclusion, suspension, serious Covenant breach, or governance restriction preventing participation under current rules.

Individual onboarding should be educational before it is restrictive.

The objective is to help participants understand Prometheus, not to create unnecessary barriers.

Where a participant is a minor, vulnerable person, student, or dependent participant, participation should follow appropriate safeguarding, consent, parental or guardian authorization, educational, privacy, and legal requirements.

Prometheus must protect the dignity of every individual participant.

A person enters the ecosystem as a human being, not as a wallet, score, data point, productivity unit, or governance weight.

**7.2 Communities, Unincorporated Groups, Bioregional Networks, and Cooperatives**

Communities may participate when they can demonstrate a meaningful collective relationship to regeneration, stewardship, territory, education, food systems, ecological restoration, cultural continuity, mutual aid, or governance learning.

**Minimum criteria may include:**

• identification of the community or group;\
• at least one designated contact or representative;\
• a description of purpose, territory, membership, or shared activity;\
• acknowledgment of the Access Covenant by representatives and, where appropriate, by individual members;\
• basic governance description;\
• internal dispute-resolution pathway;\
• commitment to human dignity, voluntary participation, non-coercion, and regenerative alignment;\
• commitment to avoid using community status as a shield for extraction, exclusion, domination, or governance capture.

Sociocratic, consent-based, cooperative, customary, indigenous, bioregional, religious, spiritual, civic, educational, or other governance forms may be recognized when compatible with the Access Covenant and applicable law.

Prometheus should respect local governance diversity while preserving constitutional boundaries.

Community participation does not create automatic ownership, token entitlement, land rights, PRU claims, investor rights, or veto power unless separately and legally structured.

A community may remain itself while becoming interoperable with Prometheus.

That is the purpose of sovereign alignment.

**7.3 Companies and Business Entities**

Companies and business entities may participate when their activities, governance, products, services, or capital deployment remain compatible with the Prometheus constitutional architecture.

**Eligibility review may include:**

• legal identity and registration documentation;\
• authority of the representative entering the Access Covenant;\
• business model review;\
• ownership or control disclosure where relevant;\
• conflict-of-interest disclosure;\
• governance and compliance contact;\
• commitment to avoid extractive practices inconsistent with the Access Covenant;\
• commitment to avoid misleading environmental, regenerative, financial, tokenomic, or health-related claims;\
• agreement that participation does not authorize PRU value, investor claims, token issuance, securities offering, or public impact claims without evidence and review.

Where technically implemented, documents may be hashed, referenced, or linked to source-chain records for traceability.

However, document hashing is an integrity-support mechanism.

It is not a substitute for legal review, governance review, or substantive alignment.

A company may be eligible for limited participation before full institutional participation, depending on scope, risk profile, evidence quality, legal clarity, and governance maturity.

**Prometheus may therefore distinguish between:**

• observer participation;\
• pilot-specific participation;\
• service-provider participation;\
• operational participation;\
• governance participation;\
• capital-facing participation;\
• institutional participation.

Each category should be explicitly scoped.

**7.4 Foundations, Trusts, Philanthropic Entities, and Mission-Aligned Institutions**

Foundations, trusts, philanthropic entities, and mission-aligned institutions may support research, pilots, ecological restoration, education, community programs, MRV development, public-interest deployment, food-system resilience, public-health research, or catalytic capital.

**Eligibility review may include:**

• legal identity and governing documents;\
• authority of representative;\
• mission and purpose alignment;\
• grantmaking or investment-policy alignment where relevant;\
• conflict-of-interest disclosure;\
• commitment to avoid funding activities that violate the Access Covenant;\
• respect for local autonomy and community consent;\
• reporting expectations appropriate to the participant’s role;\
• periodic review when the relationship is material or long-term.

Philanthropic participation should not become hidden control.

Mission alignment does not exempt any entity from the Access Covenant.

Trust or foundation structures may support Prometheus only when they preserve the separation between mission, governance, legal rights, financial rights, ecological evidence, and community sovereignty.

A philanthropic actor may fund regeneration.

It may not own the meaning of regeneration.

**7.5 Investment Funds, Asset Managers, Family Offices, and Capital Allocators**

Capital allocators require enhanced review because capital can support regeneration, but it can also distort, accelerate, capture, or prematurely financialize the system.

**Eligibility review may include:**

• legal identity and regulatory status;\
• authority of representative;\
• investment strategy;\
• mandate alignment;\
• source-of-funds review where appropriate;\
• conflict-of-interest disclosure;\
• relevant portfolio or exposure disclosure, subject to confidentiality;\
• time horizon;\
• liquidity expectations;\
• exit strategy;\
• leverage profile;\
• investor disclosure discipline;\
• acceptance that PRU, RAP, tokenization, and investor-facing claims require evidence, legal structure, and compliance review.

Prometheus should avoid universal fixed thresholds such as a mandatory percentage of aligned invested capital unless such thresholds are formally adopted by governance for a specific program.

Instead, Prometheus should use a risk-based alignment review.

**A participant’s capital strategy may be classified as follows:**

| **Alignment status** | **Meaning** | **Access implication** |
|----|----|----|
| **Aligned** | Strategy is substantially compatible with regenerative infrastructure, evidence discipline, and constitutional constraints. | Eligible for ordinary capital-participation review. |
| **Conditionally aligned** | Strategy may become compatible if specific exclusions, disclosures, limitations, or review conditions are accepted. | Eligible only with conditions. |
| **Transitional** | Entity is moving from conventional or mixed exposure toward regenerative alignment. | Eligible for limited, observed, or pilot-specific participation. |
| **High-risk** | Strategy includes leverage, extraction, greenwashing, speculative tokenization, short-term exit pressure, or conflict with the Covenant. | Requires enhanced review, restriction, or rejection. |
| **Incompatible** | Strategy materially violates foundational articles, Eternity Clauses, or Access Covenant. | Not eligible unless strategy changes. |

Capital allocators may be subject to enhanced scrutiny, additional documentation, staged access, reporting requirements, observation periods, or legal review.

Any observation period should be defined by governance according to risk, not imposed as a universal fixed duration.

No capital allocator may use Prometheus participation to imply investment approval, PRU entitlement, token rights, public offering, guaranteed return, or preferential access to future financial instruments.

Capital may enter Prometheus only as disciplined support for verified regeneration.

It may not become sovereignty over life.

**7.6 Governments and Public Agencies**

Governments and public agencies may participate in public-interest, territorial, ecological, educational, procurement, research, food-system, health, climate-adaptation, resilience, or infrastructure roles.

**Eligibility review may include:**

• legal authority to participate;\
• identification of responsible office or representative;\
• scope of participation;\
• public-interest alignment;\
• data-governance requirements;\
• procurement or funding rules;\
• respect for community participation and local autonomy;\
• avoidance of coercive deployment;\
• commitment to transparency, proportionality, and due process;\
• legal review of any conflict between public-law obligations and Prometheus governance.

The relationship with public agencies must be handled carefully.

Prometheus cannot erase sovereign, statutory, administrative, procurement, or public-law obligations by private covenant language.

A public agency may be bound by the Access Covenant within the scope of its participation, but the consequences of breach must be handled through legally appropriate instruments, agreements, remedies, governance pathways, and applicable law.

**The correct principle is not that sovereign immunity “does not exist.”**

**The correct principle is:**

public authority does not grant permission to violate the Prometheus constitutional source layer within the Prometheus ecosystem.

Where legal doctrines limit remedies, Prometheus may still restrict ecosystem access, classify actions as non-admissible, refuse participation pathways, require review, suspend specific interfaces, or terminate participation according to the applicable agreement and law.

Prometheus respects public authority. It also preserves its own constitutional boundary.

**7.7 Token Communities, DAOs, Digital Networks, and Token Projects**

Token communities, DAOs, digital networks, and token projects may participate only if they respect the strict separation between governance, participation, access, token representation, financial rights, PRU value, land rights, investor exposure, and ecological verification.

**Eligibility review may include:**

• public governance process or equivalent governance disclosure;\
• tokenomics review where relevant;\
• legal classification review;\
• rights and claims associated with the token;\
• treasury policy;\
• voting mechanism;\
• concentration and whale-risk analysis;\
• bridge or interoperability risks;\
• compliance with the Access Covenant;\
• avoidance of false claims about regeneration, PRU, land, yield, ownership, liquidity, or investor entitlement.

Token projects must not be admitted merely because they use regenerative language.

They must demonstrate that their token logic does not convert narrative into value, governance into entitlement, or speculation into ecological performance.

**Potential access statuses may include:**

| **Status** | **Meaning** |
|----|----|
| **Observer status** | May learn, participate in non-sensitive forums, or explore compatibility. |
| Research / sandbox status | May test interoperability under restricted conditions. |
| **Limited participation** | May access specific functions under defined boundaries. |
| **Governance participation** | May participate in governance only within approved scope and legal boundaries. |
| Rejected / incompatible | Not eligible due to tokenomic, legal, governance, ecological, or constitutional conflict. |

Any observation period should be risk-based and governance-defined.

A fixed six-month minimum may be used as a policy option, but it should not be presented as a universal constitutional requirement unless formally adopted.

Where a token ecosystem has its own governance, treasury, token logic, role structure, and community identity, Prometheus should interact through sovereign alignment and interoperability, not absorption.

**7.8 Digital Agents, AI Systems, Infrastructure Providers, and Technical Operators**

Digital agents, AI systems, infrastructure providers, model operators, connector providers, data-service providers, and technical maintainers may participate only through strict role boundaries.

**Eligibility review may include:**

• operator identity;\
• technical function;\
• access scope;\
• data permissions;\
• model or system description where relevant;\
• security posture;\
• auditability;\
• logging requirements;\
• incident-response procedure;\
• model-risk review where applicable;\
• compliance with non-bypassability constraints;\
• prohibition on autonomous execution without authorization.

No AI system, autonomous agent, dashboard, connector, API, or technical provider may independently authorize:

• PRU value;\
• RAP inclusion;\
• investor-facing claims;\
• token issuance;\
• ecological interventions;\
• legal rights;\
• governance decisions;\
• capital deployment.

Technical systems may assist.

They may observe, route, calculate, compare, flag, recommend, and explain.

They may not become constitutional authority.

Prometheus uses intelligence.

It does not surrender sovereignty to intelligence.

**7.9 Auditors, Verifiers, Researchers, and Methodology Contributors**

Auditors, verifiers, researchers, universities, laboratories, and methodology contributors may participate when their role strengthens evidence quality, verification discipline, scientific integrity, or methodology development.

**Eligibility review may include:**

• professional identity or institutional affiliation;\
• relevant expertise;\
• conflict-of-interest disclosure;\
• methodology transparency;\
• data-access boundaries;\
• confidentiality obligations;\
• publication or reporting rules;\
• independence requirements where relevant;\
• evidence-handling procedures;\
• agreement not to overstate findings beyond data quality and methodology.

Researchers may explore frontier hypotheses.

Auditors and verifiers must preserve institutional credibility.

A research hypothesis is not PRU value.

A laboratory result is not automatically admissible value.

A model output is not verification unless methodology and review conditions are satisfied.

Scientific curiosity is welcome.

Financial admissibility requires evidence discipline.

**7.10 Religious, Spiritual, Cultural, and Ancestral Communities**

Prometheus may collaborate with religious, spiritual, cultural, and ancestral communities when such collaboration supports dignity, stewardship, ecological care, education, community resilience, food systems, land restoration, intergenerational continuity, or human coherence.

**Eligibility review may include:**

• identification of the community or institution;\
• representative authority where relevant;\
• scope of collaboration;\
• internal governance or leadership structure;\
• safeguarding and consent policies where needed;\
• respect for voluntary participation and freedom of conscience;\
• clear distinction between spiritual meaning and Prometheus evidence;\
• clear distinction between ritual, testimony, observation, and verified indicators;\
• commitment not to use Prometheus participation for coercion, exclusion, manipulation, or financial overclaiming.

Prometheus recognizes that communities may speak different languages of meaning.

Some may speak of Creation.

Some may speak of Nature.

Some may speak of Earth.

Some may speak of God, Spirit, ancestry, science, ecology, or interdependence.

Prometheus does not impose one sacred language.

It protects the living foundation behind many languages.

A spiritual or cultural community may contribute meaning, care, ritual, memory, stewardship, and human coherence.

However, spiritual or cultural legitimacy does not automatically create MRV evidence, PRU value, governance authority, investor rights, or legal entitlement.

This boundary protects both the community and Prometheus.

Meaning is honored.

Evidence is verified.

**7.11 General Eligibility Outcomes**

After review, an entity may receive one of several eligibility outcomes.

| **Eligibility outcome** | **Meaning** |
|----|----|
| Eligible | Participant may enter within the approved scope. |
| Eligible with conditions | Participant may enter subject to defined limitations, disclosures, review, or controls. |
| Limited / sandbox access | Participant may test compatibility without full participation rights. |
| Observer status | Participant may learn or engage without governance, capital, or operational authority. |
| Review required | Additional evidence, legal review, governance review, or documentation is required. |
| Restricted | Participant may access only narrow functions due to risk profile. |
| Suspended | Existing participation is paused pending review or corrective action. |
| Rejected | Participant is not eligible under current facts or constitutional constraints. |
| Quarantined | Participant or pathway presents serious capture, legal, ecological, financial, tokenomic, or governance risk. |

Eligibility is not a permanent badge. It is a governed status.

It may evolve as evidence, conduct, legal status, governance maturity, risk profile, Covenant compliance, community-field health, or technical implementation changes.

**7.12 Eligibility Review Matrix**

Prometheus may use an eligibility review matrix to make onboarding more transparent.

| **Review dimension** | **Key question** |
|----|----|
| Identity / authority | Who is entering, and who has authority to bind the entity? |
| Purpose alignment | Why does the participant want to enter Prometheus? |
| Constitutional compatibility | Does participation align with foundational articles, Eternity Clauses, and the Access Covenant? |
| Role clarity | What function will the participant perform? |
| Evidence responsibility | Will the participant generate, handle, verify, or rely on evidence? |
| Governance scope | What decisions, if any, can the participant influence? |
| Financial boundary | Does participation create or imply financial rights? |
| Token boundary | Is any token involved, and what does it represent or not represent? |
| Legal boundary | Does the relationship require legal documentation or jurisdictional review? |
| Risk classification | What risks does this participant introduce or reduce? |
| Review cycle | When should eligibility be reviewed again? |

This matrix helps Prometheus remain open without becoming vague.

It gives the onboarding process a spine.

**7.13 Final Eligibility Principle**

Eligibility exists to protect openness from capture.

Prometheus should be open to persons, communities, institutions, capital, technology, research, governance innovation, cultural wisdom, spiritual traditions, and future human coordination forms.

But openness without constitutional discipline becomes vulnerability.

**The eligibility layer therefore asks one question before all others:**

can this participant enter without weakening the living foundation, evidence discipline, governance integrity, legal clarity, human dignity, or regenerative fidelity of Prometheus?

If yes, access may be granted within the correct scope.

If not, access must be conditioned, limited, deferred, or refused.

Participation is welcome.

Contribution is welcome.

Difference is welcome.

Plurality is welcome.

Capture is not.

**8. Procedures for Gaining Access**

Access to Prometheus is not granted through assumption, proximity, enthusiasm, capital, technical capacity, community status, token exposure, or institutional prestige.

Access is granted through a reviewable procedure. The purpose of the access procedure is to translate the Access Covenant and the eligibility criteria into a clear operational pathway.

**The procedure must remain:**

• proportional;\
• documented;\
• reviewable;\
• role-specific;\
• evidence-aware;\
• legally appropriate;\
• technically grounded where implemented;\
• respectful of human dignity;\
• aligned with the constitutional source layer.

The access procedure is not designed to create unnecessary bureaucracy.

It is designed to prevent ambiguity.

**A participant should know:**

• what they are applying for;\
• which role or access scope is being requested;\
• which documents or declarations are required;\
• which governance body or process reviews the request;\
• which risks are being evaluated;\
• which rights are granted;\
• which rights are not granted;\
• which renewal or review duties apply;\
• which consequences may follow from breach, misrepresentation, or drift.

Prometheus must remain open enough to welcome real contribution and disciplined enough to protect the living field from capture. The access procedure is the bridge between openness and institutional integrity.

**8.1 Application**

An application for access should identify the participant, the requested access scope, the intended role, and the relevant eligibility pathway.

Depending on the participant type, the application may be submitted through a Prometheus hApp interface, governance portal, legal intake process, institutional onboarding workflow, written application, signed agreement, or other approved pathway.

Prometheus should not assume that all access procedures are already fully automated.

Where digital infrastructure is implemented, the application may generate a signed digital record, source-chain entry, hash reference, or access-status record.

Where digital infrastructure is not yet implemented, the application may be processed through documented governance, legal, operational, or institutional procedures.

**The application may include:**

• participant type;\
• basic identifying information;\
• requested access scope;\
• intended role or function;\
• contact or representative details;\
• authority of representative, where applicable;\
• Access Covenant acknowledgment;\
• eligibility documentation required under Section 7;\
• legal documentation, where relevant;\
• governance disclosures, where relevant;\
• token, treasury, or financial-rights disclosures, where relevant;\
• data-access or technical-access requests, where relevant;\
• conflict-of-interest disclosures;\
• risk disclosures;\
• any other information reasonably required by the reviewing body.

**The application must distinguish between:**

• ordinary participation;\
• community participation;\
• governance participation;\
• operational participation;\
• technical participation;\
• MRV participation;\
• capital-facing participation;\
• institutional participation;\
• legal or financial participation.

This distinction is essential.

A participant may be eligible for one form of access and not another.

A community may be eligible for educational participation but not PRU-related claims.

A technical provider may be eligible for infrastructure support but not governance authority.

A capital allocator may be eligible for exploratory dialogue but not investor-facing participation.

A token community may be eligible for sandbox interoperability but not Prometheus governance rights. Access must be scoped before it is granted.

**8.2 Intake and Completeness Review**

Before substantive review, the application should undergo an intake and completeness check.

The purpose of this step is to determine whether the application contains enough information to be evaluated.

**The intake process may verify:**

• identity or entity information;\
• representative authority;\
• requested access scope;\
• required documentation;\
• Access Covenant acknowledgment;\
• eligibility pathway;\
• obvious conflicts;\
• missing information;\
• need for legal review;\
• need for technical review;\
• need for MRV review;\
• need for community consultation;\
• need for enhanced risk classification.

If the application is incomplete, the reviewing body should request additional information.

An incomplete application should not be treated as denial unless the participant refuses to provide required information, provides materially false information, or presents a clear constitutional, legal, ecological, financial, technical, or governance risk.

**The correct outcome for an incomplete but potentially valid application is:**

review pending. This preserves openness without weakening diligence.

**8.3 Substantive Review**

After intake, the application enters substantive review.

The reviewing body may be a Stewardship Circle, onboarding circle, governance circle, protocol-stewardship body, institutional review body, legal reviewer, technical reviewer, MRV reviewer, or other approved process depending on the access type.

**The review should evaluate:**

• constitutional compatibility;\
• Access Covenant alignment;\
• eligibility under Section 7;\
• role clarity;\
• risk profile;\
• legal requirements;\
• governance implications;\
• technical implications;\
• MRV implications;\
• financial or tokenomic implications;\
• community-field implications;\
• conflict-of-interest issues;\
• public-communication risks;\
• potential for capture, drift, overclaiming, or category collapse.

The review should not rely on one signal alone.

A strong application may require convergence across documents, declarations, governance records, public information, operational evidence, legal instruments, technical review, community consultation, or institutional due diligence.

**Where appropriate, the reviewing body may classify the application according to risk:**

| **Risk class** | **Meaning** | **Review intensity** |
|----|----|----|
| Low risk | Ordinary participation with limited access and no sensitive claims. | Standard review. |
| Moderate risk | Participation affects community, governance, operations, data, or public representation. | Enhanced review. |
| High risk | Participation affects capital, MRV, PRU, RAP, tokenization, institutional claims, legal rights, or sensitive infrastructure. | Formal review with legal, governance, technical, or MRV input. |
| Critical risk | Participation may affect constitutional integrity, investor-facing claims, protected infrastructure, major public commitments, or systemic trust. | Constitutional, legal, and multi-layer review required. |

Risk classification does not imply hostility.

It determines the level of care required.

Prometheus should be friendly at the door and forensic at the boundary.

**8.4 Community and Affected-Party Consultation**

Where an application affects an existing community, territory, bioregion, stakeholder group, Tribe, DAO, token community, land base, data community, or institutional partner, consultation may be required.

**Consultation may include:**

• affected community representatives;\
• local stewards;\
• relevant governance circles;\
• technical maintainers;\
• MRV validators;\
• legal counterparties;\
• institutional partners;\
• community-interface roles;\
• conflict or integrity roles;\
• bioregional representatives.

Consultation is not automatic veto.

It is field intelligence.

The purpose is to identify context, risk, trust conditions, historical tensions, operational constraints, local knowledge, and possible hidden impacts.

**Prometheus should distinguish:**

• consultation;\
• consent;\
• advice;\
• approval;\
• legal authorization;\
• governance decision;\
• constitutional review.

These are not the same thing.

A community may provide context without holding formal decision authority.

A legal entity may authorize an agreement without creating community legitimacy.

A governance circle may approve a scope while still requiring MRV or legal review.

The procedure must keep these layers legible.

**8.5 Decision Outcomes**

After review, the reviewing body may issue one of several outcomes.

| **Decision outcome** | **Meaning** |
|----|----|
| **Approved** | Access is granted within the defined scope. |
| **Approved with conditions** | Access is granted subject to limitations, disclosures, review duties, corrective action, time limits, or monitoring. |
| Limited / sandbox access | Access is granted only for testing, observation, research, learning, or restricted interoperability. |
| **Observer status** | Participant may learn or engage without operational, governance, capital, or technical authority. |
| **Request for additional information** | Application is paused until missing information is provided. |
| **Manual review required** | Application requires higher-level governance, legal, technical, MRV, or constitutional review. |
| **Deferred** | Application may be reconsidered later after specific conditions change. |
| **Restricted** | Access is narrower than requested due to risk profile. |
| **Rejected** | Access is denied under current facts or constitutional constraints. |
| **Quarantined** | The pathway presents serious capture, legal, ecological, financial, tokenomic, technical, or governance risk and must be isolated. |

Every material decision should be documented.

**Documentation should include:**

• participant identity or entity reference;\
• requested scope;\
• review pathway;\
• decision outcome;\
• granted scope;\
• limitations or conditions;\
• review date;\
• responsible reviewing body;\
• reasoned explanation where appropriate;\
• legal or technical references where relevant;\
• appeal or reconsideration pathway, where applicable.

The purpose of documentation is not bureaucracy.

It is institutional memory.

A system without memory becomes capturable by charisma, urgency, capital, or confusion.

**8.6 Access Grant and Scope Definition**

When access is approved, the scope of access must be defined.

**Access may include one or more of the following:**

• learning access;\
• community access;\
• governance access;\
• operational access;\
• technical access;\
• MRV access;\
• data-room access;\
• research access;\
• service-provider access;\
• capital-facing access;\
• institutional access;\
• sandbox access;\
• pilot-specific access.

**Each access grant should specify:**

• what the participant may do;\
• what the participant may not do;\
• which systems or spaces they may access;\
• which data they may access;\
• which decisions they may influence;\
• which claims they may make publicly;\
• whether legal documentation is required;\
• whether renewal is required;\
• whether monitoring or reporting applies;\
• whether access is time-bounded;\
• whether access depends on continued Covenant compliance.

No participant should receive ambiguous access.

Ambiguous access becomes future conflict.

Prometheus should therefore make the access grant precise enough that the participant, the community, governance reviewers, auditors, and future institutional counterparties can understand what was actually authorized.

**8.7 Record Architecture**

Approved access should generate an appropriate record.

The record may be digital, legal, governance-based, operational, or combined depending on implementation stage and access type.

**A mature record may include:**

• participant identity or entity reference;\
• Access Covenant version;\
• eligibility category;\
• requested access scope;\
• approved access scope;\
• conditions or restrictions;\
• timestamp;\
• reviewer or reviewing body;\
• legal agreement reference, where applicable;\
• hash reference, where applicable;\
• source-chain entry, where implemented;\
• governance decision reference;\
• renewal date;\
• suspension or revocation status, if any.

Where Prometheus uses Holochain-compatible infrastructure, access records may be represented through signed entries, source-chain records, DHT-published references, or validation-controlled access states.

However, the document should preserve implementation discipline.

A record may support traceability.

It does not by itself create financial rights, PRU value, land rights, investor rights, token entitlement, or governance authority beyond the scope explicitly granted.

The access record is evidence of status.

It is not a blank cheque from the gods of distributed systems.

**8.8 Renewal and Reaffirmation**

Access should be periodically reviewed.

The review frequency should depend on role, risk, access scope, legal context, and implementation maturity.

**Possible renewal triggers include:**

• annual reaffirmation;\
• expiration of access term;\
• change of participant role;\
• change of entity structure;\
• change of control;\
• change of tokenomics;\
• change of investment strategy;\
• change of legal status;\
• change of MRV role;\
• change of technical permissions;\
• breach or suspected breach;\
• material governance concern;\
• material ecological, financial, technical, or reputational risk.

**Reaffirmation may include:**

• renewed Access Covenant acknowledgment;\
• confirmation of current role;\
• confirmation of current representative authority;\
• updated documentation;\
• conflict-of-interest update;\
• confirmation of continued eligibility;\
• review of conditions or restrictions;\
• update of access scope.

Failure to renew should be handled proportionately.

**Possible responses include:**

• reminder;\
• grace period;\
• review-required status;\
• temporary limitation of sensitive access;\
• suspension of governance or technical functions;\
• suspension of capital-facing privileges;\
• termination of access where appropriate.

Prometheus should avoid automatic punitive escalation unless formally defined, technically implemented, legally appropriate, and governance-approved.

The purpose of renewal is continuity of coherence, not bureaucratic ambush.

**8.9 Material Changes**

A participant must disclose material changes that may affect eligibility, risk, access scope, or Covenant alignment. Material changes may include:

• change of legal identity;\
• change of ownership or control;\
• change of representative authority;\
• change of governance structure;\
• change of tokenomics;\
• change of treasury policy;\
• change of investment strategy;\
• change of regulatory status;\
• change of data-processing practices;\
• change of technical infrastructure;\
• change of MRV methodology or role;\
• change of public claims;\
• change of business model;\
• change of land-access arrangement;\
• change of community governance;\
• emergence of conflict of interest;\
• litigation, enforcement, insolvency, or serious reputational event.

Material changes do not automatically terminate access.

**They trigger review. The review may result in:**

• no change;\
• updated access scope;\
• additional documentation;\
• enhanced monitoring;\
• temporary restriction;\
• reclassification of risk;\
• legal review;\
• governance review;\
• suspension;\
• termination.

The principle is adaptive governance. Prometheus should respond to reality as it changes.

**8.10 Ongoing Compliance and Monitoring**

Participation in Prometheus is not a one-time event.

It is a continuing relationship.

**Ongoing compliance may include:**

• Covenant compliance;\
• role compliance;\
• reporting obligations;\
• technical-access discipline;\
• MRV integrity;\
• governance participation rules;\
• legal agreement compliance;\
• public-communication discipline;\
• data-protection compliance;\
• conflict-of-interest updates;\
• periodic review.

Monitoring may be human, procedural, legal, technical, governance-based, or automated where implementation allows.

Prometheus must not overclaim automatic enforcement.

Where automated alerts, validation rules, warrant logic, reputation signals, anomaly detection, or technical controls are implemented, they should support review.

They should not replace due process where human, legal, ecological, financial, or constitutional interpretation is required.

The system may flag.

The system may restrict.

The system may pause.

The system may escalate.

But high-impact consequences should remain reviewable according to the applicable governance and legal pathway.

This is essential for institutional credibility.

Automatic punishment is not governance.

It is machinery wearing a judge’s wig.

Prometheus must be better than that.

**8.11 Appeal, Reconsideration, and Review**

Where access is denied, restricted, suspended, or materially conditioned, the participant may be entitled to appeal or reconsideration depending on the nature of the decision, access scope, legal context, and governance rules.

**An appeal pathway may include:**

• reasoned request for reconsideration;\
• submission of additional evidence;\
• review by a higher or separate circle;\
• legal review, where relevant;\
• technical review, where relevant;\
• MRV review, where relevant;\
• integrity review, where relevant;\
• constitutional review for source-layer questions.

Appeals should not become a mechanism to pressure the system into lowering constitutional standards.

**The reviewing body should distinguish between:**

• factual error;\
• missing information;\
• misunderstanding of access scope;\
• changed circumstances;\
• governance disagreement;\
• legal dispute;\
• constitutional incompatibility.

A participant may correct facts.

A participant may provide missing evidence.

A participant may narrow the requested scope.

A participant may enter through sandbox access.

But no appeal may override the foundational articles, Eternity Clauses, Access Covenant, evidence-before-value discipline, PRU/MRV boundaries, or source-layer non-bypassability.

The highest appeal is not power.

The highest appeal is fidelity.

**8.12 Emergency Limitation of Access**

In urgent situations, Prometheus may temporarily limit access before full review is complete.

**Emergency limitation may be appropriate where there is credible risk of:**

• ecological harm;\
• data manipulation;\
• MRV falsification;\
• investor-facing misrepresentation;\
• tokenomic confusion;\
• technical compromise;\
• security breach;\
• governance capture;\
• coercion or exploitation;\
• serious Covenant violation;\
• legal or regulatory exposure;\
• reputational harm to the ecosystem;\
• constitutional bypass.

**Emergency limitation should be:**

• temporary;\
• documented;\
• proportionate;\
• reviewable;\
• limited to the affected function where possible;\
• escalated to the proper reviewing body.

Emergency action should not become a shortcut for ordinary governance.

It is an immune response, not a permanent constitution.

After emergency limitation, the system should proceed to review, corrective action, suspension, restoration, restriction, or termination according to the applicable process.

**8.13 Final Access Procedure Principle**

The access procedure exists to ensure that Prometheus remains both open and coherent.

It should welcome contribution. It should respect pluralism.

It should make space for communities, capital, technology, science, culture, spirituality, governance innovation, and future forms of human coordination.

But it should never confuse welcome with waiver.

Access does not waive the Covenant.

Participation does not waive evidence.

Community status does not waive dignity.

Capital does not waive law.

Technology does not waive review.

AI does not waive authorization.

Tokens do not waive legal structure.

Governance does not waive the source layer.

**The final principle is therefore:**

access is not possession;\
participation is not entitlement;\
approval is not immunity;\
status is not sovereignty;\
contribution is not capture;\
and openness is protected by disciplined boundaries.

Prometheus becomes trustworthy because it does not close the door to the world.

It builds a threshold worthy of what may enter.

**9. Covenant Violation, corrective action, and Consequence Architecture**

The Access Covenant must be enforceable enough to protect Prometheus and proportionate enough to preserve justice.

A Covenant violation is not merely a procedural error.

It is a disturbance in the relationship between participant, community, evidence, governance, and the constitutional source layer.

For this reason, Prometheus treats violations through a graduated architecture of review, corrective action, limitation, suspension, exclusion, and external escalation where necessary.

The purpose is not punishment for its own sake. The purpose is to protect the living field, preserve evidence integrity, maintain trust, prevent capture, and restore coherence wherever restoration remains possible. Prometheus therefore adopts a consequence architecture based on:

• evidence;\
• proportionality;\
• due process;\
• right to respond where appropriate;\
• corrective action before exclusion where possible;\
• protection of vulnerable participants;\
• protection of ecological and investor-facing integrity;\
• protection of the constitutional source layer;\
• legal and governance review for high-impact cases;\
• technical enforcement only where implemented, tested, and governed.

The system must be firm without becoming arbitrary.

It must be protective without becoming punitive by default.

It must be capable of exclusion without turning exclusion into its first language.

A regenerative constitution must know how to say no. But it must also know how to repair.

**9.1 Definition of Covenant Violation**

A Covenant violation occurs when a participant, entity, role-holder, community, technical operator, capital allocator, digital agent, or institutional actor acts in a manner materially inconsistent with the Access Covenant, foundational articles, Eternity Clauses, source-layer constraints, evidence-before-value discipline, or approved participation scope. A violation may occur through:

• action;\
• omission;\
• misrepresentation;\
• concealment;\
• negligence;\
• repeated non-compliance;\
• attempted bypass;\
• coercion;\
• data manipulation;\
• governance manipulation;\
• tokenomic confusion;\
• investor-facing overclaiming;\
• ecological harm;\
• misuse of community status;\
• misuse of capital influence;\
• misuse of technical access;\
• misuse of AI or automated systems;\
• public communication that materially misrepresents Prometheus status, rights, evidence, PRU, RAP, MRV, TRBK, tokenization, financial entitlement, or legal authority.

Not every error is a violation.

Not every disagreement is a violation.

Not every failed proposal is a violation.

Not every weak signal is misconduct.

**Prometheus must distinguish between:**

• mistake;\
• misunderstanding;\
• immaturity;\
• negligence;\
• breach;\
• manipulation;\
• capture attempt;\
• constitutional violation.

This distinction protects fairness. It also protects the system from both naivety and overreaction.

**9.2 Violation Review Pathway**

A potential violation should pass through a review pathway before significant consequences are imposed, except where emergency limitation is required to prevent serious harm.

**The review pathway may include:**

1.  Signal or report\
    A concern, anomaly, complaint, evidence gap, technical flag, governance concern, community concern, audit finding, or legal notice is identified.

2.  Initial classification\
    The concern is classified by severity, domain, urgency, affected parties, and possible constitutional relevance.

3.  Evidence collection\
    Relevant documents, records, logs, communications, MRV evidence, technical events, governance records, legal instruments, or witness statements are gathered.

4.  Participant response\
    Where appropriate, the participant is given an opportunity to respond, clarify, correct, or provide missing information.

5.  Review body assessment\
    The appropriate governance, legal, MRV, technical, integrity, or constitutional review body assesses the matter.

6.  Consequence determination\
    A proportionate response is selected based on severity, intent, repetition, harm, evidence, legal context, and willingness to remediate.

7.  Documentation\
    The decision and reasoning are recorded.

8.  Correction or enforcement\
    Corrective, restrictive, restorative, suspension, exclusion, or escalation measures are applied.

9.  Appeal or reconsideration\
    Where applicable, the participant may seek review through the defined pathway.

10. Closure or monitoring\
    The case is closed, monitored, or escalated depending on residual risk.

This pathway ensures that Prometheus does not confuse automated detection with judgment.

A signal may trigger review. It does not automatically become guilt.

**9.3 Severity Classification**

Prometheus may classify Covenant violations by severity.

**Severity should be determined by considering:**

• intent;\
• harm;\
• repetition;\
• role of the participant;\
• access scope;\
• affected parties;\
• ecological impact;\
• evidence impact;\
• governance impact;\
• legal or regulatory exposure;\
• investor-facing consequences;\
• risk to community trust;\
• risk to constitutional integrity;\
• willingness to disclose, repair, and cooperate.

**A severity framework may be expressed as follows:**

| **Severity** | **Examples** | **Default response orientation** |
|----|----|----|
| **Minor** | Inadvertent reporting error, incomplete documentation, procedural delay, minor misunderstanding of scope. | Clarification, education, correction, warning, or corrective action. |
| **Moderate** | Repeated negligence, failure to correct after notice, role overreach, weak documentation affecting trust, minor misleading claim. | Formal corrective plan, restricted access, role review, monitoring, or temporary limitation. |
| **Major** | Deliberate MRV distortion, serious public misrepresentation, governance manipulation, significant breach of access scope, exploitative conduct, material data integrity issue. | Suspension, quarantine, non-admissible status, governance review, legal review, public correction where required. |
| **Critical** | Intentional falsification of PRU-relevant evidence, serious ecological harm, coercion, investor-facing fraud, constitutional bypass attempt, capture attempt, serious technical compromise, systemic abuse. | Emergency limitation, constitutional review, exclusion, external legal or regulatory escalation where appropriate. |

Severity classification is not mechanical.

It must be interpreted through evidence and context.

A minor error repeated after warnings may become moderate.

A moderate issue affecting investor-facing claims may become major.

A single critical action may justify immediate protective limitation before full review.

Prometheus must be able to protect itself without abandoning due process.

**9.4 Consequence Types**

Prometheus may apply different consequence types depending on severity and context.

| **Consequence type** | **Function** |
|----|----|
| **Clarification** | Corrects misunderstanding or ambiguous scope. |
| **Education / corrective action** | Helps participant realign with the Covenant. |
| **Formal warning** | Records a breach and requires correction. |
| **Correction notice** | Requires correction of inaccurate claim, record, report, or communication. |
| **Corrective plan** | Defines actions, deadlines, responsibilities, and review points. |
| **Role review** | Reviews whether the participant should continue holding a role. |
| **Restricted status** | Limits specific rights, accesses, functions, or claims. |
| **Suspended status** | Temporarily pauses sensitive access pending review or corrective action. |
| **Non-admissible status** | Prevents a claim, record, output, or pathway from entering PRU, RAP, governance, investor-facing, token, MRV, or public-reporting pathways. |
| **Quarantined status** | Isolates a pathway, dataset, participant, project, or claim due to serious risk. |
| **Corrective disclosure** | Requires public or private correction where prior communication created confusion or risk. |
| **Exclusion** | Removes participant from defined forms of participation according to governance and legal procedure. |
| **External escalation** | Refers serious misconduct to competent legal, regulatory, fiduciary, enforcement, or institutional authorities. |

Consequence does not always mean exclusion. In many cases, the correct response is correction, education, limitation, or corrective action.

Where harm is serious, repeated, intentional, or constitutional, stronger measures become necessary.

Prometheus protects participation by protecting trust.

**9.5 Corrective Action First, Where Possible**

Prometheus should prefer corrective action where the violation is minor, moderate, unintentional, correctable, and not structurally harmful.

**A corrective plan may include:**

• corrected documentation;\
• corrected public communication;\
• renewed Access Covenant affirmation;\
• additional training;\
• role clarification;\
• role limitation;\
• supervision;\
• technical access review;\
• data correction;\
• MRV resubmission;\
• community repair process;\
• conflict mediation;\
• legal documentation update;\
• reporting correction;\
• review date.

corrective action is appropriate when the participant demonstrates:

• good faith;\
• willingness to correct;\
• transparency;\
• cooperation with review;\
• respect for the Covenant;\
• no serious intent to manipulate, extract, or bypass.

corrective action is not appropriate where it becomes a shield for repeated harm.

A regenerative system forgives error.

It does not normalize abuse.

**9.6 Non-Admissibility of Claims and Outputs**

One of the most important consequences in Prometheus is non-admissibility.

Non-admissibility means that a claim, output, record, dataset, communication, action, or pathway may not enter protected Prometheus pathways.

**These may include:**

• MRV validation;\
• PRU assessment;\
• PRU maturity classification;\
• RAP inclusion;\
• investor reporting;\
• public impact reporting;\
• token-mediated representation;\
• governance decision-making;\
• capital-facing communication;\
• institutional data rooms;\
• legal or financial structuring.

Non-admissibility protects the system without necessarily excluding the participant entirely.

**For example:**

• a community narrative may remain meaningful but not MRV-admissible;\
• a dataset may remain archived but not PRU-admissible;\
• a pilot claim may remain exploratory but not investor-facing;\
• a token claim may remain internal to another ecosystem but not Prometheus-valid;\
• an ecological observation may remain useful but not financially admissible.

This distinction is essential.

Prometheus does not need to punish every ambiguity.

It needs to prevent ambiguity from becoming value.

**9.7 Technical Enforcement Boundary**

Technical systems may support Covenant enforcement.

**They may:**

• detect anomalies;\
• flag invalid entries;\
• restrict permissions;\
• pause sensitive functions;\
• log suspicious behavior;\
