Restoration should be precise. A participant may be restored for ordinary participation but remain restricted from MRV validation, technical administration, investor-facing communication, or constitutional roles.

This protects the system while allowing repair.

A regenerative governance system should understand recovery.

If the soil can be restored, governance must also know how to restore trust.

**I.13 Privacy and data minimization**

Governance and reputation data can be sensitive. Prometheus should apply privacy, consent, and minimization principles.

**Governance records should distinguish:**

• public records;\
• internal records;\
• restricted governance records;\
• confidential legal records;\
• sensitive personal records;\
• external human-learning research appendix or external human-learning research records;\
• investor or data-room records.

**Reputation data should be:**

• purpose-limited;\
• explainable;\
• reviewable;\
• appealable where material;\
• minimized;\
• access-controlled;\
• corrected when inaccurate;\
• time-bounded where appropriate.

Sensitive data should not become permanent public identity by default.

The system should preserve accountability without creating reputational captivity.

**I.14 Contribution history**

Contribution history is valuable when handled properly.

**Contribution history may include:**

• code commits;\
• documentation work;\
• MRV submissions;\
• field stewardship;\
• governance service;\
• research contribution;\
• community facilitation;\
• legal drafting;\
• technical review;\
• financial architecture;\
• education and training;\
• conflict resolution;\
• archive preservation;\
• continuity stewardship.

**Contribution history may support:**

• role eligibility;\
• recognition;\
• responsibility assignment;\
• access review;\
• grant or compensation review;\
• community memory;\
• institutional diligence.

Contribution history should not become coercive surveillance.

Contributions should be recognized with context.

Quantity alone is not quality.

Visibility alone is not value.

Prometheus should recognize visible labor, invisible labor, care work, field work, intellectual work, relational work, and continuity work.

**I.15 Token and wallet boundary**

Token and wallet records may exist in some Prometheus-adjacent contexts.

**They may provide limited technical or operational signals, such as:**

• proof of transaction;\
• access credential;\
• contribution reference;\
• governance participation record where legally and technically valid;\
• treasury trace where appropriate;\
• reward distribution record where lawful.

However, wallet history should not become a default proxy for trust, virtue, competence, alignment, or human value.

Token holdings should not automatically create constitutional authority.

Token movement should not automatically create governance rank.

Token penalties should not be assumed without legal structure.

**The token boundary is:**

tokens may record or mediate defined functions;\
they do not define the person;\
they do not override the source layer.

**I.16 AI-assisted governance support**

**AI may support governance analysis. It may:**

• summarize meeting records;\
• identify unresolved tensions;\
• detect missing documentation;\
• map decision dependencies;\
• flag possible conflicts;\
• compare proposals to canonical definitions;\
• suggest agenda structures;\
• classify warrants;\
• identify protected-domain triggers;\
• draft review memos;\
• support appeal preparation.

**AI may not:**

• cast votes;\
• determine consent;\
• remove roles;\
• declare breaches;\
• assign reputation as final authority;\
• rank human worth;\
• reinterpret the constitution as final authority;\
• issue warrants as final judgment;\
• authorize protected-domain decisions.

AI governance support remains assistive, logged, and review-bound.

The decision belongs to the appropriate governance pathway.

**I.17 Anti-capture safeguards**

Prometheus governance should contain safeguards against capture.

**Capture may come from:**

• founders;\
• investors;\
• token whales;\
• technical administrators;\
• charismatic leaders;\
• institutions;\
• coordinated groups;\
• bot swarms;\
• AI agents;\
• community elites;\
• data holders;\
• legal gatekeepers;\
• reputation incumbents.

**Anti-capture safeguards may include:**

• role rotation;\
• term limits where appropriate;\
• conflict-of-interest disclosures;\
• double-linking;\
• appeal pathways;\
• independent review;\
• public decision logs where appropriate;\
• constitutional review;\
• quorum quality controls;\
• separation of powers;\
• least-privilege technical access;\
• multi-party signing;\
• emergency limitation review;\
• protection of minority objections;\
• transparent criteria for role eligibility;\
• clear separation between capital, governance, MRV, and technical authority.

**The anti-capture principle is:**

power must remain visible, bounded, reviewable, and reversible where possible.

**I.18 Governance interfaces**

**Prometheus may implement governance interfaces to support:**

• role registry;\
• proposal intake;\
• consent process;\
• objection integration;\
• decision logging;\
• warrant tracking;\
• appeal filing;\
• corrective-action tracking;\
• contribution records;\
• access review;\
• constitutional review;\
• release approval;\
• fork classification;\
• public correction;\
• audit trails.

**These interfaces should be designed with:**

• human-readable explanations;\
• accessibility;\
• privacy controls;\
• role-based access;\
• auditability;\
• exportability;\
• offline or local-first continuity where feasible;\
• anti-bot and anti-Sybil controls where relevant.

The interface supports governance.

It does not become governance.

**I.19 Minimal governance data schema**

**A governance decision record may include:**

{

"decision_id": "gov_decision_0001",

"decision_type": "ordinary \| technical \| mrv \| legal \| constitutional \| emergency",

"title": "Decision title",

"circle_or_body": "review_body_reference",

"date": "2026-01-01",

"participants": \[

{

"role": "steward",

"identifier": "participant_or_role_reference"

}

\],

"source_layer_references": \[

"access_covenant_v1",

"canonical_glossary_v1"

\],

"evidence_references": \[

"mrv_record_or_document_hash"

\],

"decision_outcome": "approved \| approved_with_conditions \| review_required \| deferred \| rejected \| non_admissible",

"scope": "defined_scope",

"limitations": "defined_limitations",

"review_date": "2026-06-01",

"appeal_pathway": "appeal_body_reference",

"record_hash": "sha256_hash_where_available"

}

**A warrant record may include:**

{

"warrant_id": "warrant_0001",

"domain": "MRV \| governance \| legal \| technical \| token \| external human-learning research appendix \| constitutional",

"severity": "informational \| procedural \| material \| critical",

"signal_source": "human_report \| technical_flag \| AI_risk_flag \| audit \| legal_notice \| MRV_anomaly",

"affected_record": "record_reference",

"status": "open \| review_required \| resolved \| escalated \| closed",

"temporary_measure": "none \| restricted \| quarantined \| suspended_pending_review",

"review_body": "body_reference",

"response_deadline": "2026-01-15",

"resolution": "clarified \| corrected \| corrective action \| non_admissible \| escalated \| dismissed",

"appeal_available": true

}

Schemas are illustrative and should be versioned before implementation.

**I.20 Final governance and reputation principle**

Prometheus governance is not the automation of obedience.

It is the disciplined coordination of responsibility.

Reputation is not a ranking of persons.

It is a contextual memory of accountable participation.

Warrants are not punishments.

They are integrity signals.

Tokens are not constitutional authority.

They are bounded instruments.

AI is not governance.

It is support.

The source layer is the boundary.

Human dignity is the floor.

Evidence is the bridge.

Due process is the path.

Repair is part of regeneration.

**The final principle is:**

recognize contribution;\
remember responsibility;\
flag risk;\
review fairly;\
repair where possible;\
restrict where necessary;\
restore where justified;\
and keep governance accountable to life.

# Technical Annex I-A — Governance & Reputation DNA Specification

The Governance & Reputation DNA implements the sociocratic and review-bound governance principles of Prometheus and provides the technical infrastructure for decentralized decision-making, role-based authority, contribution recognition, integrity signaling, and accountability within the h•eart•h ecosystem.

It is designed to be circle-aware, token-aware where legally and constitutionally relevant, token-non-deterministic, privacy-conscious, non-coercive, and auditable through Holochain-compatible agent-centric infrastructure.

Its purpose is not to create automatic authority. Its purpose is to make governance context, contribution history, decision records, role responsibilities, and integrity signals reviewable without collapsing them into human worth, investor rights, ecological validity, PRU value analysis, or token-based supremacy.

**The Governance & Reputation DNA should preserve the distinction among:**

• governance participation;

• role suitability;

• contribution recognition;

• reputation context;

• integrity signals;

• review pathways;

• warrants or adverse integrity records;

• constitutional constraints;

• source-layer authority.

Reputation may inform governance review.

It should not replace governance judgment.

It should not create automatic governance weight.

It should not create financial modulation.

It should not create investor entitlement.

It should not create human ranking.

It should not override human dignity, MRV evidence, legal review, source-layer constraints, or protected-domain safeguards.

**I-A.1 Circle structure and role definitions**

The DNA encodes a nested circle structure compatible with sociocratic governance, stewardship accountability, bioregional coordination, and Prometheus source-layer review.

| **Circle level** | **Domain** | **Composition** | **Double-linking** |
|----|----|----|----|
| Stewardship circle | Personal regenerative practice, local stewardship, OHE support, operational participation, local evidence context | Individual agents, families, local stewards, project contributors | Elects delegate to Community circle |
| Community circle | Local projects, mutual support, community governance, local MRV coordination, operational review | Representatives from Stewardship circles | Double-linked to Bioregional circle |
| Bioregional circle | Cross-community coordination, resource allocation, territorial strategy, watershed or bioregional coherence, shared infrastructure | Delegates from Community circles | Double-linked to Prometheus circle |
| Prometheus circle | Constitutional maintenance, source-layer interpretation, protocol evolution, canonical definitions, protected-domain review | Representatives from bioregional circles, protocol stewards, and defined governance participants | Ultimate review circle within the governance architecture; subject to constitutional constraints, transparency, recall, and source-layer non-bypassability |

**Role definitions within each circle:**

| **Role** | **Responsibilities** | **Selection method** | **Term** |
|----|----|----|----|
| Facilitator | Guides meetings, protects consent process, maintains circle focus, supports inclusive deliberation | Sociocratic election based on consent | 12 months, renewable |
| Secretary | Maintains records, documents decisions, preserves governance artifacts, tracks proposal history | Sociocratic election based on consent | 12 months, renewable |
| Delegate to parent circle | Represents circle context in the higher-level circle and brings information back to the originating circle | Elected by circle members through consent | 12 months, renewable |
| Operational roles | Project-specific responsibilities, such as treasurer, MRV coordinator, validator, steward, technical maintainer, data-room custodian, or reporting lead | Created as needed through circle governance | Variable |
| Protocol steward | Supports source-layer fidelity, methodology coherence, protected terminology, release alignment, and boundary discipline | Selected under defined governance process | Defined by governance rules |

**I-A.2 Decision processes**

The DNA may support multiple decision-making protocols, selected by the relevant circle according to the nature, risk, and scope of the decision.

No decision process should bypass the source layer, human dignity, MRV discipline, legal review, public-claim boundaries, investor-facing safeguards, or protected-domain review.

**I-A.2.1 Consent as default governance protocol**

Consent is the standard decision protocol for ordinary governance matters.

A proposal is adopted when no circle member has a reasoned and paramount objection.

A reasoned and paramount objection is an evidence-based concern that the proposal may harm the circle’s ability to achieve its purpose, violate source-layer principles, create material risk, or exceed the circle’s authority.

**Process flow:**

1.  Proposal is formulated and published with sufficient context, scope, rationale, and review period.

2.  Facilitator confirms the decision domain and applicable review pathway.

3.  Members may state consent, stand aside, or objection.

4.  Objections are explored, documented, and integrated where possible.

5.  Modified proposal returns for renewed consent.

6.  If no unresolved reasoned and paramount objection remains, the proposal passes.

7.  The decision is recorded with scope, date, participants, rationale, objections, modifications, and implementation status.

**Technical implementation example:**

struct ConsentVote {

proposal_id: Hash,

member_id: AgentPubKey,

decision: ConsentDecision, // Consent, StandAside, Objection

objection_reason: Option\<String\>,

timestamp: Timestamp,

signature: Signature

}

**I-A.2.2 Consent with reputation-informed context**

For certain operational decisions, a circle may choose to consider reputation signals as non-binding contextual inputs.

Reputation may inform role suitability, contribution history, reliability context, and participation maturity.

It should not automatically determine consent, override objections, create token authority, exclude participants, or convert governance into weighted plutocracy.

**Where reputation-informed context is used, the circle should define:**

• the decision scope;

• the reputation dimensions considered;

• whether reputation is advisory or operationally relevant;

• the privacy and disclosure boundaries;

• the appeal or correction pathway;

• the protected-domain review requirements.

Reputation-informed decision support may be appropriate for operational roles, technical maintenance, validator eligibility, steward responsibility, or contribution recognition.

It should not be used to decide human worth, ecological truth, PRU value analysis, investor rights, constitutional amendments, legal rights, medical claims, public certification, or source-layer changes.

**I-A.2.3 Scenario-Informed Governance Research**

For complex, high-stakes decisions, a circle may use scenario analysis, forecasting tools, prediction-market research, probabilistic modeling, AI-assisted comparison, stress testing, or risk simulations as non-binding decision-support inputs.

These tools may inform deliberation, surface assumptions, identify downside cases, and improve risk awareness.

They may not determine constitutional interpretation, MRV admissibility, legal rights, investor exposure, human dignity, source-layer constraints, public-claim boundaries, or protected-domain decisions.

Scenario tools support judgment.

They do not replace judgment.

**I-A.2.4 Constitutional amendments and source-layer review**

Constitutional amendments must follow the constitutional review pathway defined by the source layer.

**Any amendment process must preserve:**

• foundational articles;

• Eternity Clauses;

• Access Covenant;

• canonical glossary;

• human dignity;

• MRV discipline;

• legal boundaries;

• public-claim discipline;

• no-double-counting controls;

• source-layer non-bypassability;

• protected-domain review.

Token participation may support bounded signaling, contribution recognition, or defined governance participation where legally and constitutionally appropriate.

It may not create unilateral constitutional authority, sovereign veto power, financial override, ordinary bypass of source-layer constraints, or automatic amendment authority.

**I-A.3 Reputation algorithms**

Reputation is a multidimensional, non-transferable, context-bound signal that tracks an agent’s history of contributions, reliability, review outcomes, and alignment with Prometheus governance discipline.

**It may inform:**

• contextual governance review;

• role suitability;

• contribution recognition;

• stewardship reliability;

• validator eligibility;

• operational trust;

• access assessment within defined scope.

**It does not create:**

• automatic governance weight;

• human ranking;

• token authority;

• financial modulation;

• role entitlement;

• investor rights;

• PRU value;

• MRV validity;

• legal rights;

• source-layer authority.

Reputation is an integrity signal.

It is not a social-credit system.

It is not a measure of human worth.

**I-A.3.1 Reputation components**

| **Component** | **Indicative weight** | **Data source** | **Update frequency** | **Boundary** |
|----|----|----|----|----|
| Contribution Score | 40% | REA events, completed commitments, verified project participation, documented stewardship | After relevant contribution records | Recognizes contribution; does not create entitlement |
| Reliability Score | 30% | Commitments respected, commitments breached, corrective action history, adverse integrity records | After each relevant commitment | Supports trust assessment; requires appeal and correction pathways |
| Governance Participation | 15% | Meeting participation, proposal quality, review contribution, circle attendance, constructive deliberation | Per governance event | Supports governance maturity; not a voting-right guarantee |
| Validation Accuracy | 10% | MRV validation quality, review consistency, false-positive and false-negative history, audit alignment | Per validation or review event | Supports validator suitability; does not replace MRV methodology |
| Coherence Context | 5% | Voluntary, aggregated, privacy-preserving human-state research or self-reflection context where lawfully and explicitly consented | Periodic and optional | Research or reflection only; never human ranking or financial authority |

**I-A.3.2 Reputation calculation**

Reputation may be calculated locally by each node and shared through Holochain-compatible infrastructure under appropriate privacy and disclosure controls.

**A simplified formula may be expressed as:**

**R_total = (Σ w_i · s_i) / (Σ w_i) · (1 - p_warrants)**

**Where:**

• w_i are component weights;

• s_i are normalized component scores between 0 and 1;

• p_warrants is a penalty factor based on active, reviewed, and procedurally valid adverse integrity records.

Reputation decay may be applied over time to prevent stale reputation from becoming permanent authority.

A default decay rate may be defined by governance, for example 5% per year, subject to review.

The purpose of decay is to encourage continued participation, current reliability, and ongoing stewardship rather than static prestige.

**I-A.3.3 Cross-community reputation context**

The reputation system may be token-aware and wallet-aware where legally, technically, and privacy-wise appropriate.

It should remain token-non-deterministic.

An agent’s history of contributions across multiple communities, projects, DAOs, bioregional systems, or Prometheus-compatible implementations may inform a unified reputation context only where consent, privacy, provenance, and compatibility conditions are satisfied. This may enable:

• trust to travel across communities;

• reduced onboarding friction;

• stronger contribution recognition;

• detection of inconsistent or harmful behavior;

• mitigation of Sybil risk.

However, cross-community reputation should not create automatic governance power, financial eligibility, investment suitability, social ranking, identity scoring, or source-layer authority.

**I-A.4 Warrant handling and adverse integrity records**

Warrants are signed integrity records that identify alleged misbehavior, data falsification, constitutional violation, governance abuse, or other harmful acts.

They function as part of the immune system of Prometheus.

A warrant should not be treated as guilt by declaration.

It is an adverse integrity signal that must pass through due process, review, evidence assessment, challenge, and appeal where relevant.

**I-A.4.1 Warrant types**

| **Warrant type** | **Description** | **Issuance threshold** |
|----|----|----|
| Data integrity violation | Falsified MRV data, invalid evidence records, altered provenance, unsupported claims | Initial detection by node, reviewer, auditor, or governance process |
| Constitutional violation | Code, action, proposal, claim, or implementation that may violate source-layer constraints, Eternity Clauses, human dignity, or protected-domain rules | Detection plus appropriate constitutional or Prometheus circle review |
| Reputation fraud | Artificial inflation of reputation through collusion, circular endorsements, false contribution records, or Sybil behavior | Neighborhood review or defined governance threshold |
| Double-counting attempt | Attempt to claim the same regenerative event, ecological improvement, credit, PRU-relevant evidence, or impact result across incompatible pathways | Validation detection plus no-double-counting review |
| Public-claim violation | External use of Prometheus language, mark, certification, PRU, MRV, RAP, or investor-facing claim outside permitted boundaries | Claim-control review and governance assessment |
| Partner overreach | Institution, partner, fork, or implementation claiming authority beyond granted agreement or verified status | Governance review and, where relevant, legal review |

**I-A.4.2 Warrant lifecycle**

1\. Issuance.

A warrant or adverse integrity record may be issued by a node, reviewer, auditor, governance circle, or authorized process.

The record should identify the alleged issue, affected record, evidence basis, issuer, timestamp, and applicable review domain.

2\. Notice.

The affected agent, project, implementation, partner, or fork should receive notice where feasible and appropriate.

3\. Challenge period.

The affected party should have a defined period to respond, provide evidence, correct misunderstanding, contest the warrant, or request review.

A default period may be seven days unless urgency, legal risk, public-harm risk, data integrity, or protected-domain concerns require faster containment.

4\. Neighborhood or governance review.

The warrant should be reviewed by the relevant validation neighborhood, governance circle, protocol steward group, or protected-domain review body.

5\. Determination.

**The review may result in:**

• no action;

• correction request;

• warning;

• review-required status;

• compatibility downgrade;

• restricted status;

• non-admissible status;

• quarantine;

• certification suspension or revocation where applicable;

• role limitation;

• access limitation;

• public correction notice;

• legal review.

6\. Appeal.

Warrants and material adverse determinations should be appealable to the appropriate higher circle or review body, up to the Prometheus circle where source-layer meaning is implicated.

7\. Record update.

The outcome should be recorded with rationale, evidence reviewed, determination, scope, duration, and appeal status.

**I-A.4.3 False warrant penalties**

To prevent abuse, false, reckless, malicious, or bad-faith warrant issuance should itself create consequences.

**Possible consequences may include:**

• reputation loss for the issuing node or agent;

• review-required status;

• restriction from validation roles;

• corrective requirement;

• compatibility downgrade;

• temporary or permanent role limitation;

• access limitation;

• legal review where applicable.

Any TRBK-related consequence, if ever applicable, must be governed by a separate legally reviewed instrument, explicit disclosures, governance approval, and jurisdiction-specific compliance.

Ordinary Prometheus governance should prefer review-required status, restriction, non-admissibility, quarantine, corrective action, compatibility revocation, or role limitation over automatic token penalties.

**I-A.5 Integration with other DNAs**

| **DNA** | **Integration point** |
|----|----|
| REA / Valueflows | Reputation updates may be informed by completed commitments, contribution records, disputed events, and role performance |
| MRV | Validation accuracy, evidence quality, audit alignment, and data-integrity issues may inform reputation or warrant status |
| TRBK | TRBK may support bounded governance participation, access, contribution recognition, or coordination where legally and constitutionally defined; it should not create automatic authority, staking supremacy, or slashing-based constitutional control |
| Holo Hosting / operational infrastructure | Uptime, service reliability, node behavior, and operational integrity may inform role suitability and infrastructure reputation |
| Decision Log | Governance decisions, objections, consent records, amendments, reviews, and appeals should be recorded for auditability and future learning |
| Canonical Registry | Fork status, implementation status, compatibility claims, certification claims, and public-claim boundaries may be linked to registry status |

**I-A.6 Privacy and transparency**

The Governance & Reputation DNA should balance radical auditability with privacy, dignity, purpose limitation, and proportional disclosure.

Prometheus should distinguish between public decisions, private deliberation, individual voting choices, aggregate reputation, component-level reputation, warrants, protected-domain records, and legally sensitive materials.

| **Data type** | **Visibility** | **Privacy mechanism** |
|----|----|----|
| Final decisions | Public or registry-visible where appropriate | No privacy required unless legally or ethically sensitive |
| Individual votes | Circle-configurable: public, delayed disclosure, private, or selectively disclosed | Selective disclosure, zero-knowledge proof, or circle-level confidentiality where appropriate |
| Reputation aggregate | Context-dependent; may be public, limited, or role-specific | Selective disclosure, Merkle proofs, permissioned access, or privacy-preserving attestations |
| Reputation components | Generally private or limited to relevant review body | Component-level privacy, purpose limitation, access logs |
| Warrants / adverse integrity records | Visible to relevant review bodies; public only where necessary for protection, certification, registry, or public-claim clarity | Due process, scope limitation, appeal records |
| human-state research-related context | Private, voluntary, consent-based, time-limited | No personally identifiable disclosure by default; research or reflection only |

Detailed technical specifications, including complete data structures, validation rules, privacy controls, DHT behavior, zome functions, and API endpoints, should be maintained in the separate technical documentation package.

# Appendix J: REA / Valueflows DNA Specification

**J.1 Overview**

The REA / Valueflows DNA implements a multidimensional accounting framework based on the Resource-Event-Agent ontology, extended for regenerative applications.

It provides foundational data structures for tracking value flows within the h•eart•h ecosystem: ecological, social, operational, economic, relational, and governance-related.

Its purpose is not to reduce living systems to financial entries.

Its purpose is to preserve the path from resource to event, from event to agent, from agent to evidence, from evidence to review, and from review to admissible value interpretation.

**The DNA is designed to be:**

• agent-centric: each agent maintains its own source chain of events, with shared validation through Holochain-compatible infrastructure;

• multidimensional: resources, events, commitments, agents, evidence, locations, and time windows can be represented without collapsing them into one metric;

• temporally aware: every event is linked to time windows, allowing historical reconstruction, performance review, and future planning;

• ecologically grounded: relevant events may be linked to site, ecosystem, OHE, MRV evidence, PHI context, PRU analysis, or RAP screening;

• constitutionally compliant: all entries should carry the syntropic sovereignty header or equivalent canonical integrity metadata;

• review-bound: entries may support value interpretation only where evidence, methodology, governance, and legal structure allow.

**J.2 Core data structures**

**J.2.1 Resource**

A resource is anything that is identified, quantified, and relevant to agents within the Prometheus ecosystem.

A resource may be tangible or intangible.

Examples may include land-use rights, water access, biomass, food output, labor, knowledge, operational capacity, ecological performance claims, data records, reputation context, or digital coordination assets.

The resource model should not imply ownership of living beings, automatic ecological credit issuance, investor rights, or market entitlement.

| **Field** | **Type** | **Description** | **Required** |
|----|----|----|----|
| id | Hash | Unique identifier or hash of genesis entry | ✓ |
| name | String | Human-readable name | ✓ |
| resource_type | Enum | land_use_right, water_access, biomass, food_output, ecological_performance_claim, biodiversity_record, data_record, labor, knowledge, reputation_context, digital_asset, other | ✓ |
| quantity | Float | Current amount or recorded quantity, where applicable | ✓ |
| unit | String | Unit of measurement, such as hectares, tons, kWh, hours, index points, records, or equivalent | ✓ |
| location | GeoJSON | Optional geographic coordinates or boundary |  |
| ecosystem_id | Hash | Link to ecosystem, OHE, site, or project context where applicable |  |
| metadata | JSON | Extended attributes, limitations, methodology references, or certification status where applicable |  |
| header | SyntropicHeader | Constitutional compliance or canonical integrity header | ✓ |

**J.2.2 Event**

An event is an observable transformation, transfer, action, measurement, commitment fulfillment, or status change affecting resources, agents, evidence, or governance records.

Events are the atomic units of operational and economic activity.

| **Field** | **Type** | **Description** | **Required** |
|----|----|----|----|
| id | Hash | Unique identifier | ✓ |
| event_type | Enum | production, consumption, transfer, exchange, measurement, verification, stewardship_action, commitment_fulfillment, governance_decision, correction, review | ✓ |
| action | String | Verb describing the action, such as plant, harvest, measure, verify, certify, review, correct, transfer, report, or reinvest | ✓ |
| provider | AgentPubKey | Agent providing the resource, action, service, or evidence |  |
| receiver | AgentPubKey | Agent receiving the resource, action, service, or evidence |  |
| resource_id | Hash | Link to affected resource |  |
| quantity | Float | Amount involved where applicable | ✓ |
| unit | String | Unit of measurement | ✓ |
| location | GeoJSON | Where the event occurred where applicable |  |
| time_window | TimeWindow | When the event occurred | ✓ |
| mrv_id | Hash | Link to MRV evidence where applicable |  |
| commitment_id | Hash | Link to commitment being fulfilled where applicable |  |
| review_status | Enum | draft, submitted, reviewed, rejected, corrected, admissible, non_admissible |  |
| metadata | JSON | Extended attributes, limitations, methodology references, legal boundary, or audit notes |  |
| header | SyntropicHeader | Constitutional compliance or canonical integrity header | ✓ |

**J.2.3 Agent**

An agent is any entity capable of action within the system.

Agents may include individuals, organizations, DAOs, projects, devices, auditors, validators, laboratories, public institutions, or automated processes operating under defined authority.

| **Field** | **Type** | **Description** | **Required** |
|----|----|----|----|
| id | AgentPubKey | Public key or DPKI identifier | ✓ |
| agent_type | Enum | individual, organization, dao, project, device, auditor, validator, laboratory, public_institution, automated | ✓ |
| name | String | Human-readable name |  |
| profile | JSON | Optional profile data, credentials, role information, or public metadata |  |
| reputation_id | Hash | Link to reputation context where applicable |  |
| authority_scope | JSON | Defined scope of authority, role, or permitted action |  |
| header | SyntropicHeader | Constitutional compliance or canonical integrity header | ✓ |

**J.2.4 Commitment**

A commitment is an agreement, plan, proposal, contract, stewardship undertaking, delivery obligation, or governance-approved intention to perform future events.

It creates accountability and enables forward planning.

| **Field** | **Type** | **Description** | **Required** |
|----|----|----|----|
| id | Hash | Unique identifier | ✓ |
| commitment_type | Enum | agreement, contract, plan, proposal, stewardship_commitment, service_commitment, funding_commitment, governance_commitment | ✓ |
| provider | AgentPubKey | Agent committing to provide |  |
| receiver | AgentPubKey | Agent expecting to receive or benefit |  |
| resource_id | Hash | Resource involved where applicable |  |
| quantity | Float | Amount committed where applicable | ✓ |
| unit | String | Unit of measurement | ✓ |
| due_start | Timestamp | Earliest expected fulfillment |  |
| due_end | Timestamp | Latest expected fulfillment | ✓ |
| conditions | JSON | Conditions, limitations, legal boundary, methodology references, or governance constraints |  |
| fulfilled_by | \[Hash\] | Events that fulfill this commitment |  |
| status | Enum | active, fulfilled, expired, cancelled, under_review, disputed | ✓ |
| header | SyntropicHeader | Constitutional compliance header carrying relevant sovereignty hashes and signature metadata | ✓ |

**J.2.5 TimeWindow**

A reusable time specification for events, commitments, monitoring periods, or reporting windows.

| **Field** | **Type**  | **Description**  | **Required** |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| id        | String    | Named identifier | ✓            |
| start     | Timestamp | UTC start time   | ✓            |
| end       | Timestamp | UTC end time     | ✓            |

**J.3 Validation rules**

**J.3.1 Intrinsic validation**

**All entries should pass basic structural validation:**

• required fields present and correctly typed;

• signatures valid;

• syntropic sovereignty header or canonical integrity metadata present and verified;

• hash links point to existing entries or permitted null values;

• timestamps valid;

• authority scope not exceeded;

• review status properly assigned.

**J.3.2 Economic and operational validation**

**Economic and operational entries should satisfy applicable consistency rules:**

• conservation of value where double-entry or reciprocal accounting applies;

• quantity non-negativity unless explicitly allowed for liabilities, obligations, deficits, or corrective records;

• unit consistency;

• commitment fulfillment matched to valid commitment;

• event authorization by relevant agent or governance process;

• clear separation between operational events, financial events, ecological evidence, and legal rights.

**J.3.3 Ecological validation**

**Events claiming ecological relevance should satisfy ecological validation rules:**

• MRV linking where ecological impact is claimed;

• location validity within the claimed site, OHE, project, bioregion, or ecosystem;

• methodology compatibility;

• confidence scoring where required;

• no-double-counting controls;

• PHI or Planetary Boundaries context where applicable;

• review pathway for PRU-relevant or public-facing claims.

Ecological validation does not automatically create credits, investor rights, PRU value, token issuance, or financial instruments.

**J.3.4 Constitutional validation**

All entries should remain compatible with the Prometheus source layer.

**Constitutional validation should enforce:**

• non-commodification of life;

• human dignity;

• agent sovereignty;

• consent and lawful authority;

• MRV discipline;

• legal boundary;

• no-double-counting;

• source-layer non-bypassability;

• public-claim discipline;

• token-boundary separation.

Any event violating constitutional principles may be rejected, marked non-admissible, quarantined, or routed to governance review.

**J.4 Integration with other DNAs**

| **DNA** | **Integration point** |
|----|----|
| Governance & Reputation | Agent records may link to governance roles, contribution context, reputation context, review status, and participation history. Commitments may originate from governance decisions, consent processes, delegated mandates, or reviewed operational plans. Disputed events may trigger review, correction, quarantine, or appeal pathways. |
| MRV | Events may link to MRV evidence through mrv_id, evidence_bundle_id, methodology_version, confidence_score, and review_status. MRV outcomes may support evidence-backed reporting, PRU analysis, maturity classification, or review pathways where admissible. MRV outcomes do not automatically trigger token issuance, PRU value, investor rights, or legal recognition. |
| TRBK / tokens | Token-related events may represent governance, access, coordination, contribution, participation, settlement, or legally structured rights only where explicitly defined, legally reviewed, and jurisdictionally appropriate. Token-related records must not imply PRU value, RAP exposure, investor rights, land rights, MRV validity, or constitutional authority by default. |
| Decision Log | Commitments, consent decisions, objections, reviews, amendments, corrections, withdrawals, adverse events, and protected-domain decisions may be logged for auditability, governance continuity, and institutional traceability. |
| Planetary Boundaries / PHI | Event validation may check PHI context, protected-domain constraints, planetary-boundary relevance, ecological-risk signals, and public-claim boundaries. PHI signals may trigger review but do not automatically approve, reject, downgrade, finance, certify, or reclassify an event, PRU, RAP, project, or portfolio. |

**J.5 Query patterns**

The REA DNA may support complex queries for dashboards, reporting, audits, data rooms, and analysis.

// Example: Get all events for a project in a time range

fn get_events_by_project(project_id: Hash, start: Timestamp, end: Timestamp) -\> Vec\<Event\> {

// DHT query by basis address (project_id + event_type)

}

// Example: Calculate net resource change for an agent

fn net_resource_change(agent_id: AgentPubKey, resource_type: String) -\> Float {

// Sum provider events and receiver events where applicable

}

// Example: Find unfulfilled commitments

fn unfulfilled_commitments() -\> Vec\<Commitment\> {

// Query where status = active and due_end \< now

}

// Example: Get all PRU-relevant evidence events

fn get_pru_relevant_events(project_id: Hash) -\> Vec\<Event\> {

// Query events linked to MRV evidence, admissibility review, or PRU methodology scope

}

**J.6 Example flow: Regenerative agriculture project**

1\. Agent registration.

Land steward, project entity, verifier, and relevant institutional actors register as agents.

2\. Commitment.

A funder, partner, public entity, or internal treasury creates a defined commitment to support a soil-regeneration project.

3\. Resource.

A land-use-right or project-resource entry is created, linked to site boundaries, OHE context, and legal access documentation.

4\. MRV event.

Satellite data, soil sensors, field observations, laboratory tests, and stewardship records produce MRV-linked evidence events.

5\. Evidence review.

MRV evidence is reviewed for provenance, confidence, methodology alignment, no-double-counting, PHI context, and admissibility.

6\. PRU-relevant interpretation.

Verified indicators may support PRU value analysis only after evidence, methodology, confidence, governance review, admissibility, and legal boundary checks.

7\. Economic or operational event.

Operational outputs, service delivery, procurement, hospitality, education, food production, or other real economic activity may be recorded as REA events.

8\. Legal or institutional pathway.

Any future credit, note, fund interest, revenue right, tokenized representation, or compliant digital instrument requires separate legal structuring, disclosure, investor-protection review, jurisdiction-specific compliance, and anti-double-counting controls.

9\. Commitment fulfillment.

Events fulfilling the original commitment are linked back to the commitment record.

10\. Governance review.

The relevant circle reviews project performance, evidence quality, adverse events, lessons learned, and parameter updates. All steps are recorded through agent-centric source chains and shared validation where appropriate, preserving temporal linking, auditability, and constitutional compliance.

**J.7 Schema definitions**

The following simplified schema illustrates the conceptual structure of REA / Valueflows-compatible records.

syntax = "proto3";

package rea.v1;

import "google/protobuf/timestamp.proto";

import "syntropic_header.proto";

message Resource {

bytes id = 1;

string name = 2;

enum ResourceType {

LAND_USE_RIGHT = 0;

WATER_ACCESS = 1;

BIOMASS = 2;

FOOD_OUTPUT = 3;

ECOLOGICAL_PERFORMANCE_CLAIM = 4;

BIODIVERSITY_RECORD = 5;

DATA_RECORD = 6;

LABOR = 7;

KNOWLEDGE = 8;

REPUTATION_CONTEXT = 9;

DIGITAL_ASSET = 10;

OTHER = 11;

}

ResourceType resource_type = 3;

double quantity = 4;

string unit = 5;

string location = 6; // GeoJSON

bytes ecosystem_id = 7;

string metadata = 8; // JSON

SyntropicHeader header = 9;

}

message Event {

bytes id = 1;

enum EventType {

PRODUCTION = 0;

CONSUMPTION = 1;

TRANSFER = 2;

EXCHANGE = 3;

MEASUREMENT = 4;

VERIFICATION = 5;

STEWARDSHIP_ACTION = 6;

COMMITMENT_FULFILLMENT = 7;

GOVERNANCE_DECISION = 8;

CORRECTION = 9;

REVIEW = 10;

}

EventType event_type = 2;

string action = 3;

bytes provider = 4;

bytes receiver = 5;

bytes resource_id = 6;

double quantity = 7;

string unit = 8;

string location = 9;

TimeWindow time_window = 10;

bytes mrv_id = 11;

bytes commitment_id = 12;

string review_status = 13;

string metadata = 14; // JSON

SyntropicHeader header = 15;

}

message Agent {

bytes id = 1;

enum AgentType {

INDIVIDUAL = 0;

ORGANIZATION = 1;

DAO = 2;

PROJECT = 3;

DEVICE = 4;

AUDITOR = 5;

VALIDATOR = 6;

LABORATORY = 7;

PUBLIC_INSTITUTION = 8;

AUTOMATED = 9;

}

AgentType agent_type = 2;

string name = 3;

string profile = 4; // JSON

bytes reputation_id = 5;

string authority_scope = 6; // JSON

SyntropicHeader header = 7;

}

message Commitment {

bytes id = 1;

enum CommitmentType {

AGREEMENT = 0;

CONTRACT = 1;

PLAN = 2;

PROPOSAL = 3;

STEWARDSHIP_COMMITMENT = 4;

SERVICE_COMMITMENT = 5;

FUNDING_COMMITMENT = 6;

GOVERNANCE_COMMITMENT = 7;

}

CommitmentType commitment_type = 2;

bytes provider = 3;

bytes receiver = 4;

bytes resource_id = 5;

double quantity = 6;

string unit = 7;

google.protobuf.Timestamp due_start = 8;

google.protobuf.Timestamp due_end = 9;

string conditions = 10; // JSON

repeated bytes fulfilled_by = 11;

enum Status {

ACTIVE = 0;

FULFILLED = 1;

EXPIRED = 2;

CANCELLED = 3;

UNDER_REVIEW = 4;

DISPUTED = 5;

}

Status status = 12;

SyntropicHeader header = 13;

}

message TimeWindow {

string id = 1;

google.protobuf.Timestamp start = 2;

google.protobuf.Timestamp end = 3;

}

**J.8 Constitutional integration**

Every entry in the REA / Valueflows DNA should include a syntropic sovereignty header or equivalent canonical integrity metadata.

The header ensures that value-flow records remain connected to source-layer constraints, release identity, governance boundaries, and constitutional compliance. Any attempt to record non-compliant events may be rejected, marked non-admissible, quarantined, or routed to governance review.

Detailed implementation specifications, including Holochain zome code, validation callbacks, query optimization, privacy controls, and API endpoints, should be maintained in the separate technical documentation package.

# Appendix K: Planetary Boundaries, PHI, and Evidence Review Framework

**K.0 Overview**

The h•eart•h Prometheus protocol integrates the Planetary Boundaries framework as a foundational context layer for ecological integrity assessment, regenerative design, MRV prioritization, PHI synthesis, PRU analysis, RAP screening, and capital-readiness review.

The purpose of this appendix is not to convert Planetary Boundaries into automatic financial value, investment eligibility, ecological credit issuance, legal rights, or tokenized representation.

Its purpose is to define how planetary-boundary context may inform evidence review, risk interpretation, methodological caution, protected-domain routing, and capital-readiness discipline.

**Within Prometheus, the Planetary Boundaries layer helps answer:**

• whether an activity supports or weakens ecological integrity;

• whether a project operates in a sensitive ecological domain;

• whether MRV requirements should be strengthened;

• whether PHI signals indicate heightened review needs;

• whether PRU-related analysis should remain internal, review-bound, or non-admissible;

• whether RAP inclusion requires additional safeguards;

• whether public claims require stricter language discipline;

• whether capital-readiness interpretation should be limited, conditioned, or paused.

Planetary Boundaries and PHI should therefore function as context and review infrastructure.

They should not function as automatic scoring authority.

They should not automatically create financeability.

They should not automatically downgrade, approve, certify, tokenize, or reject a project without the appropriate evidence, methodology, governance, and legal review pathway.

**The operational rule is:**

planetary context informs risk;

PHI signals review needs;

MRV verifies evidence;

PRU analyzes only admissible value;

RAP aggregates only where portfolio criteria are satisfied;

capital-readiness remains downstream from evidence, governance, legal structure, and admissibility.

This preserves the distinction between ecological context, evidence review, financial interpretation, legal rights, and institutional use.

Planetary Boundaries provide Prometheus with a global scientific horizon.

They help the system understand where human activity interacts with the Earth-system processes that regulate planetary stability, biospheric resilience, ecological function, climate integrity, freshwater cycles, land systems, nutrient flows, ocean chemistry, atmospheric conditions, and novel-entity pressure.

Prometheus uses this framework to connect global ecological risk with local regenerative action.

The purpose is not to reduce Earth-system science into a single score.

The purpose is to make planetary context operationally legible inside a regenerative infrastructure system.

**Within Prometheus, planetary-boundary logic informs:**

• ecological baseline interpretation;\
• bioregional stress assessment;\
• MRV indicator selection;\
• Planetary Health Index, or PHI, domain structure;\
• boundary-pressure profiling;\
• PRU-relevant ecological analysis;\
• RAP portfolio screening;\
• regenerative design priorities;\
• capital-readiness diligence;\
• long-term continuity and resilience planning.

The framework follows the 2023 revision of the Planetary Boundaries architecture and aligns with the scientific direction associated with the Stockholm Resilience Centre, the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research, and the Planetary Boundaries Science initiative.

**For Prometheus, this scientific architecture becomes part of the wider source-layer discipline:**

planetary reality provides context;\
bioregional reality provides interpretation;\
field evidence provides grounding;\
MRV provides verification;\
PHI provides synthesis;\
PRU provides value discipline;\
governance provides admissibility;\
capital follows verified regeneration.

This appendix specifies the technical implementation of planetary boundary monitoring, threshold calculations, and the derivation of the Planetary Health Index (PHI) used throughout the ecosystem for ecological-context assessment, protective review triggers, systemic-risk awareness, and review-bound ecological response pathways. It establishes a disciplined bridge between Earth-system science and site-level regenerative intelligence.

**K.1 Purpose and boundary**

Prometheus exists within planetary reality.

Every regenerative claim made inside Prometheus must remain accountable to the living systems that make life possible: soil, water, biodiversity, climate, nutrient cycles, land systems, ecological resilience, biospheric integrity, and the capacity of human communities to live within those systems over time.

The purpose of this appendix is to define how Prometheus integrates planetary-boundary thinking, bioregional stress indicators, Planetary Health Index logic, and ecological evidence review into its MRV, PRU, RAP, governance, design, legal, reporting, and capital-readiness architecture.

This appendix gives Prometheus a planetary-context layer.

**It helps answer questions such as:**

• Which Earth-system pressures are relevant to a given site, project, OHE, bioregion, or portfolio?\
• Which planetary-boundary domains should influence MRV indicator selection?\
• Which ecological risks should be visible before capital formation?\
• Which PHI domains are mature enough for evidence-backed interpretation?\
• Which indicators remain contextual, research-use, MRV-admissible, PRU-eligible, or non-admissible?\
• Which claims require scientific review, governance review, or further field evidence?\
• Which local regenerative actions may reduce pressure, increase resilience, or improve ecological function in relation to planetary-boundary domains?

The role of this appendix is therefore to create disciplined ecological legibility.

Planetary Boundaries do not operate inside Prometheus as an oracle, automatic approval mechanism, or sovereign enforcement layer. They operate as a global scientific context. PHI does not operate as a magic number. It operates as a structured synthesis of ecological, bioregional, evidentiary, governance, and resilience indicators.

MRV remains the evidence-verification pathway.

Governance remains the admissibility pathway.

Legal review remains the enforceability pathway.

PRU and RAP remain downstream from methodology, evidence, confidence, and review.

Capital remains downstream from verified regeneration.

Prometheus may therefore use planetary-boundary and PHI logic to understand whether a project, OHE, bioregional cluster, PRU-relevant unit, or RAP candidate is operating within conditions of ecological stability, rising stress, threshold pressure, or systemic vulnerability.

This assessment is not merely descriptive. It supports design intelligence.

It helps Prometheus identify where regeneration is most needed, which indicators matter most, how evidence should be prioritized, where public claims must remain cautious, and how capital can be aligned with real ecological repair.

**The core operating principle is:**

planetary context informs design;\
bioregional reality grounds interpretation;\
field evidence anchors claims;\
MRV verifies performance;\
PHI organizes ecological intelligence;\
governance determines admissibility;\
legal structure defines rights;\
capital follows verified regeneration.

Prometheus is planetary in awareness, bioregional in interpretation, local in evidence, and constitutional in fidelity.

**K.2 The Nine Planetary Boundaries: Indicators and Data Sources**

The Planetary Boundaries framework provides Prometheus with a global Earth-system context for interpreting regenerative performance. The table below should be treated as a versioned planetary-boundary reference snapshot, not as an automatic enforcement mechanism. Its purpose is to identify:

• Earth-system process;\
• control variable;\
• pre-industrial or reference condition;\
• planetary-boundary range;\
• current global condition;\
• primary data sources;\
• suggested update frequency.

This table supports PHI, boundary-pressure profiling, MRV prioritization, PRU contextual analysis, RAP portfolio screening, and capital-readiness diligence.

It does not automatically issue PRU value, approve RAP inclusion, trigger token issuance, determine investor rights, replace MRV, or replace scientific review.

Planetary Boundaries provide context.

MRV provides evidence. PHI organizes ecological intelligence.

Governance determines admissibility.Capital follows verified regeneration.

| **Earth system process** | **Control variable** | **Pre-industrial reference** | **Planetary boundary / Zone of increasing risk** | **Current value / 2023 reference snapshot** | **Data sources** | **Update frequency** |
|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| Climate change | Atmospheric CO₂ concentration | 280 ppm | 350–450 ppm | 417 ppm | NOAA/ESRL, Copernicus, WMO GAW | Monthly |
| Climate change | Radiative forcing | 0 W/m² | +1.0 to +1.5 W/m² | +2.91 W/m² | IPCC AR6, NASA GISS | Annual |
| Biosphere integrity | Genetic diversity: extinction rate | 0.1–1 E/MSY, background rate | 10–100 E/MSY | \>100 E/MSY | IUCN Red List, Living Planet Index | Annual |
| Biosphere integrity | Functional diversity: HANPP, percentage of pre-industrial NPP | 1.9% | 10–20% | 30% | FLUXCOM, MODIS, EarthStat | Annual |
| Stratospheric ozone depletion | Ozone concentration | 290 DU | 276–261 DU | 284.6 DU | NASA Ozone Watch, Copernicus CAMS | Daily / Monthly |
| Ocean acidification | Aragonite saturation state, Ωarag | 3.44 Ωarag | 2.75–2.4 Ωarag, approximately 80–70% of pre-industrial level | 2.8 Ωarag, approximately 84% | NOAA PMEL, GOA-ON, SOCAT | Monthly |
| Biogeochemical flows — phosphorus | Global phosphorus flow to oceans | 0 Mt/year | 11–100 Mt/year | 22.6 Mt/year | FAO, global nutrient-cycle databases | Annual |
| Biogeochemical flows — phosphorus | Regional phosphorus fertilizer application | 0 Mt/year | 6.2–11.2 Mt/year | 17.5 Mt/year | FAO, IFASTAT / International Fertilizer Association statistics | Annual |
| Biogeochemical flows — nitrogen | Reactive nitrogen release | 0 Mt/year | 62–82 Mt/year | 190 Mt/year | FAO, EDGAR | Annual |
| Land-system change | Global forest cover, percentage of original forest | 100% | 75–54%, global weighted range | 60% | ESA CCI, MODIS, Global Forest Watch | Annual |
| Land-system change | Tropical forest cover, percentage of original forest | 100% | 85–60% | Americas: 83.9%; Africa: 54.3%; Asia: 37.5% | FAO FRA, Global Forest Watch | Annual |
| Land-system change | Temperate forest cover, percentage of original forest | 100% | 50–30% | Americas: 51.2%; Europe: 34.2%; Asia: 37.9% | FAO FRA, Global Forest Watch | Annual |
| Land-system change | Boreal forest cover, percentage of original forest | 100% | 85–60% | Americas: 56.6%; Eurasia: 70.3% | FAO FRA, Global Forest Watch | Annual |
| Freshwater change — blue water | Human-induced flow disturbance, 95th percentile deviation | 9.4% | 10.2–50% | 18.2% | WaterGAP, PCR-GLOBWB, GRDC | Annual |
| Freshwater change — green water | Soil-moisture deviation from Holocene variability, percentage of ice-free land | 9.8% | 11.1–50% | 15.8% | ESA CCI, GLEAM, ERA5 | Monthly |
| Atmospheric aerosol loading | Interhemispheric difference in aerosol optical thickness, AOD | 0.03 | 0.1–0.25 | 0.076 | MODIS, MISR, Copernicus CAMS | Monthly |
| Novel entities | Qualitative assessment of synthetic chemicals and novel entities released without adequate safety testing | 0 | Threshold not quantitatively defined | Exceeded | UNEP, OECD, national chemical registries | Annual review |

This table should be maintained as a versioned reference table.

**Each future release should record:**

• table version;\
• data year;\
• source update;\
• methodology note;\
• uncertainty note;\
• affected PHI domains;\
• affected MRV priorities;\
• affected boundary-pressure profiles;\
• change from prior release.

Where updated scientific assessments modify values, thresholds, or interpretations, Prometheus should update the table through the canonical release process.

**The table should therefore be understood as:**

global context for ecological intelligence;\
not automatic project-level proof.

A local Prometheus pilot may be inspired by planetary-boundary pressure, but its claims must still be grounded in site-level evidence, bioregional interpretation, MRV methodology, confidence scoring, and review.

**For example:**

• climate-boundary pressure may guide carbon, biomass, energy, and resilience indicators;\
• biosphere-integrity pressure may guide biodiversity, habitat, pollinator, and corridor indicators;\
• freshwater-boundary pressure may guide infiltration, retention, soil-moisture, irrigation, and watershed indicators;\
• biogeochemical-flow pressure may guide nutrient cycling, composting, fertilizer-dependency, and runoff indicators;\
• land-system-change pressure may guide agroforestry, soil cover, canopy structure, and regenerative land-use indicators;\
• novel-entities pressure may guide material safety, toxic-input reduction, chemical substitution, and contamination-risk review.

**This is how Prometheus connects planetary reality to local evidence:**

The planet gives the warning.

The bioregion gives the context.

The field gives the data.

MRV gives the verification.

PHI gives the synthesis.

PRU gives the value discipline.

**K.3 Bioregional interpretation**

Prometheus should interpret planetary-boundary logic through bioregional context.

A bioregion is not only a map.

It is a living pattern of climate, water, soil, biodiversity, culture, food systems, settlement, and ecological memory. Bioregional interpretation should consider:

• climate zone;\
• rainfall pattern;\
• drought risk;\
• flood risk;\
• watershed position;\
• soil type;\
• erosion risk;\
• salinity risk;\
• fire risk;\
• biodiversity corridors;\
• land-use history;\
• agricultural system;\
• cultural and community context;\
• food-system dependency;\
• infrastructure fragility;\
• governance capacity;\
• local knowledge.

This allows Prometheus to avoid universal metrics without context.

An indicator that matters in Sicily may not matter in the same way in Florida, the Andes, the Sahel, or a future off-world habitat. Prometheus therefore reads planetary boundaries through place. The principle is:

planetary science sets the horizon;\
bioregional reality sets the interpretation;\
local evidence sets the claim.

A sustained decline in PHI, even while absolute values remain above critical thresholds, may trigger a yellow protective review signal, prompting proactive governance, MRV, ecological, or operational review before negative trends become harder to reverse.

**K.4 Planetary Health Index logic**

Prometheus may use a Planetary Health Index, or PHI, as an analytical lens.

PHI should not be treated as a single sovereign number.

It should be treated as a composite decision-support framework.

**PHI may help organize ecological and systemic indicators across domains such as:**

• soil function;\
• water cycling;\
• biodiversity;\
• carbon dynamics;\
• productive resilience;\
• input dependency;\
• pollution reduction;\
• human nourishment;\
• community resilience;\
• governance maturity;\
• evidence quality;\
• continuity resilience.

**A PHI-style score may support:**

• baseline understanding;\
• pilot comparison;\
• bioregional diagnosis;\
• risk communication;\
• MRV prioritization;\
• PRU research;\
• RAP portfolio screening;\
• institutional diligence;\
• capital-readiness review.

**PHI should not automatically:**

• issue PRU value;\
• approve RAP inclusion;\
• trigger token issuance;\
• determine investor rights;\
• determine governance authority;\
• replace MRV;\
• replace scientific review;\
• replace local stewardship judgment.

PHI is a compass. It is not a court.

**K.5 PHI domain structure**

A Prometheus PHI framework may be organized into domains.

| **PHI domain** | **Example indicators** | **Use** |
|----|----|----|
| **Soil vitality** | Organic matter, soil carbon, aggregate stability, infiltration, microbial indicators. | Regenerative soil function. |
| **Water resilience** | Infiltration, retention, irrigation reduction, runoff reduction, water quality. | Hydrological stability. |
| **Biodiversity function** | Species richness, habitat structure, pollinators, functional diversity. | Ecological resilience. |
| **Carbon and biomass** | Biomass return, perennial cover, carbon storage proxies, energy/input reduction. | Climate-relevant performance. |
| **Productive resilience** | Yield diversity, input dependency, seasonal stability, edible biomass. | Food and economic viability. |
| **Pollution reduction** | Chemical inputs, waste loops, nutrient leakage, material safety. | Novel-entity and contamination risk. |
| **Community resilience** | Local participation, stewardship continuity, food sovereignty, skills. | Social-ecological durability. |
| **Governance maturity** | Role clarity, decision records, review pathways, conflict handling. | Institutional resilience. |
| **Evidence quality** | Provenance, confidence, sampling, independent review, admissibility. | Claim reliability. |
| **Continuity resilience** | Canonical records, offline archives, governance memory, recovery pathways. | Long-term system survival. |

Domains should be weighted according to methodology, site type, bioregion, pilot maturity, and intended use.

Weights should be documented.

Where weights are experimental, they should be labelled as research-use or pilot-use.

**K.6 PHI evidence maturity**

PHI should incorporate evidence maturity.

An indicator should not be scored with high confidence merely because it is important.

It should be scored according to evidence strength.

**A PHI record may therefore include:**

| **Evidence level** | **Meaning** |
|----|----|
| Narrative / claim | Meaningful but not yet evidenced. |
| Observation | Recorded field note, image, measurement, or testimony. |
| Evidence | Observation with provenance and method. |
| Validated indicator | Evidence passed quality checks. |
| MRV-admissible | Evidence accepted for reporting under scope. |
| PRU-eligible | Evidence may contribute to PRU analysis under methodology. |

This prevents PHI from becoming decorative scoring.

A low-confidence indicator should remain low confidence even if it is emotionally or strategically important.

Prometheus should reward evidence maturity, not rhetorical intensity.

**K.7 Composite scoring caution**

Composite indices can be useful.

They can also hide uncertainty.

Prometheus should therefore treat PHI scoring with caution.

**Composite PHI outputs should disclose:**

• indicator list;\
• data source;\
• methodology version;\
• weighting logic;\
• confidence level;\
• missing data;\
• uncertainty;\
• reviewer status;\
• intended use;\
• limitations;\
• update date.

A PHI score should never be presented without its evidence profile.

**The correct reporting format is not merely:**

PHI = 82.

**The correct reporting format is:**

PHI = 82\
methodology version: vX\
confidence: moderate\
missing data: biodiversity lab proxy unavailable\
review status: internal review\
use: pilot comparison only.

The number becomes credible only when its boundary travels with it.

**K.8 Boundary pressure profile**

Prometheus may create a Boundary Pressure Profile for each project or bioregion.

This profile maps where a site may reduce or increase pressure in relation to planetary-boundary domains.

**Possible profile categories:**

| **Profile category** | **Meaning** |
|----|----|
| Pressure-reducing | Evidence suggests intervention reduces pressure in a boundary domain. |
| Neutral / low relevance | Limited relationship to the boundary domain. |
| Pressure-uncertain | Evidence is insufficient or ambiguous. |
| Pressure-increasing risk | Potential negative effect requires review or mitigation. |
| High concern | Serious risk to ecological integrity or public claim reliability. |

**Boundary pressure profiles support:**

• design review;\
• MRV prioritization;\
• risk disclosure;\
• intervention redesign;\
• PRU analysis;\
• RAP portfolio screening;\
• investor diligence;\
• public communication discipline.

They should not become automatic enforcement. They are ecological intelligence for review.

**K.9 MRV Review and Verification Network**

**This network may include:**

• soil scientists;\
• ecologists;\
• hydrologists;\
• agroecologists;\
• biodiversity specialists;\
• remote-sensing analysts;\
• field auditors;\
• laboratories;\
• local stewards;\
• indigenous or traditional ecological knowledge holders where appropriate and consented;\
• bioregional experts;\
• methodology reviewers;\
• legal or reporting reviewers where necessary.

The network does not act as an oracle.

It reviews evidence.

It validates methods.

It identifies uncertainty.

It supports admissibility.

It improves interpretation.

It brings plural intelligence into disciplined review.

Prometheus does not need ecological prophecy.

It needs accountable ecological verification.

**K.10 Integration with MRV**

PHI and planetary-boundary logic should integrate with MRV through evidence classification.

**Each PHI or boundary-related indicator should reference:**

• data source;\
• methodology version;\
• evidence maturity;\
• confidence score;\
• reviewer status;\
• admissibility status;\
• reporting boundary;\
• update frequency.

**MRV should determine whether PHI-relevant evidence is:**

• claim;\
• observation;\
• evidence;\
• validated indicator;\
• research-use only;\
• MRV-admissible;\
• PRU-eligible;\
• non-admissible.

This keeps PHI from becoming a parallel authority.

PHI organizes ecological meaning.

MRV verifies ecological evidence.

**K.11 Integration with PRU**

PHI may inform PRU analysis only through approved methodology.

**Possible PHI-to-PRU relationships include:**

| **PHI role** | **PRU implication** |
|----|----|
| Contextual baseline | Helps describe initial ecological condition. |
| Indicator family | Helps select relevant indicators. |
| Risk screen | Flags ecological domains requiring stronger evidence. |
| Confidence input | May support confidence adjustment where methodology allows. |
| Performance signal | May contribute to PRU analysis if MRV-admissible. |
| Portfolio screen | May help compare PRU-relevant units for RAP review. |

PHI should not directly create PRU value. PHI may support PRU only when underlying indicators are evidence-backed, methodology-aligned, and admissible.

**The PRU rule remains:**

verified regenerative performance first;\
value-analysis interface second.

**K.12 Integration with RAP**

RAP logic may use PHI and planetary-boundary profiles for portfolio-level assessment.

**Possible uses include:**

• bioregional diversification;\
• ecological risk mapping;\
• boundary-pressure diversification;\
• climate and water-risk screening;\
• biodiversity exposure;\
• evidence-quality comparison;\
• governance maturity screening;\
• resilience weighting;\
• portfolio stress testing.

RAP should not aggregate weak claims into strong portfolio language.

A portfolio of weak evidence does not become strong evidence by being grouped.

RAP maturity depends on the maturity of underlying units.

PHI may improve portfolio intelligence.

It may not launder uncertainty.

**K.13 Integration with Ravel risk-signaling**

PHI and boundary-pressure profiles may support the Ravel Risk-Signaling Model.

**They may provide risk signals such as:**

• ecological stress increasing;\
• water vulnerability rising;\
• biodiversity evidence weak;\
• soil indicators improving slowly;\
• climate risk exposure high;\
• public claims exceeding boundary evidence;\
• portfolio concentrated in high-stress bioregions;\
• governance underprepared for ecological complexity.

Ravel may use these signals for review routing. Ravel should not use PHI as automatic enforcement.

**The correct relationship is:**

PHI informs ecological risk;\
Ravel routes systemic risk;\
governance reviews;\
MRV verifies;\
legal structures consequences where needed.

**K.14 Planetary Boundaries and capital readiness**

Planetary-boundary logic may strengthen capital readiness when used correctly.

Capital partners increasingly need to understand ecological risk, transition risk, climate resilience, natural capital dependency, physical risk, and environmental claims reliability.

**Prometheus may use boundary-aware reporting to show:**

• how a site reduces ecological pressure;\
• where risks remain;\
• which indicators are verified;\
• which indicators are emerging;\
• where further investment improves resilience;\
• how regenerative intervention affects long-term productive capacity;\
• how PRU-relevant performance may become more robust over time.

This makes capital more intelligent. It also makes claims more disciplined.

**A capital-facing boundary report should distinguish:**

• verified indicators;\
• preliminary signals;\
• risk assumptions;\
• methodology limitations;\
• scenario analysis;\
• non-admissible claims;\
• future research pathways.

Planetary language should increase diligence, not inflate valuation.

**K.15 Local knowledge and cultural context**

Planetary-boundary analysis should not erase local knowledge.

Local communities, farmers, stewards, indigenous knowledge holders, elders, field workers, and bioregional practitioners may understand patterns invisible to remote systems.

**Prometheus should include local knowledge where:**

• consent is obtained;\
• cultural context is respected;\
• attribution is handled properly;\
• data sovereignty is considered;\
• extractive knowledge capture is avoided;\
• evidence classification remains clear.

**Local knowledge may guide:**

• indicator selection;\
• seasonal interpretation;\
• water behavior;\
• species presence;\
• land-use history;\
• restoration design;\
• risk interpretation;\
• stewardship practice.

Local knowledge is not automatically MRV-admissible. It is also not inferior. It becomes part of the evidence pathway when structured, consented, contextualized, and reviewed.

**K.16 Network-independent planetary continuity**

Planetary-boundary and PHI knowledge should be preserved in network-independent forms.

**A Prometheus continuity archive may include:**

• local ecological baselines;\
• bioregional maps;\
• seed and species lists;\
• soil and water records;\
• climate-risk notes;\
• agroecological designs;\
• syntropic succession plans;\
• restoration protocols;\
• MRV methods;\
• PHI domain definitions;\
• planetary-boundary educational materials;\
• local-language stewardship manuals;\
• printed field protocols;\
• offline datasets;\
• institutional archive copies.

If the network fails, ecological memory must remain.

**A community should be able to continue stewardship with:**

paper maps;\
field notebooks;\
seed archives;\
soil knowledge;\
water knowledge;\
local governance memory;\
offline MRV forms;\
and a verified canonical package.

Prometheus survives when ecological intelligence can still be practiced without a dashboard.

**K.17 Suggested PHI record schema**

**A simplified PHI record may include:**

{

"phi_record_id": "phi_record_0001",

"site_id": "ohe_or_project_reference",

"bioregion": "bioregional_reference",

"methodology_version": "phi_method_v1.0",

"assessment_date": "2026-01-01",

"domains": \[

{

"domain": "soil_vitality",

"indicator": "soil_organic_matter",

"value": 3.2,

"unit": "%",

"evidence_status": "evidence",

"confidence": 0.76,

"review_status": "internal_review",

"data_reference": "mrv_record_hash"

}

\],

"boundary_pressure_profile": {

"climate_change": "pressure_reducing",

"biosphere_integrity": "pressure_uncertain",

"freshwater_change": "pressure_reducing"

},

"limitations": \[

"Biodiversity survey incomplete"

\],

"permitted_use": "pilot_comparison_only",

"pru_relevance": "not_yet_pru_eligible"

}

This schema is illustrative.

The operative schema should be versioned, tested, and aligned with the MRV methodology.

**K.18 Final planetary-boundary principle**

Prometheus uses planetary-boundary logic to remember scale.

It uses bioregional interpretation to remember place.

It uses PHI to organize ecological intelligence.

It uses MRV to verify evidence.

It uses PRU to translate verified regeneration into value logic.

It uses RAP to aggregate only what is mature enough to be aggregated.

It uses governance to keep these layers distinct.

**The final principle is:**

the planet gives context;\
the bioregion gives meaning;\
the field gives evidence;\
MRV gives verification;\
PRU gives value discipline;\
capital follows what life has made real.

Prometheus is planetary in awareness, bioregional in interpretation, local in evidence, and constitutional in fidelity.

# Appendix L: Integration Zome API Documentation

**L.1 Overview**

The Integration Zome is the controlled interface layer through which external systems, dashboards, applications, data services, institutional tools, MRV pipelines, and partner systems may interact with the h•eart•h Prometheus ecosystem.

Its purpose is not to create unrestricted external access. Its purpose is to provide structured, authenticated, review-bound, and constitutionally constrained integration pathways for data ingestion, evidence retrieval, query execution, operational reporting, governance interaction, and future interoperability.

**The Integration Zome should preserve the following principles:**

• source-layer fidelity;

• agent sovereignty;

• privacy and purpose limitation;

• syntropic sovereignty header validation;

• MRV provenance;

• no-double-counting discipline;

• public-claim boundaries;

• token-boundary separation;

• legal and jurisdictional review where relevant;

• Holochain-compatible agent-centric operation;

• review before protected-domain action.

The Integration Zome is designed to be protocol-flexible.

It may support REST, GraphQL, gRPC, webhooks, signed agent calls, internal bridge calls, and future integration methods where appropriate.

However, protocol flexibility does not mean authority flexibility.

All external requests remain subject to defined authentication, authorization, validation, rate limiting, logging, constitutional constraints, and protected-domain review.

The Integration Zome should be understood as a gateway to reviewable interoperability, not as a gateway to automatic rights, token issuance, financial execution, certification, PRU value creation, or legal status.

**The integration architecture may support the following API paradigms:**

| **Protocol** | **Primary use case** | **Advantages** | **Boundary** |
|----|----|----|----|
| REST | Simple data retrieval, public reports, webhook integrations, legacy-system connectivity, operational status endpoints | Universal compatibility, stateless operation, cacheable responses, broad developer familiarity | Suitable for low-risk and clearly bounded access; not sufficient by itself for protected-domain actions |
| GraphQL | Complex queries, dashboards, filtered reporting, internal tools, visualization interfaces | Efficient data fetching, typed schemas, flexible aggregation, subscription support | Query scope must respect permissions, privacy, disclosure classification, and public-claim boundaries |
| gRPC | High-throughput data exchange, inter-service communication, sensor ingestion, internal bridge communication | Streaming support, strong typing, high performance, suitable for infrastructure-level integration | Best used for authenticated infrastructure services and not as a public authority layer |
| Signed Holochain calls | Agent-centric operations, source-chain actions, DHT-published records, governance-relevant events | Cryptographic identity, agent sovereignty, contextual validation, source-chain integrity | Required for actions that affect agent records, governance records, MRV evidence, or protected status |
| Webhooks | Asynchronous notifications for external systems, dashboards, partners, auditors, and operational monitors | Event-driven integration, near-real-time updates, system interoperability | Webhooks notify; they do not authorize protected-domain action by themselves |

All API and integration pathways should validate syntropic sovereignty header requirements where applicable.

The syntropic sovereignty header ensures that requests, records, responses, and data packets remain connected to the constitutional principles of Prometheus, including non-commodification of life, agent sovereignty, source-layer constraints, evidence discipline, and the spiral of abundance.

**L.2 Authentication and Authorization**

All non-public API requests should be authenticated and authorized.

Authentication identifies the caller.

Authorization determines what the caller is permitted to do.

Prometheus should treat authentication and authorization as separate controls.

A valid identity does not automatically create permission.

A valid API key does not automatically create governance authority.

A valid partner credential does not automatically create public-claim authority.

A valid investor-data-room credential does not automatically create investor entitlement.

A valid system token does not automatically authorize protected-domain actions.

**L.2.1 Authentication Methods**

The Integration Zome may support multiple authentication methods, each appropriate for different risk levels and use cases.

| **Method** | **Description** | **Appropriate use case** | **Boundary** |
|----|----|----|----|
| API key | Simple token-based authentication for low-risk access | Public reports, dashboard queries, non-sensitive aggregated data, system health endpoints | Should not authorize protected-domain actions, private data access, governance decisions, or financial workflows |
| JWT | Time-limited signed token containing agent identity, session context, role claims, and permissions | User-authenticated requests, partner portals, dashboard access, limited governance interaction | Role claims must be verified against canonical governance or access records |
| Holochain agent signature | Cryptographic signature using an agent key, verified through Holochain-compatible identity logic | Agent-owned records, source-chain actions, MRV submissions, governance-relevant events, high-security operations | Preferred for agent-centric actions and protected record creation |
