AI may flag missing data.\
AI may draft a report.\
AI may propose questions for reviewers.

AI may not become the source of the evidence.

For every AI-assisted output used in MRV, PRU, RAP, investor reporting, governance, legal review or data-room preparation, the system must preserve:

• the source evidence;\
• the model or tool used;\
• the prompt or task class where relevant;\
• the output timestamp;\
• the reviewer trail;\
• the method version;\
• the confidence status;\
• the admissibility status;\
• the limitations and unresolved assumptions.

The evidence rule is:

AI output without source-layer evidence remains draft interpretation.

The admissibility rule is:

No AI-generated statement may enter PRU value analysis, RAP inclusion, investor-facing claims or legal-wrapper materials unless linked to evidence, method, review and admissibility status.

**W.1.5 AI Failure Modes and Protective Responses**

Prometheus recognizes the following AI and data-integrity failure modes:

| **Failure mode** | **Description** | **Protective response** |
|----|----|----|
| Hallucination | AI invents facts, methods, references, values or causal claims. | Source-required output, citation check, reviewer validation. |
| Data poisoning | Input data or reference material is corrupted or manipulated. | Hash checks, provenance review, anomaly detection, red-team sampling. |
| Sensor spoofing | Device outputs false or manipulated measurements. | Device identity, calibration logs, cross-source comparison. |
| False geolocation | Evidence is attributed to the wrong site or location. | Geotag validation, image metadata review, field confirmation. |
| Synthetic imagery | AI-generated or altered imagery is submitted as evidence. | Image provenance, metadata checks, human review, repeat observation. |
| Prompt injection | Malicious text attempts to override system constraints. | Tool isolation, prompt sanitation, policy guardrails, non-authority invariant. |
| Model drift | Model behavior changes across versions or over time. | Version pinning, regression tests, release notes, periodic validation. |
| Over-automation | AI output bypasses human, legal, MRV or governance review. | Workflow denial, authority gate, escalation requirement. |
| Selective sampling | Only favorable evidence is submitted or emphasized. | Sampling protocol, missing-data penalty, adverse-event disclosure. |
| Boundary collapse | AI turns research, narrative or forecast into admissible value. | Claims Register, evidence-before-value rule, reviewer gate. |

Any material AI failure affecting MRV, PRU, RAP, governance, legal review or investor-facing materials must trigger correction, disclosure and release review.

**W.1.6 Offline Resilience and Recovery**

Prometheus must be able to degrade gracefully. If cloud AI is unavailable, the system should fall back to local models, rule engines, cached references, offline checklists and human review. If internet access is unavailable, the system should continue local evidence capture and queue synchronization.

If a local model fails, deterministic schema checks and manual review should remain available.

If synchronization fails, local signed evidence should remain preserved until synchronization becomes possible.

If conflicting evidence appears during synchronization, the conflict should be flagged rather than silently overwritten. The resilience rule is:

No single cloud service, model provider, API endpoint, VPS, dashboard, runtime bridge or AI agent may become a single point of authority.

The minimum recovery pathway is:

local record -\> local hash -\> offline queue -\> reviewer note -\> later sync -\> conflict review -\> source-layer publication -\> governance or MRV review where required.

**W.1.7 Energy, Water and Cost Discipline**

Prometheus cannot claim regenerative alignment while using computation wastefully.

AI routing should consider:

• energy cost;\
• water impact of computation where relevant;\
• cloud dependency;\
• latency;\
• privacy exposure;\
• model size;\
• task necessity;\
• review burden;\
• operational cost;\
• field resilience.

The cost discipline rule is:

Use no model where rules are sufficient.\
Use a small model where parsing is sufficient.\
Use a local reasoner where contextual interpretation is sufficient.\
Use a verifier where consistency matters.\
Use frontier cloud models only for complex synthesis, institutional drafting or expert review support.

AI cost must be visible in institutional deployments. Budget caps, access tiers, fallback rules and review boundaries should be disclosed before capital-facing use.

**W.1.8 Non-Admissibility of AI-Only Outputs**

The following are non-admissible inside Prometheus:

• AI-only ecological claims;\
• AI-only PRU calculations;\
• AI-only RAP inclusion decisions;\
• AI-only maturity classifications;\
• AI-only investor-facing claims;\
• AI-only legal interpretations;\
• AI-only health or wellbeing claims;\
• AI-only public procurement claims;\
• AI-only insurance or risk-reduction claims;\
• AI-only governance eligibility or participant-ranking outputs.

AI may assist review. It may not replace the admissibility pathway. The admissibility pathway remains:

living system -\> observation -\> evidence -\> provenance -\> method -\> confidence -\> review -\> admissibility -\> PRU analysis -\> RAP review -\> legal structure -\> capital-facing use where lawful.

**W.1.9 W.1 Operating Invariant**

The W.1 operating invariant is:

Prometheus may accelerate observation, organization and learning.\
Prometheus may not accelerate beyond evidence, governance, legal structure or living-system fidelity.

This means:

symbols may move faster than life;\
markets may move faster than evidence;\
code may move faster than law;\
AI may move faster than governance; but Prometheus must move only as fast as its source-layer fidelity, evidence discipline, legal structure and living purpose allow.

Regeneration comes first.\
Financial meaning comes later.

## W.2 AI maintenance and validation cycle

Local models, schema validators and AI-routing policies should be reviewed at least semi-annually, or immediately after a major model, license, security or performance change. Deployment requires regression tests against known prompts, schema outputs, hallucination traps, privacy constraints, cost limits and no-authority invariants.

Model updates should not be silently deployed into capital-facing workflows. A model may assist drafting before it is approved for operational support; it may support operational support before it is allowed near institutional reporting; it never becomes authority.

**AI role boundary**

**Prometheus may use AI to assist with:**

parsing field notes;

normalizing operator text into structured events;

drafting reports;

flagging anomalies;

mapping evidence to Valueflows/hREA categories;

summarizing documentation;

generating hypotheses;

supporting user interfaces.

**AI must not:**

validate MRV evidence;

approve PRU values;

approve RAP maturity;

create legal rights;

override governance;

replace human review;

become the source of ecological truth;

silently change method versions;

convert Tier 3 material into admissible evidence.

### Regenerative Intelligence Metabolism

**Prometheus uses a hierarchical AI metabolism:**

| **Layer** | **AI role** | **Model class** | **Authority status** |
|----|----|----|----|
| Field Layer | no LLM; rules, thresholds, compact event encoding | deterministic/tiny classifier | no authority |
| Edge Gateway | parse, classify, compress, structure | Gemma 3n / Llama 3.2 1B-3B / Qwen small | assistant only |
| Local Node | MRV assistant, RAG, Valueflows mapping | Qwen 4B/8B or equivalent | assistant only |
| Verifier | logic/schema consistency check | Phi-like verifier | reviewer aid |
| Institutional Layer | draft reports, legal/science summaries | frontier cloud model on demand | draft only |
| Authority Layer | MRV, governance, legal, human review | no autonomous AI | controlling authority |

### Offline continuity

**Prometheus must preserve local operation under network degradation through:**

s.7 local event queue;

local evidence store;

human-readable export;

signed or hashable recovery bundles;

delayed synchronization;

conflict marking;

no dependency on cloud AI for core evidence preservation.

**Core rule:**

Prometheus must be able to think locally, remember locally, recover locally and synchronize globally only when connectivity is available.

## W.3 Structural Coherence and Single-Source Discipline 

Prometheus maintains one normative source for each material construct. Definitions, formulas, admissibility gates, AI authority boundaries, runtime roles and evidence rules must be expressed once, referenced consistently and interpreted through the canonical methodology of this White Paper.

The purpose of structural coherence is to prevent semantic drift. A PRU formula should not appear in multiple inconsistent forms. A confidence model should not be implied in one section and defined differently elsewhere. Tier 3 research context should not leak into capital-facing claims. AI interfaces should not appear as authority in operational sections after being bounded in the architecture.

Where a concept appears in more than one part of Prometheus, the later appearance should function as a contextual reference, not as a competing definition. This preserves legal readability, governance clarity, auditability, model consistency and institutional trust.

## W.4 Repository and Runtime Interface Boundary

Prometheus may be implemented through modular repositories, operational runbooks, runtime bridges, Holochain-compatible components, API endpoints, dashboards, gateway devices, model-routing tools and local evidence stores. These implementation assets support execution and traceability; they do not define ecological truth or financial admissibility by themselves.

Repository organization should mirror the canon architecture: methodology records, claims register, distributed workload model, bounded PRU value function, normalization operators, confidence model, validation protocol, causal identification strategy, adversarial MRV specification, portfolio-risk model, AI/offline boundary, reproducibility package and acceptance checklist should remain modular, versioned and checksum-verifiable.

The repository is an operational memory. The White Paper is the canon source. Runtime artifacts, scripts, endpoints, checksums and dashboards may support implementation, but methodology, authority, legal boundary and financial admissibility remain governed by the canon, evidence, review and applicable legal structure.

## W.5 Final Acceptance Checklist

A Prometheus release is acceptable for canonical governance only when the following conditions are satisfied: the full canon corpus is preserved; media and tables are conserved; Appendix A defines a distributed workload model; Appendix R contains the bounded PRU value function, normalization operators and Conf_total model; Appendix T contains the pre-registered validation protocol; Appendix U preserves frontier material as research-only context; Appendix V contains portfolio-risk and no-double-counting logic; Appendix W contains the AI/offline runtime architecture and authority boundary; and all material formulas, claims and implementation pathways remain reproducible, reviewable and legally bounded.

# Appendix X - Canon Release Gate and Runtime Synchronization Contract

## X.1 Canon release signature rule

A canon release is institutionally complete only when it is accompanied by a decision record and at least three independent approvals where available: one protocol steward or foundation custodian, one technical/MRV reviewer and one legal or governance reviewer. The release record should include file hash, method hash, date, approvers, dissent if any and scope of authority. A release may be technically complete while still awaiting governance approval. Technical completeness and canon approval are separate states.

**Purpose**

This appendix defines the conditions under which the Prometheus canon, runtime architecture, Holochain-compatible implementation workstream, VPS/deployment path and offline-resilience layer may be treated as synchronized for governance purposes. It is not empirical validation, production certification, securities qualification or institutional accreditation. It is a release-gate contract for coherence, traceability and operational readiness.

**Runtime evidence status**

The local Holochain runtime bridge, health endpoint, smoke-test endpoint, runtime runbook, automation scripts, status and shutdown scripts, VPS/systemd/Nginx deployment plan, templates, preflight checklist and real-VPS preflight path are implementation assets. They support runtime readiness and operational traceability. They do not validate PRU value, ecological claims, investment claims or institutional certification.

**VPS role**

A VPS may act as bootstrap, public API, dashboard, institutional bridge, uptime support and synchronization facilitator. It must not become the source of truth. The source of trust remains local evidence, Holochain-compatible source-layer records, MRV methodology, provenance, confidence, governance and legal review.

**Off-grid role**

Meshtastic, MeshCore, LoRa nodes, Reticulum, Raspberry Pi, mini-PC, Android gateways and local event stores are implementation options for field resilience. They support event capture, operator messaging, emergency signals, evidence queueing and delayed synchronization. They do not replace MRV, governance or legal review.

**AI model hierarchy**

Field Layer: deterministic rules, thresholds, event compression and no LLM. Edge Gateway: Gemma 3n/Gemma 3 1B, Llama 3.2 1B/3B and Qwen3 0.6B/1.7B may support parsing, normalization and classification. Prometheus Local Node: Qwen3 4B/8B and optional Phi-4-mini verifier may support MRV assistance, Valueflows/hREA mapping and schema checks. Institutional Layer: larger external models may support draft reports, legal/scientific review support and non-authoritative synthesis.

**No-authority invariant**

No AI model, VPS service, API route, dashboard, runtime bridge, zome call, GitHub workflow, checksum register, LoRa message, Reticulum packet or external assistant may approve PRU value, validate MRV evidence, create legal rights, issue tokens, approve RAP maturity, authorize ecological intervention or create investor-facing claims.

**Release acceptance conditions**

The canon release gate requires: structural preservation, media and table conservation, defined bounded PRU uplift, distributed workload interpretation, Conf_total specification, Appendix R integration, Appendix T validation protocol, Appendix U research boundary, Appendix W AI/offline runtime specification, runtime-evidence traceability and checksum-verifiable release artifacts.

**Canon rule**

This document is the current methodological canon edition for governance acceptance. It preserves the Prometheus canon while making the methodology computable, falsifiable, reproducible, adversarially reviewable, AI-bounded and offline-resilient.

# Appendix Y - Canon Integrity and Structural QA Report

This appendix records the canon integrity logic for the final integrated edition.

**Canon continuity**

The protected Prometheus canon corpus is preserved as the continuity corpus and interpretive base. Integrated methodology segments - Appendix 0, Appendix A, Appendix R, Appendix T, Appendix U, Appendix V, Appendix W, Appendix X and Appendix Y - are present as native Prometheus methodology and release-governance sections.

**Structural integrity**

The document preserves tables, images/media, constitutional language, access covenant, operational architecture, institutional boundaries, source-layer discipline and review-bound financial logic. The body language is written as current Prometheus architecture rather than as an external correction layer.

**Methodology integrity**

The current methodology includes: Claims Register; distributed workload model; bounded PRU value function; normalization operators; Bayesian-compatible confidence-weighted Conf_total; H1-H5 validation logic; causal identification strategy; Tier 3 research boundary; adversarial MRV; no-double-counting invariant; CVaR/time-positive risk logic; reproducibility package; red-team governance; AI/offline runtime architecture; and authority boundary.

# Appendix Z Executive Roadmap and Investor Navigation Guide

## Z.1 Purpose and Boundary

This appendix provides a high‑level navigation roadmap for institutional readers, capital allocators, family offices, fund managers, public procurement officers, and insurance/reinsurance partners. It does not replace the canonical source layer, evidence disciplines, or legal review. It is an orientation tool. The roadmap answers three core questions:

1.  What must be true before regenerative value becomes financeable?

2.  Which sections of the White Paper correspond to each step of the transformation pipeline?

3.  What are the mandatory gates and common pitfalls?

## Z.2 The Prometheus Transformation Pipeline – Visual Summary

The Prometheus transformation pipeline is not a conveyor belt from nature to finance.

It is a gated admissibility sequence.

Each step may mature only when the relevant evidence, integrity, legal, market, runtime and governance conditions are satisfied.

The visual summary is:

Living system\
→ regenerative process\
→ observation\
→ evidence\
→ provenance\
→ integrity review\
→ verified indicator\
→ Claims Register status\
→ admissibility\
→ PRU value analysis\
→ PRU maturity classification\
→ RAP aggregation\
→ Anchor Demand File\
→ legal wrapper\
→ runtime support where externally relied upon\
→ market-active pathway where admitted\
→ capital allocation\
→ reinvestment\
→ expanded regeneration.

This sequence is a discipline, not a guarantee.

It does not mean that every living system becomes a candidate for institutional structuring.

It does not mean that every verified indicator becomes PRU value.

It does not mean that every PRU becomes RAP-ready.

It does not mean that every RAP becomes market-active.

It does not mean that every legal wrapper creates liquidity.

It does not mean that every dashboard, API, zome, runtime module or repository release creates external reliance.

For each step, the table below identifies the primary White Paper sections, the evidence maturity required and the controlling institutional gate.

| **Step** | **Primary sections** | **Required maturity** | **Controlling gate** |
|----|----|----|----|
| Living system, OHE or regenerative unit | Part III, Appendix M | Site existence, ecological boundary, stewardship context and baseline readiness. | Site existence and baseline gate. |
| Regenerative process | Part III, Part V, Appendix M | Defined intervention, management logic, ecological rationale and operator responsibility. | Operational stewardship gate. |
| Observation | Part IV, Appendix G | Raw data, timestamp, location, source, device / human origin, unit and observation context. | Observation-recording gate. |
| Evidence | Part IV, Appendix G | Data plus method, provenance, reviewer trail, missing-data notes and evidence lineage. | Evidence gate. |
| Provenance | Part IV, Appendix G, Appendix 0 | Method hash, data hash, source, timestamp, location, chain of custody and Claims Register link. | Provenance gate. |
| Integrity review | Technical Validation Boundary, Appendix Q, Appendix 0 | No-double-counting invariant, event identity tuple, duplicate-claim detection and threat model where applicable. | Integrity gate. |
| Verified indicator | Part IV, Appendix G, Appendix R | Quality checks, confidence scoring, normalization and disclosed uncertainty. | Indicator-validation gate. |
| Claims Register status | Appendix 0 | Claim ID, type, status, admissibility class and required corrective action. | Claims Register gate. |
| Admissibility | Part VIII, Appendix R, Appendix 0 | Methodological, validation, threshold, legal-status and claim-status gates satisfied. | Admissibility gate. |
| PRU value analysis | Part VI, Appendix R, Appendix V | Bounded PRU function, U_max rationale, Conf_total, sensitivity analysis and reproducibility package. | Valuation gate. |
| PRU maturity classification | Part VI, Appendix R, Appendix R.25 | PRU-R status assigned through reviewed evidence and bounded methodology. | PRU maturity gate. |
| RAP aggregation | Part VI, Appendix R.25, Appendix V | Eligible PRUs aggregated with diversification logic, portfolio-risk review and data-room support. | RAP-M4 portfolio-readability gate. |
| Anchor Demand File | Executive Summary, Part X, Appendix R, Appendix Z | Counterparty qualification, ADE classification, demand rationale, price-discovery memo, liquidity / exit disclosure and Claims Register update. | Market gate / RAP-M5 counterparty-reviewability. |
| Legal wrapper | Part VIII, Appendix S, Appendix Q | Jurisdiction-specific legal review, rights mapping, disclosure package, instrument terms and regulatory classification. | Legal gate / RAP-M6 legal wrapping. |
| Runtime support where externally relied upon | Gate 5, Appendix W, Data Room Governance, Appendix 0 | Runtime Verification and Release Safety Package, release decision record, test evidence, security review where applicable, rollback, incident response, monitoring and Claims Register alignment. | Runtime-support rule. |
| Market-active pathway where admitted | Part VI, Part VII, Appendix R.25, Appendix Z | Binding instrument, closed financing, binding offtake, insurance / procurement / public-program pathway or operationally effective external demand. | Market gate / RAP-M7 market-active status. |
| Capital allocation | Part VI, Part VII, Appendix Q, Appendix S, Appendix Z | Capital deployment only within admitted evidence, integrity, legal, market, disclosure, custody / settlement and governance boundaries. | Capital-facing gate. |
| Reinvestment | Part VII, Appendix R, governance sections | Net surplus, fee model, stewardship reinvestment, maintenance, ecological improvement and community-capacity allocation tracked. | Reinvestment governance gate. |
| Expanded regeneration | Part III, Part VI, Part VII, Appendix V | Scaling only after evidence, operational resilience, governance capacity, legal clarity and market-readiness remain coherent under replication. | Scaling and coherence gate. |

The pipeline must be read with the following rules:

Evidence precedes value.

Integrity precedes representation.

Legal review precedes rights.

Anchor demand precedes market-facing language.

Runtime verification precedes external software reliance.

Governance review precedes capital-facing use.

No PRU, RAP, token, dashboard, API, data-room record, legal wrapper, runtime release or market narrative may bypass the relevant gate.

A living system is not automatically an asset.

Evidence is not automatically value.

Value analysis is not automatically a right.

A PRU score is not automatically investable.

A RAP structure is not automatically market-ready.

A legal wrapper is not automatically liquid.

A runtime component is not automatically production-admitted.

A data room is not automatically external demand.

Capital allocation is permitted only when the applicable evidence, valuation, integrity, legal, market, runtime, custody, settlement, operational and governance dependencies are documented, reviewed and bounded. The lowest unresolved gate controls the permitted claim.

## Z.3 Investor Decision Flowchart (Textual)

Ask → Is there a living regenerative system, OHE or equivalent regenerative unit?

- → No: Not investable. No PRU, RAP or capital-facing claim may be made.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Is there a defined site boundary, baseline condition and operational stewardship record?

- → No: Classify as research concept or pre-baseline design only.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Is there baseline and ongoing MRV evidence with provenance?

- → No: Classify as research/pilot only. Not investable.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Is the relevant claim registered in the Claims Register?

- → No: Non-admissible for financial, institutional, investor-facing, insurance-facing, procurement-facing, dashboard-facing, API-facing or public reliance.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Does the claim require pilot evidence under Gate 1?

- → Yes, and pilot evidence is absent: claim remains hypothesis-level, pilot-required and non-admissible for PRU uplift, RAP formation or investor-facing use.

- → Yes, and pilot evidence is present: proceed only within the evidence scope, confidence range, reviewer trail and Claims Register status.

- → No: Proceed to evidence admissibility review.

Ask → Does the evidence package include method hash, data hash, source, timestamp, location, reviewer trail, missing-data notes and confidence model?

- → No: Evidence is incomplete. Return to MRV and data-room completion.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Do indicators pass the applicable methodological, validation, threshold, legal-status and Claims Register admissibility gates?

- → No: Non-admissible for PRU analysis. Return to MRV, methodology review or legal review as applicable.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Is there an applicable integrity or no-double-counting exposure?

Examples include token representation, bridge logic, wrapped representation, registry synchronization, custody, settlement, oracle dependency, dashboard state, API state, external verifier, credit registry or multi-ledger reporting.

- → No: Proceed.

- → Yes: Proceed only if the no-double-counting invariant, event identity tuple, cross-system representation tuple where applicable, No-Double-Spending Threat Model, duplicate-claim detection rule, security review where applicable, quarantine pathway, governance decision and Claims Register status are complete.

- → If incomplete: internal or sandbox status only. No external representation.

Ask → Does PRU value analysis produce a reproducible score with bounded PRU function, U_max rationale, Conf_total, admissibility gates and sensitivity analysis disclosed?

- → No: PRU-R2 or lower. Not capital-ready.

- → Yes: PRU-R3 or higher. Proceed.

Ask → Is the PRU maturity class at least PRU-R4 or PRU-R5?

- → No: Internal analytical use only.

- → Yes: Eligible for portfolio screening.

Ask → Is there a RAP structure aggregating eligible units with RAP-M4 portfolio-readability?

- → No: Not portfolio-ready.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Is there an Anchor Demand File supporting RAP-M5 counterparty-reviewability?

- → No: Institutionally reviewable but not market-facing.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Is there a jurisdiction-specific legal wrapper, rights mapping, disclosure package and legal review supporting RAP-M6?

- → No: Not investable. No enforceable investor rights, token rights, land rights, revenue rights, custody rights, settlement rights or transferable claims may be implied.

- → Yes: Proceed.

Ask → Is any runtime component, dashboard, API endpoint, zome, schema, AI/offline module, bridge connector, registry connector, data pipeline, VPS deployment, repository release or software build being relied upon externally?

- → No: Proceed without runtime reliance, provided no runtime claim is made.

- → Yes: Proceed only if the Runtime Verification and Release Safety Package, runtime and release safety data-room layer, Runtime Release Decision Record, governance approval and Claims Register alignment support the claimed runtime status.

- → If incomplete: the runtime component remains non-admitted. No production-ready, production-admitted, externally relied-upon, runtime-verified, security-reviewed, dashboard-certified, API-certified, PRU-certified by software, RAP-certified by software, legally admissible by code, market-ready by dashboard or token-ready by runtime claim may be made.

Ask → Is there a binding instrument, closed financing, binding offtake, binding insurance / procurement / public-program pathway, legally effective counterparty arrangement or operationally effective external demand supporting RAP-M7?

- → No: Not market-active. May remain legally wrapped or counterparty-reviewable only.

- → Yes: Market-active only within the relevant instrument, counterparty, jurisdiction, disclosure package, custody / settlement pathway where applicable, governance decision and Claims Register status.

Final interpretation:

- PRU-R5 means reviewed analytical maturity, not market-readiness.

- RAP-M4 means portfolio-readable, not market-readable.

- RAP-M5 means counterparty-reviewable, not binding demand.

- RAP-M6 means legally wrapped, not liquid.

- RAP-M7 means market-active only within the admitted legal, disclosure, counterparty and Claims Register boundary.

- Runtime admission supports software reliance only within its admitted scope.

- The lowest unresolved evidence, valuation, integrity, legal, market, runtime, security, custody, settlement, operational or governance gate controls the permitted claim.

## Z.4 Mandatory Gates for Capital‑Facing Use 

Prometheus may become capital-facing only when the relevant claim, pathway or instrument satisfies the mandatory gates below. These gates are cumulative. No gate replaces another gate. No maturity label, dashboard output, runtime component, legal wrapper, data-room record, counterparty conversation, portfolio model or governance decision may bypass an unresolved gate.

The controlling rule is: lowest unresolved gate, lowest permitted claim.

| **Gate** | **Required evidence** | **Consequence if not met** |
|----|----|----|
| Evidence gate | MRV records with method hash, data hash, source, timestamp, location, reviewer trail, missing-data notes, confidence model, Claims Register link and applicable pilot-evidence status. | Claims must not be described as observed, verified, pilot-supported, admissible, PRU-relevant, RAP-relevant, insurance-facing, procurement-facing, investor-facing or capital-facing beyond the documented evidence scope. |
| Valuation gate | Bounded PRU function, U_max rationale, Conf_total, normalized indicators, admissibility gates, sensitivity analysis, reproducibility package, reviewer trail and disclosed uncertainty. | No PRU value may be represented as unbounded, guaranteed, self-validating, investor-return-equivalent, cash-flow-equivalent, asset-backed, token-backed, market-ready or financially admissible by itself. |
| Integrity gate | No-double-counting invariant, event identity tuple, cross-system representation tuple where applicable, No-Double-Spending Threat Model, duplicate-claim detection rule, bridge/oracle/finality/replay risk review where applicable, quarantine pathway, adverse integrity record procedure, governance decision and Claims Register update. | No PRU, RAP, credit, certificate, token, credential, dashboard state, API state, registry entry, custody record, settlement record, bridge message or wrapped representation may be described as duplicate-proof, double-spend-resistant, bridge-ready, cross-chain-ready, registry-synchronised, custody-ready, settlement-ready, transferable or externally admissible unless the integrity controls support that classification. |
| Legal gate | Jurisdiction-specific legal review, disclosure package, instrument terms, rights mapping, regulatory classification, investor-protection analysis, tax / securities review where applicable and legal-opinion status. | No PRU, RAP, token, dashboard, certificate, data-room record, access pathway, governance pathway, revenue pathway, land-related pathway, fund interest, note, SPV interest, digital representation or bundled structure creates enforceable rights by itself. |
| Market gate | Anchor Demand File with ADE classification, qualified counterparty record, binding / non-binding status, price-discovery memo, liquidity and exit disclosures, legal review status, custody / settlement pathway where applicable, data-room completeness, governance decision and Claims Register update. | No RAP may be described as market-ready, investable, liquid, offtake-backed, buyer-validated, insurance-recognised, procurement-ready, secondary-market-ready or portfolio-deployable unless the Anchor Demand File, RAP-M Decision Record, legal review, disclosure package, data-room record and Claims Register status support that classification. |
| Runtime-support rule | Runtime Verification and Release Safety Package, runtime and release safety data-room layer, source commit hash, build artefact hash, release version, test-suite reference, security review where applicable, rollback procedure, incident-response procedure, monitoring plan, Runtime Release Decision Record, governance approval and Claims Register alignment. | No zome, schema, dashboard, API endpoint, AI/offline module, bridge connector, registry connector, data pipeline, VPS deployment, repository release or software build may be described as production-ready, production-admitted, externally relied-upon, runtime-verified, security-reviewed, dashboard-certified, API-certified, PRU-certified by software, RAP-certified by software, legally admissible by code, market-ready by dashboard or token-ready by runtime unless the runtime-support rule is satisfied within the admitted scope. |

The runtime-support rule is not a substitute for evidence, valuation, integrity, legal or market review.

It applies whenever a runtime component, dashboard, API, zome, schema, AI/offline module, bridge connector, registry connector, data pipeline, VPS deployment, repository release or software build is used to support external reliance, investor-facing reporting, market-facing language, Claims Register status, PRU calculation, RAP reporting, token representation, custody, settlement or registry synchronization.

Where no runtime component is externally relied upon, no runtime readiness claim may be made.

Where runtime is externally relied upon, runtime admission is mandatory.

The mandatory-gate interpretation is:

Evidence gate controls what may be claimed.

Valuation gate controls what may be calculated.

Integrity gate controls what may be represented.

Legal gate controls what may create rights.

Market gate controls what may face demand, liquidity or investor-readiness language.

Runtime-support rule controls what software output may be relied upon.

A PRU-R score does not pass the market gate.

A RAP-M score does not pass the legal gate.

A legal wrapper does not pass the evidence gate.

A data room does not pass the market gate.

A blockchain or Holochain-compatible record does not pass the integrity gate.

A dashboard or API does not pass the runtime-support rule by existing.

A runtime release does not pass Gate 1, Gate 2, Gate 3 or Gate 4.

No capital-facing use is permitted unless the applicable evidence, valuation, integrity, legal, market, runtime, security, custody, settlement, operational and governance dependencies are disclosed, reviewed and bounded.

The lowest unresolved gate controls the permitted capital-facing claim.

| **Gate** | **Required Evidence** | **Consequence if not met** |
|----|----|----|
| Evidence gate | MRV records with method hash, data hash, source, timestamp, location, reviewer trail, missing‑data notes | Claims must not be described as observed or verified. |
| Valuation gate | Bounded PRU function, U_max rationale, Conf_total, admissibility gates, sensitivity analysis | No PRU value may be represented as unbounded, guaranteed or self‑validating. |
| Legal gate | Jurisdiction‑specific legal review, disclosure, instrument terms and rights mapping | No PRU, RAP, token or dashboard creates investor rights by itself. |

**Z.4A Legal Gate Navigation for Capital-Facing Use**

This section provides the legal navigation test for any reader assessing whether a Prometheus pathway remains architectural, analytical or operational, or whether it has become legally instrumented and potentially capital-facing.

The legal gate must be reviewed before any Prometheus-related PRU, RAP, TRBK, HoloFuel reference, dashboard status, certificate, token representation, digital record, access pathway, governance pathway, revenue pathway, land-related pathway, fund structure, note, SPV interest or bundled structure is described as rights-bearing, investor-facing, market-facing or capital-facing.

Readers evaluating Prometheus capital-readiness shall verify:

1.  whether the pathway is architectural, analytical, operational, governance-related or legally instrumented;

2.  whether jurisdiction-specific legal review exists;

3.  whether enforceable rights have been mapped;

4.  whether instrument terms have been drafted where required;

5.  whether disclosure has been prepared;

6.  whether marketing language has been reviewed;

7.  whether investor eligibility has been defined where applicable;

8.  whether custody and settlement issues have been reviewed where applicable;

9.  whether tax, AML/KYC, sanctions and data-protection issues have been reviewed where applicable;

10. whether governance has admitted the pathway;

11. whether the Claims Register reflects the correct legal status;

12. whether the relevant legal data-room artefacts are complete.

A pathway without legal review remains architectural, analytical or operational only.

It is not capital-facing.

It is not investor-facing.

It is not rights-bearing.

It is not market-facing.

It is not token-facing.

It is not instrument-ready.

PRU maturity does not create legal rights.

RAP maturity does not create liquidity.

Token representation does not create investor entitlement.

Dashboard status does not create legal status.

Claims Register status does not create enforceable rights.

Governance approval does not replace jurisdiction-specific legal review.

The legal gate is passed only when legal review, instrument terms, rights mapping, disclosure, counsel sign-off, governance admission and Claims Register status are complete for the intended use.

Where any of these elements is missing, the pathway must remain classified as non-capital-facing and non-rights-bearing.

**Z.4B Anchor Demand and Market Validation Navigation**

This section provides the market-validation navigation test for any reader assessing whether a Regenerative Asset Pool remains portfolio-readable, has become counterparty-reviewable, has become legally wrapped, or has become market-active.

A RAP is not market-ready merely because it aggregates PRU logic.

A RAP is not investable merely because its evidence architecture is coherent.

A RAP is not liquid merely because it is diversified.

A RAP is not offtake-backed merely because a counterparty has expressed interest.

A RAP is not insurance-recognised merely because an insurance pathway has been discussed.

A RAP is not procurement-ready merely because public-program relevance exists.

A RAP is not portfolio-deployable merely because a data room exists.

Market validation requires documented external demand.

Readers evaluating RAP market-readiness shall verify:

1.  whether the RAP is only internally portfolio-readable;

2.  whether a qualified external counterparty has been identified;

3.  whether the counterparty category has been defined;

4.  whether the counterparty has authority, mandate or institutional relevance;

5.  whether demand evidence is documented;

6.  whether the evidence is binding or non-binding;

7.  whether any letter of interest, memorandum of understanding, procurement indication, insurer feedback, bank feedback, public-program pathway or equivalent document exists;

8.  whether price discovery has been separated from ecological performance claims;

9.  whether productive cash-flow value, ecological-performance value, credits, public incentives, contractual revenue, optional upside and scenario-only values are separated;

10. whether liquidity limitations are disclosed;

11. whether exit limitations are disclosed;

12. whether hold-period assumptions are disclosed;

13. whether regulated counterparty, custody or settlement pathways are required;

14. whether legal review has been completed for the intended market-facing use;

15. whether the Anchor Demand File has been linked to the Claims Register;

16. whether governance has admitted the claimed market-readiness level.

The following interpretation rules are mandatory:

Portfolio-readable means internally structured for diligence.

Counterparty-reviewable means external interest has been documented.

Legally wrapped means the relevant legal structure has been reviewed or prepared.

Market-active means a binding or operationally effective external pathway exists.

Portfolio-readable does not mean market-readable.

Counterparty-reviewable does not mean binding demand.

A letter of interest does not mean offtake.

A memorandum of understanding does not mean liquidity.

A legal wrapper does not mean market demand.

A data room does not mean transaction readiness.

A price-discovery memo does not create market price.

A qualified counterparty file does not create investor protection.

A public-program pathway does not create procurement award.

An insurer conversation does not create coverage.

A bank review does not create financing.

No RAP may be described as market-active unless the corresponding Anchor Demand File, legal review, disclosure package, counterparty record, data-room evidence, governance decision and Claims Register status support that classification.

Where any required element is missing, the RAP shall remain at the lowest defensible market-readiness level.

No forced progression to market-active status is permitted.

The absence of market validation must be disclosed.

The correct investor-navigation principle is:

RAP-M4 is reviewable architecture.

RAP-M5 is documented external interest.

RAP-M6 is legal structuring.

RAP-M7 is binding or operationally effective market activation.

Only RAP-M7 may support market-active language, and only within the limits of the relevant legal instrument, counterparty agreement, disclosure package, jurisdiction and Claims Register status.

**Z.4C Interoperability Integrity and No-Double-Spending Navigation**

This section provides the integrity-navigation test for any reader assessing whether a Prometheus pathway remains internal, sandboxed or architectural, or whether it may be externally represented through a bridge, token, registry, API, dashboard, custody rail, settlement rail or cross-system pathway.

No PRU reference, RAP reference, evidence package, claim, credit, certificate, token representation, wrapped representation, bridge message, oracle-dependent state, registry entry, custody record, settlement record, dashboard state or API response may be treated as externally admissible unless the no-double-spending controls are complete.

Readers evaluating interoperability readiness shall verify:

1.  whether the underlying regenerative event has a complete event identity tuple;

2.  whether the evidence package is linked to method hash, data hash, source, time, location and reviewer trail;

3.  whether the Claims Register entry exists and reflects the correct status;

4.  whether the representation scope is defined;

5.  whether the source system and target system are identified;

6.  whether the cross-system representation tuple exists;

7.  whether the pathway depends on a bridge, oracle, external registry, custody rail, settlement rail, dashboard, API or token representation;

8.  whether finality assumptions have been documented;

9.  whether replay protection exists where applicable;

10. whether lock / mint / burn / release logic has been tested where applicable;

11. whether duplicate-claim detection is active;

12. whether conflict-resolution rules exist;

13. whether revocation and quarantine pathways exist;

14. whether any adverse integrity record exists;

15. whether security review has been completed;

16. whether legal review has been completed where rights, transferability, custody, settlement, market-facing use or external reliance may arise;

17. whether the Interoperability Integrity Decision Record supports the claimed status;

18. whether the data-room layer supports the representation;

19. whether governance has admitted the pathway;

20. whether public, investor-facing, token-facing, insurance-facing, procurement-facing or market-facing language remains within the admitted scope.

The following interpretation rules are mandatory:

A Holochain-compatible record does not by itself prevent cross-system duplication.

A blockchain transaction does not by itself prove ecological uniqueness.

A bridge event does not by itself prove admissibility.

A token wrapper does not by itself create valid representation.

An oracle does not create ecological truth.

A custody record does not create PRU validity.

A dashboard state does not create legal, market or settlement status.

A registry entry does not prevent incompatible reuse.

A cross-chain message does not prove that the source event remains valid, unspent, unrevoked and unclaimed elsewhere.

The following pathway statuses shall be distinguished:

| **Status** | **Meaning** | **External-use consequence** |
|----|----|----|
| Internal architecture | Design logic exists but no tested external representation exists | No external reliance |
| Sandbox-tested | Pathway has been tested in a non-production environment | No capital-facing, investor-facing or market-facing use |
| Reviewable pathway | Threat model, event identity, representation tuple and risk review exist | External review only within disclosed limits |
| Conditionally admitted pathway | Security, legal where applicable, data-room, governance and Claims Register controls support limited use | Use only within stated scope |
| Production-admitted pathway | Full controls, tests, reviews, governance admission and Claims Register status support external use | External representation permitted only within admitted boundaries |

No pathway may be described as bridge-ready, cross-chain-ready, duplicate-proof, double-spend-resistant, registry-synchronised, custody-ready, settlement-ready, token-facing, transferable, externally admissible or production-ready unless the corresponding No-Double-Spending Threat Model, Interoperability Integrity Decision Record, security review, data-room record, legal review where applicable and Claims Register status support that classification.

Where any required element is missing, incomplete, expired, inconsistent, quarantined or contradicted by an adverse integrity record, the pathway shall remain non-admitted.

The correct investor-navigation principle is:

no event identity tuple;

no external PRU or RAP reference.

no representation tuple;

no cross-system use.

no threat model;

no bridge.

no security review;

no production admission.

no legal review where applicable;

no transferability or rights-bearing claim.

no Claims Register status;

no public reliance.

The lowest unresolved integrity gate controls the permitted representation.

**Z.4D Runtime Verification and Release Safety Navigation**

This section provides the runtime-navigation test for any reader assessing whether a Prometheus software component remains conceptual, sandboxed, internally testable, release-candidate, production-admitted or externally relied upon.

Runtime implementation does not create institutional admissibility by itself.

A repository does not create production readiness.

A source commit does not create release admission.

A successful build does not create operational resilience.

A local test does not create external reliance.

A dashboard does not create claim authority.

An API response does not create legal, market, evidentiary or financial authority.

A zome validation callback does not create full-system assurance.

A VPS deployment does not create institutional infrastructure.

An AI/offline module does not create ecological truth, legal rights, PRU value, RAP maturity, market demand, investor entitlement, governance authority, no-double-spending assurance or production safety.

Readers evaluating runtime readiness shall verify:

1.  whether the component has a defined component identifier;

2.  whether the component has a repository reference;

3.  whether the source commit hash is recorded;

4.  whether the release version is recorded;

5.  whether the build artefact hash is recorded;

6.  whether the schema version is recorded where applicable;

7.  whether the validation rule set is defined;

8.  whether the test-suite reference exists;

9.  whether test results are recorded;

10. whether static analysis has been completed or its absence justified;

11. whether dependency review has been completed;

12. whether security review has been completed;

13. whether migration review has been completed where applicable;

14. whether adversarial test cases have been run;

15. whether failure-mode analysis exists;

16. whether offline recovery has been tested where applicable;

17. whether synchronization conflict testing has been completed where applicable;

18. whether data-integrity testing has been completed;

19. whether no-double-counting testing has been completed where applicable;

20. whether access-control review has been completed;

21. whether key-management review has been completed where applicable;

22. whether logging and audit-trail review has been completed;

23. whether rollback procedure exists;

24. whether incident-response procedure exists;

25. whether monitoring record or monitoring plan exists;

26. whether the Runtime Verification and Release Safety Package exists;

27. whether the runtime and release safety data-room layer supports the claimed status;

28. whether the Runtime Release Decision Record exists;

29. whether the Claims Register entries affected by the release are identified;

30. whether cross-gate dependencies have been disclosed;

31. whether unresolved evidence, integrity, legal, market, security, operational, custody, settlement, data-protection or governance gates limit the permitted runtime claim;

32. whether the Independent Methodology Committee or functionally equivalent review body has admitted the requested runtime status;

33. whether permitted runtime language has been defined;

34. whether prohibited runtime language has been defined;

35. whether expiry, review, downgrade or revocation conditions exist.

Prometheus distinguishes the following runtime states:

1.  Design specification;

2.  Prototype;

3.  Sandbox build;

4.  Internal test build;

5.  Release candidate;

6.  Production-admitted release;

7.  Externally relied-upon release.

The following interpretation rules are mandatory:

Design specification means the component is described, not implemented.

Prototype means the component may demonstrate function but is not operational infrastructure.

Sandbox build means the component may be tested in a non-production environment.

Internal test build means the component may be used for controlled internal validation.

Release candidate means the component is under final review but is not externally admitted.

Production-admitted release means the component has passed the relevant Runtime Verification and Release Safety Package, release decision record, governance review and Claims Register alignment within the admitted scope.

Externally relied-upon release means the component may be relied upon outside the internal Prometheus environment only within the stated evidence, integrity, legal, market, operational, security and governance boundaries.

No Prometheus material may collapse these stages.

A prototype is not production.

A sandbox build is not institutional infrastructure.

An internal test build is not external admission.

A release candidate is not production admission.

A production-admitted release is not automatically externally relied upon.

An externally relied-upon release is not automatically legally admissible, market-ready, token-ready, custody-ready or settlement-ready.

A dashboard release does not authorize claims beyond the Claims Register.

An API release does not authorize claims beyond the Claims Register.

A runtime release does not bypass Gate 1, Gate 2, Gate 3 or Gate 4.

Where a component affects PRU calculation, RAP status, Claims Register status, data-room evidence, legal status, token representation, bridge logic, registry synchronization, custody, settlement, market-facing language or investor-facing reporting, the runtime pathway must also satisfy the relevant evidence, integrity, legal, market and governance gates.

The following language is prohibited unless the Runtime Verification and Release Safety Package, Runtime Release Decision Record, data-room layer, governance decision and Claims Register status support the claim:

- production-ready;

- production-admitted;

- externally relied-upon;

- institutionally deployed;

- runtime-verified;

- security-reviewed;

- audit-ready;

- formally verified;

- fail-safe;

- tamper-proof;

- autonomous authority;

- dashboard-certified;

- API-certified;

- MRV-certified by runtime;

- PRU-certified by software;

- RAP-certified by software;

- legally admissible by code;

- market-ready by dashboard;

- token-ready by runtime;

- custody-ready by runtime;

- settlement-ready by runtime.

Permitted pre-admission language includes:

- design specification;

- prototype;

- sandbox build;

- internal test build;

- release candidate;

- runtime specification;

- validation logic under review;

- test suite under development;

- security review pending;

- release decision pending;

- production admission pending;

- governance release pending;

- external reliance not yet admitted.

The correct investor-navigation principle is:

runtime can support evidence, governance, reporting and coordination only within its admitted scope.

Runtime does not create the claim.

Runtime does not validate the claim.

Runtime does not finance the claim.

Runtime does not legalize the claim.

Runtime does not market-activate the claim.

Runtime may support a claim only when the underlying evidence, integrity, legal, market, governance and Claims Register status support that claim.

Where the Runtime Release Decision Record is absent, incomplete, expired, inconsistent with the data room, inconsistent with the Claims Register, contradicted by an incident record or unsupported by the Runtime Verification and Release Safety Package, the component shall remain non-admitted.

The lowest unresolved runtime, evidence, integrity, legal, market, security, operational, custody, settlement, data-protection or governance gate controls the permitted runtime claim.

## Z.5 Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them 

**Z.5 Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them**

The most common institutional failure mode is not absence of ambition.

It is premature translation.

Prometheus fails institutionally when an analytical, ecological, operational, legal, market, integrity or runtime status is translated into a stronger status than the evidence, Claims Register, data room, legal review, market validation or runtime release record can support.

| **Pitfall** | **Consequence** | **Avoidance** |
|----|----|----|
| Treating PRU-R5 as market-ready. | Misleading investors; confusion between analytical maturity and market maturity. | Use RAP-M stages for market readiness. PRU-R5 is analytical review, not market-readiness. |
| Treating RAP-M4 as market-active. | Portfolio structure may be mistaken for demand, liquidity or investability. | RAP-M4 means portfolio-readable only. Market-facing language requires RAP-M5 or higher, and market-active language requires RAP-M7. |
| Treating RAP-M5 as binding demand. | Non-binding interest may be misread as offtake, financing, liquidity or revenue certainty. | RAP-M5 means counterparty-reviewable only. Distinguish LOI, MoU, insurer feedback, procurement indication and binding agreement. |
| Treating RAP-M6 as liquidity. | Legal structure may be misrepresented as market demand, transferability or exit. | RAP-M6 means legally wrapped only. Liquidity, exit, custody, settlement and demand require separate evidence and disclosure. |
| Treating RAP-M7 as universal market-readiness. | Market-active status may be extended beyond the instrument, counterparty or jurisdiction that supports it. | RAP-M7 applies only within the relevant instrument, counterparty, jurisdiction, disclosure package, custody / settlement pathway and Claims Register boundary. |
| Using ecological performance value as base cash flow without contractual pathway. | Inflated cash-flow model; legal, underwriting and reputational risk. | Base-case cash flows only from documented productive or contractual sources. Layer 2 requires a contractual pathway. Layer 3 remains non-admissible for financial use unless separately admitted. |
| Assuming time-positive value without hazard model. | Regenerative improvement may not occur, may regress or may mature more slowly than projected. | Include hazard model, regression probability, sensitivity analysis, time horizon and confidence range. |
| Using H1-H5 claims without pilot evidence. | Hypothesis-level claims may be presented as demonstrated facts. | H1-H5 claims require pilot evidence, baseline, comparator, confidence range, reviewer trail, Claims Register update and governance review before PRU uplift, RAP formation or investor-facing use. |
| Skipping baseline, comparator or confidence range. | Improvement, superiority or institutional-reliance claims become non-admissible. | No baseline means no improvement claim. No comparator means no superiority claim. No confidence range means no institutional reliance. |
| Treating external literature as Prometheus performance data. | Scientific plausibility may be confused with site-specific validation. | External literature may inform design and protocols. It does not substitute for Prometheus pilot evidence. |
| Skipping legal wrapper before token, note, fund interest or revenue right. | Unenforceable claims; securities, consumer-protection, tax or investor-protection risk. | Any investor-facing token, note, fund interest, revenue right, land-related right, certificate or digital representation requires jurisdiction-specific legal review and rights mapping. |
| Treating PRU, RAP, TRBK, HoloFuel, dashboard status or governance participation as enforceable rights. | Conceptual layers may be misrepresented as legal entitlements. | Only valid legal instruments create enforceable rights. PRU is an analytical interface. RAP is a portfolio structure. TRBK, HoloFuel and dashboard status do not create investor rights by themselves. |
| Treating a data room as market validation. | Organized evidence may be misread as demand, liquidity, offtake or investability. | A data room supports diligence. It does not create a market. Market validation requires Anchor Demand File, ADE classification, counterparty qualification, legal review, disclosure and Claims Register status. |
| Treating a counterparty conversation as offtake. | Informal interest may be overstated as binding demand. | Classify counterparty evidence precisely: conversation, feedback, LOI, MoU, procurement pathway, insurer review, bank review, binding offtake or closed financing. |
| Treating price-discovery memo as market price. | Scenario value may be mistaken for executable price. | Separate ecological performance value, productive cash flow, credits, public incentives, contractual revenue, optional upside and scenario-only values. |
| Treating blockchain, Holochain-compatible records, registry entries or bridge messages as duplicate-proof. | Double-counting, double-claiming, double-representation or digital double-spending risk. | Require event identity tuple, evidence lineage, representation tuple where applicable, No-Double-Spending Threat Model, duplicate-claim detection, security review where applicable, quarantine pathway and Claims Register update. |
| Treating oracle input as ecological truth. | External signals may be used as unquestioned proof. | Oracles, sensors, APIs and registries are evidence inputs or transmission layers only. Ecological truth requires method, provenance, confidence, review and admissibility. |
| Treating custody, settlement or registry synchronization as automatically ready. | Transferability, settlement or custody language may exceed admitted infrastructure. | Custody, settlement and registry-sync claims require technical review, legal review where applicable, operational pathway, data-room record, governance decision and Claims Register support. |
| Treating dashboard output as claim authority. | Dashboard state may exceed evidence, legal, market or runtime status. | Dashboards may display only Claims Register-supported statuses. A dashboard is not a certification engine, legal opinion, market validation or PRU / RAP authority. |
| Treating API response as legal, market, evidentiary or financial authority. | API outputs may be mistaken for institutional admission. | API responses must remain bounded by Claims Register status, Runtime Release Decision Record, legal review and market gate where applicable. |
| Treating a source commit, successful build or local smoke test as production admission. | Local functionality may be misrepresented as operational resilience or institutional deployment. | Runtime admission requires Runtime Verification and Release Safety Package, tests, security review where applicable, rollback, incident response, monitoring, governance approval and Claims Register alignment. |
| Treating a release candidate as externally relied-upon. | Internal or conditional release state may be overstated. | Distinguish design specification, prototype, sandbox build, internal test build, release candidate, production-admitted release and externally relied-upon release. |
| Treating AI outputs as MRV admissibility. | Hallucinations, data poisoning, synthetic evidence or non-reviewable inference may contaminate evidence. | AI assists only. MRV validity follows method, evidence source, provenance, confidence model, reviewer trail and Claims Register status. |
| Treating AI, VPS, endpoint, runtime bridge, zome call or external assistant as governance authority. | Automated systems may be incorrectly allowed to approve value, rights, maturity or investor-facing claims. | No AI model, VPS service, API route, dashboard, runtime bridge, zome call or external assistant may approve PRU value, validate MRV evidence, create legal rights, issue tokens, approve RAP maturity or authorize investor-facing claims. |
| Ignoring offline evidence continuity. | Network loss may break evidence lineage and make data unverifiable. | Maintain local signed evidence bundles, offline queues, synchronization fallbacks, recovery procedures and conflict-resolution records. |
| Publishing investor-facing material with outdated gate status. | Materials may preserve older claims after the Claims Register, legal review, market gate or runtime status has changed. | Investor-facing material must cite current Claims Register status, current gate status, current data-room evidence and current legal / market / runtime boundaries. |
| Collapsing specification-closed with validated, legally admitted, market-active or production-admitted. | Architecture may be mistaken for empirical, legal, market or implementation completion. | Specification closure is not empirical validation. Empirical validation is not legal admission. Legal admission is not market validation. Market validation is not liquidity. Runtime execution is not production admission. |
| Ignoring the lowest unresolved gate. | A claim may be advanced beyond the weakest unresolved dependency. | The lowest unresolved evidence, valuation, integrity, legal, market, runtime, security, custody, settlement, operational or governance gate controls the permitted claim. |

The correct avoidance discipline is:

1.  identify the claim;

2.  identify the Claim ID;

3.  identify the evidence status;

4.  identify the PRU-R or RAP-M status where applicable;

5.  identify whether legal review is required;

6.  identify whether market validation is required;

7.  identify whether integrity / no-double-counting controls are required;

8.  identify whether runtime support is externally relied upon;

9.  identify the current Claims Register status;

10. use only the language permitted by the lowest unresolved gate.

The core rule is:

do not translate a weaker status into a stronger status.

Observation is not evidence.

Evidence is not admissible value.

Admissible value is not a right.

PRU maturity is not market readiness.

RAP portfolio-readiness is not liquidity.

Counterparty review is not binding demand.

Legal wrapper is not market demand.

Data room is not transaction readiness.

Blockchain record is not ecological uniqueness.

Bridge message is not admissibility.

Oracle input is not truth.

Dashboard state is not authority.

API response is not legal status.

Runtime execution is not production admission.

The Claims Register, data room, legal review, market validation, integrity review, runtime release record and governance decision must remain aligned before any capital-facing, investor-facing, insurance-facing, procurement-facing, token-facing, dashboard-facing, API-facing, registry-facing, custody-facing, settlement-facing or public claim is made.

## Z.6 One‑Page Executive Summary for Capital Allocators

Prometheus transforms verified ecological regeneration into a capital‑readable asset class.

**Key claims:**

- Regeneration is measurable only within defined evidence, method, provenance and reviewer boundaries.

- Evidence precedes value: no claim becomes financeable without MRV, provenance, admissibility and Claims Register status.

- PRU is an analytical interface, not a token, security, land right, cash-flow right or investor entitlement.

- RAP aggregates verified units into portfolio structures, but portfolio-readiness does not equal market-readiness.

- Capital readiness requires: evidence gate + valuation gate + integrity gate + legal gate + market gate.

- Runtime-supported claims require runtime verification and release safety where software outputs are externally relied upon.

- Legal wrapper and jurisdiction-specific review are required before any investor-facing instrument.

- Anchor demand, counterparty evidence and RAP-M status are required before market-facing RAP language.

- No bridge-facing, token-facing, registry-sync, custody, settlement or cross-system representation is externally admissible without no-double-spending controls and governance admission.

**Risk reminders:**

- Ecological, MRV, operational, governance, legal, token, liquidity, interoperability, runtime, custody, settlement and AI risks remain.

- Time-positive dynamics are conditional, not guaranteed.

- No guarantee of return, liquidity, certification, transferability, duplicate-proof status, market demand, legal admission or production readiness.

- Dashboard, API, runtime, token, bridge, registry or data-room status must never exceed Claims Register status.

**For detailed methodology, see:**

- MRV: Appendix G

- PRU value function: Appendix R

- Validation protocol: Appendix T

- Portfolio risk: Appendix V

- AI/offline architecture: Appendix W

- Legal survival: Appendix S

## Z.7 Final Navigation Note

This roadmap is a living orientation tool.

It does not override the source layer.

It does not override the Claims Register.

It does not override evidence disciplines.

It does not override the data room.

It does not override governance rules.

It does not override legal review.

It does not override market validation.

It does not override runtime release records.

It does not create investor rights, regulatory approval, market demand, liquidity, certification, production readiness or financial admissibility.

Its function is to help institutional readers navigate the Prometheus architecture without collapsing distinct stages of maturity.

The final navigation rule is:

read Prometheus through gates, not narratives.

A claim must be read through its evidence status.

An observation must be read through its provenance.

An indicator must be read through its confidence model.

A PRU must be read through its bounded methodology.

A RAP must be read through its market-readiness state.

A legal pathway must be read through its jurisdiction-specific legal opinion.

A market pathway must be read through its Anchor Demand File.

An integrity pathway must be read through its no-double-counting controls.

A runtime pathway must be read through its Runtime Release Decision Record.

A dashboard must be read through the Claims Register.

An API response must be read through the Claims Register.

A token-facing pathway must be read through legal, integrity, custody, settlement and Claims Register status.

No reader should infer that Prometheus is capital-ready merely because its architecture is coherent.

No reader should infer that Prometheus is investable merely because regeneration is measurable.

No reader should infer that Prometheus is market-active merely because a RAP can be modelled.

No reader should infer that Prometheus creates rights merely because PRU, RAP, TRBK, HoloFuel, dashboard status, access credentials or digital representations are described.

No reader should infer that Prometheus is production-ready merely because code, runtime components, repositories, dashboards, APIs, zomes, schemas, AI/offline modules or VPS deployments exist.

Capital allocators, family offices, procurement officers, insurance or reinsurance partners, legal counsel, technical reviewers and institutional counterparties must conduct their own due diligence, jurisdictional review, legal structuring, market validation, data-room review, runtime review and risk assessment.

Prometheus provides architecture, methodology, evidence discipline, governance logic and capital-readability infrastructure.

It does not replace regulated asset management.

It does not replace legal counsel.

It does not replace financial advice.

It does not replace securities review.

It does not replace tax advice.

It does not replace insurance underwriting.

It does not replace procurement qualification.

It does not replace technical security review.

It does not replace runtime release review.

It does not replace investor diligence.

The operative closure of Appendix Z is:

evidence before value;

integrity before representation;

legal review before rights;

anchor demand before market-facing language;

runtime verification before external software reliance;

governance before capital-facing use;

lowest unresolved gate before every claim.

Prometheus may become institutionally powerful only by remaining institutionally disciplined.

Its credibility depends on refusing premature translation.

No forced progression is permitted from architecture to evidence, from evidence to value, from value to rights, from rights to market, from market to liquidity, from software to authority, or from narrative to capital allocation.

The final reader instruction is therefore:

follow the gate;

read the claim boundary;

verify the evidence;

check the Claims Register;

inspect the data room;

require legal review;

require market validation;

require runtime release records where software is relied upon;

and never permit a stronger claim than the weakest unresolved gate allows.

**Appendix AA – Regulatory Alignment Matrix**

**AA.1 Purpose and Boundary**

This appendix provides a high-level regulatory alignment matrix for Prometheus concepts, pathways and representations.

It maps PRU, RAP, MRV, Claims Register status, no-double-counting controls, Anchor Demand File, legal wrapper, TRBK, HoloFuel, dashboard outputs, API outputs, AI/offline modules, runtime releases, token-facing pathways, custody pathways and settlement pathways against existing or emerging regulatory and compliance domains.

This appendix does not constitute legal advice. It does not constitute regulatory approval. It does not constitute compliance certification. It does not determine securities status, fund status, token status, tax treatment, accounting treatment, procurement eligibility, insurance eligibility, green-claim validity, AI compliance or data-protection compliance.

It is an orientation tool for legal counsel, compliance officers, institutional reviewers, capital allocators, public-sector counterparties, insurance or reinsurance partners and technical auditors.

Actual regulatory treatment depends on jurisdiction, instrument structure, counterparty type, investor type, offering method, transferability, rights created, marketing language, custody pathway, settlement pathway, data-processing context, AI use, ecological-claim scope and specific facts.

> **Regulatory boundary: architecture is not approval; evidence is not certification; PRU is not a legal right; RAP is not a fund by itself; dashboard status is not legal status; API output is not regulatory status; runtime execution is not production admission; regulatory alignment is not regulatory approval.**

No Prometheus pathway may be described as compliant, regulated, approved, exempt, taxonomy-aligned, SFDR-ready, MiCA-compliant, securities-compliant, procurement-eligible, insurance-recognised, AI-compliant, GDPR-compliant, custody-ready, settlement-ready, token-ready, market-ready or investor-ready unless jurisdiction-specific legal review, applicable regulatory analysis, data-room evidence, Claims Register status and governance approval support that classification.

Where regulatory uncertainty exists, the permitted language is: regulatory mapping pending; legal classification pending; jurisdiction-specific review required; instrument structure not yet admitted; token-facing pathway not yet admitted; custody / settlement pathway not yet admitted; investor-facing use not yet admitted.

**AA.2 Regulatory Framework Mapping**

| ***Regulatory / compliance domain*** | ***Relevant Prometheus concept*** | ***Alignment / interpretation*** | ***Required caution / controlling gate*** |
|----|----|----|----|
| *MiCA / crypto-assets regulation* | *Token-facing PRU, token-facing RAP, TRBK, future digital representations, custody or transfer pathways.* | *PRU is an analytical interface, not a crypto-asset by itself. A tokenised or transferable representation may require classification depending on rights, issuer, transferability, custody and use.* | *No token-facing claim without jurisdiction-specific legal review, token classification, disclosure, custody review, transfer restrictions where applicable and Claims Register status.* |
| *Securities law / investment-contract analysis* | *PRU-linked contracts, RAP structures, notes, fund interests, revenue rights, land-related rights, tokenised instruments or bundled structures.* | *Prometheus concepts remain analytical until a separate legal structure creates enforceable financial rights. Securities analysis may be required when profit expectation, pooling, transferability, managerial reliance or revenue exposure is introduced.* | *No investor-facing instrument, return language, yield language, ownership language, redemption language or transferable-rights language without legal opinion, rights mapping and disclosure package.* |
| *Fund regulation / AIFMD / MiFID-type analysis* | *RAP as SPV, fund, note programme, managed portfolio, pooled exposure or capital vehicle.* | *RAP is a portfolio-readiness structure by itself, not automatically a regulated fund. Regulation may apply when capital is pooled, managed, marketed or exposed through legal instruments.* | *RAP-M status does not determine fund status. Capital-facing RAP structures require legal review, investor eligibility rules, marketing restrictions and governance approval.* |
| *EU Taxonomy / sustainable activity classification* | *Ecological performance, verified regenerative activities, use of proceeds, eligible expenditure and environmental objectives.* | *MRV evidence and Claims Register status may support taxonomy analysis but do not guarantee taxonomy alignment.* | *Taxonomy-alignment language requires technical-screening analysis, DNSH review, minimum-safeguards review, evidence package and independent verification where required.* |
| *SFDR / sustainability-related disclosure* | *PRU methodology, PRU maturity, RAP reporting, MRV integrity, risk disclosures and sustainability characteristics.* | *PRU and RAP data may support sustainability disclosures, but SFDR classification depends on fund strategy, product design and legal issuer context.* | *No Article 8, Article 9, PAI, taxonomy-alignment or sustainability-objective claim from PRU maturity alone. Legal and disclosure review required.* |
| *CSRD / ESRS / corporate sustainability reporting* | *Site-level MRV, biodiversity, water, soil, climate-risk, governance and value-chain evidence.* | *Prometheus evidence may support reporting inputs if method, provenance, assurance scope and data lineage are documented.* | *Corporate reporting use requires assurance boundary, reporting-entity control analysis, materiality assessment and non-duplication controls.* |
| *Environmental claims / anti-greenwashing* | *Public regenerative claims, impact claims, biodiversity claims, soil claims, water claims, carbon claims and resilience claims.* | *Prometheus aligns with evidence-before-value discipline when claims remain evidence-bounded.* | *No public environmental claim without evidence scope, method, confidence range, reviewer trail, missing-data notes and Claims Register status.* |
| *Carbon / biodiversity / nature-credit frameworks* | *Regenerative performance, ecological improvement, impact result, credit-like representation or external registry entry.* | *Prometheus may generate evidence relevant to credit-like frameworks but does not create credits by itself.* | *No credit, offset, certificate, registry, biodiversity unit, carbon unit or transferable environmental asset claim without standard eligibility, legal review, registry review and no-double-counting controls.* |
| *No-double-counting / registry integrity* | *PRU evidence, RAP exposure, certificates, credits, tokens, registry entries, bridge messages, custody records and settlement records.* | *Prometheus treats double-counting, double-claiming, double-representation and digital double-spending as material integrity failures.* | *Requires event identity tuple, evidence lineage, representation tuple where applicable, No-Double-Spending Threat Model, duplicate-claim detection, quarantine process, adverse integrity record and Claims Register update.* |
| *Custody / settlement / transfer infrastructure* | *Token-facing pathways, custody records, settlement records, transfer-agent pathways, bridge connectors and registry synchronization.* | *Custody and settlement become relevant only when legal instruments or transferable digital representations are admitted.* | *No custody-ready, settlement-ready, transferable, externally redeemable or cross-system representation language without legal review, technical review, security review, operational pathway and Claims Register status.* |
